files.title: Account Separation Checklist
files.content:
# Account Separation Checklist

**Purpose:** Build a clean firewall between your W2 employer's infrastructure and The Nordic Nerd. Do this *before* the agency does any real work. Re-audit quarterly.

This is operational hygiene, not legal advice. Pair this with `ip-protection-notes.md` and a 30-minute consult with a FL startup attorney before launching paid services.

---

## 1. Identity & accounts

- [ ] **Email:** Confirm `adamseandeleon@gmail.com` is a *personal* Gmail you opened yourself, on a device you own, never reset by the employer's IT. If it was ever set up on a work-issued device, change the password from a personal device and remove any MDM/work profile from your phone.
- [ ] **Anthropic / Claude:** This Claude account is tied to your personal Gmail. Never sign in on an employer-managed device or browser profile. Verify under Account Settings that no work email is a secondary login.
- [ ] **GitHub:** You have a personal GitHub account distinct from any employer org membership. Confirm: (a) personal account uses personal Gmail, (b) you are *not* on a Copilot/Enterprise seat paid by the employer, (c) your `.gitconfig` on personal hardware uses the personal email.
- [ ] **Cloud storage:** Nordic Nerd never touches employer Google Drive, Dropbox, OneDrive, Notion workspaces, etc. Create a brand-new Google Workspace tenant on `thenordicnerd.com` for all agency files.
- [ ] **Password manager:** Use a separate vault (or at minimum a separate folder with no shared items) for Nordic Nerd credentials. 1Password lets you create a personal vault inside a personal account — never sync this to an employer-managed account.

## 2. Hardware

- [ ] **Never run Nordic Nerd work on an employer-issued laptop.** This is the single biggest IP-leakage risk. Even after-hours. Even "just one Stripe login."
- [ ] **Buy/dedicate one personal machine** for agency work. A used M-series Mac mini is fine.
- [ ] **Phone:** If your personal phone has a work profile or work MDM, do Nordic Nerd work in a separate browser (Safari personal vs. Chrome work), or better, use a different phone. iOS Focus + Personal/Work split helps but isn't airtight.
- [ ] **VPN:** When in doubt about networks, route Nordic Nerd traffic through a personal VPN (Mullvad, ProtonVPN) so it never touches an employer corporate network or DNS.

## 3. Time & calendars

- [ ] Nordic Nerd never appears on the W2 employer's calendar, Slack status, or activity logs.
- [ ] Discovery calls and client meetings live on a Cal.com account on `thenordicnerd.com`, never Google Calendar tied to a work account.
- [ ] Block your *personal* calendar for agency time so you don't double-book. Use plain names like "Personal — deep work" — there's nothing wrong with the agency existing, just don't broadcast it on shared work systems.
- [ ] Agency work happens outside W2 work hours. Document this if you ever need to defend it.

## 4. Money

- [ ] **LLC formation:** File a single-member LLC in Florida (`thenordicnerd.com` LLC or similar). ~$125 state fee. SunBiz.org. Do this before opening business banking.
- [ ] **EIN:** Free from IRS (irs.gov/EIN). Takes 10 minutes.
- [ ] **Business bank account:** Mercury, Relay, or your existing bank's business product. Personal credit card paying for agency tools = piercing the corporate veil; don't do it past month 1.
- [ ] **Stripe:** Connect to the LLC EIN, not your SSN. Stripe's TOS lets you operate as a sole prop initially, but switch to the LLC the moment it's filed.
- [ ] **Wave bookkeeping:** Categorize every agency expense from day one. Future you will thank present you.

## 5. Contracts you may already have

- [ ] **Re-read your employment agreement.** Look specifically for: (a) IP assignment scope — does it claim "anything you create during the term"? (b) Moonlighting clause — is outside work prohibited or requires disclosure? (c) Non-compete — what industries/geographies? (d) Non-solicit — applies to employer's customers/employees.
- [ ] **Employee handbook:** Check moonlighting policy. Some require written disclosure.
- [ ] **Disclose if required.** Lying by omission on a moonlighting policy is a fire-able offense that can taint your eventual transition story.
- [ ] **Don't recruit from your W2 employer.** Don't pitch their customers. Don't reuse their code, templates, or playbooks.

## 6. Communication discipline

- [ ] Never mention Nordic Nerd, side projects, or revenue numbers on employer Slack, email, calls, or recorded meetings.
- [ ] No agency commits from work laptops. No agency Slack on work browsers signed into work SSO.
- [ ] Social posts about the agency from personal accounts only, on personal devices, on personal networks.

## 7. AI tool boundary

- [ ] This Claude account, plus any Claude API keys, ChatGPT, Cursor, etc. for the agency, are billed to the LLC's card and used on the personal machine only.
- [ ] Never use the employer's Claude/ChatGPT/Copilot seat for Nordic Nerd work. The prompts and outputs may be logged in their workspace.
- [ ] If your employer uses a corporate AI policy, your agency operates fully outside it.

## 8. Public posture

- [ ] LinkedIn: list Nordic Nerd as a side project if you want to build in public. Otherwise leave it off until you're ready. Both are valid; just don't lie about it.
- [ ] YouTube series (per `05-Content/`): film from home, on your hardware, with your accounts. Never reference current employer by name or share details that could be considered confidential.

## 9. Re-audit cadence

- Quarterly: re-read this file, check anything that's drifted (especially #1, #4, #7).
- Whenever your W2 changes (promotion, new contract, new policy): re-read #5.
- Before your first paying client: book the attorney consult.

---

**Last reviewed:** [date]
**Next audit due:** [date + 90 days]

files.title: Bright-Line Rules
files.content:
# Bright-Line Rules

**One-line version:** Employer's tools do employer's work. My tools do Nordic Nerd. Nothing crosses the line.

These rules are non-negotiable. When you're tired or rushed, you'll be tempted to violate them. Don't. The cost of a single violation (legal exposure, IP claim, fired-for-cause) is orders of magnitude higher than the inconvenience of switching machines.

---

## What lives where

| Asset | Employer | Nordic Nerd |
|---|---|---|
| Laptop / desktop | Work-issued | Personal Mac (separate machine) |
| Phone | Work profile (if any) | Personal device or personal profile |
| Email | `you@employer.com` | `adam@thenordicnerd.com` + cold inboxes |
| Calendar | Employer Google Workspace | Nordic Nerd Google Workspace + Cal.com |
| Slack / chat | Employer workspace only | Agency Slack / Discord (if any) |
| GitHub | Employer org seat | Personal account, personal repos |
| Claude / ChatGPT / Copilot | Employer seat for employer work | Personal API keys + personal subscriptions |
| Cloud storage | Employer Drive | Nordic Nerd Drive on `thenordicnerd.com` |
| Password vault | Employer 1Password / Bitwarden | Personal vault, separate folder |
| VPN | Employer VPN for employer apps | Personal VPN (Mullvad/Proton) for agency |
| Domain registrar | n/a | Personal account (Cloudflare/Namecheap) on personal email |
| Stripe / banking | n/a | LLC-owned, business card |
| Notion / Linear / Asana | Employer workspace | Separate Nordic Nerd workspace |

## What never happens

1. **No agency work on the work laptop.** Not "just to check something." Not "I'm on PTO." Not at all.
2. **No work code, docs, templates, customer data, or playbooks repurposed for Nordic Nerd.** Even patterns you remember are gray-zone if your employment agreement is broad.
3. **No agency communication on work systems.** No agency email forwarded to work email. No client texts on a work-managed phone. No agency Slack on a work browser profile.
4. **No employer's customers, leads, or contacts in any Nordic Nerd CRM.** Even if you "would have met them anyway."
5. **No recruiting employer's employees**, even casually ("we should work together someday"). Wait until you're fully out + past any non-solicit window.
6. **No mention of employer's name or projects in any Nordic Nerd marketing.** Generic phrasing ("I'm a Staff Frontend Engineer at a public company") is fine. Naming the employer or describing their internal systems is not.
7. **No agency calls during W2 work hours.** Lunch breaks are gray. Pre-7am and post-5pm + weekends are safe.
8. **No invoicing as an individual.** All money flows through the LLC after it's filed.

## What's always fine

- Building a side business on your own time, hardware, and accounts — most W2 agreements explicitly allow this unless it directly competes.
- Owning your evenings and weekends.
- Pitching contractors in Polk County who have nothing to do with your employer's industry.
- Using your own technical skills (Vue, Nuxt, TypeScript) — skills are yours, not the employer's IP.
- Publishing publicly about building an agency (within the rules above), provided you don't disclose employer-confidential information.

## The "would I be comfortable if this got subpoenaed" test

Before any borderline action, ask: *would this email/commit/message hold up if my employer's lawyers reviewed it?* If you have to think about it for more than three seconds, don't do it.

## When in doubt

Default to the more conservative option. Switch laptops. Switch accounts. Ask the attorney. The friction is the point — it's what keeps the firewall intact.

---

**Posted at desk?** Print this. Stick it next to your monitor for the first 90 days.

files.title: IP Protection Notes
files.content:
# IP Protection Notes

**Disclaimer:** I'm not your lawyer. This is a lay summary of the questions to walk into an attorney consult with. Florida has its own quirks (notably narrower non-compete enforceability after July 2025 federal rule shifts, but state law still controls many disputes). Do not rely on this in lieu of a $300–$500 attorney session before launching paid services.

---

## Why this matters at all

You are a Staff Frontend Engineer (W2) building a frontend-adjacent services agency on the side. The boundaries between "skills I have" (yours), "general industry knowledge" (yours), "things I learned at my employer" (often theirs), and "things I built at my employer" (almost certainly theirs) are not bright lines — they're gradients. An attorney consult clarifies which gradients matter in your specific situation.

The highest-risk failure mode is **producing a SaaS product or reusable codebase** as part of the agency. Services (writing code for a client, who owns it on delivery) is the lowest-risk model. Anything you intend to license, productize, or sell to multiple customers needs explicit cleanroom thinking.

## Documents to bring to the attorney

1. Signed employment agreement (the actual PDF, not your memory of it)
2. Any addenda, IP assignment forms, or restrictive covenants signed during employment
3. Employee handbook (specifically: moonlighting, IP, non-compete, non-solicit sections)
4. A one-paragraph description of Nordic Nerd's offer (the retainer)
5. A list of every customer/lead you have or plan to pursue (just to confirm zero overlap)
6. A list of every open-source / personal project you maintain (so we can flag any pre-existing inventions for a Schedule A)

## Questions to ask the attorney

### Scope of employer's IP claim
1. Does my IP assignment cover only inventions made *using employer resources*, or anything I create *during the term of employment* regardless of resources used?
2. Is there a "developed outside scope of employment, on personal time and equipment, unrelated to employer's business" carve-out? If yes, does Nordic Nerd qualify? (FL Stat. 688.002 and similar may help; varies by employer.)
3. Can I file a *Schedule A / Prior Inventions* disclosure listing existing personal projects and the agency itself, to protect them explicitly?

### Moonlighting
4. Does my agreement require disclosure of outside work? If yes, what's the formal process and what level of detail do I owe?
5. Is "providing services as a contractor to non-competing third parties" specifically prohibited or specifically permitted?
6. If the agreement is silent, what's the FL default?

### Non-compete & non-solicit
7. Post-departure, what's my non-compete radius, duration, and definition of "competitive"?
8. Are contractors in Polk County (Lakeland, Bartow, Winter Haven) within any geographic non-compete? (Almost certainly no, but ask.)
9. What's the non-solicit window for employees and customers, and does Nordic Nerd's contractor ICP have any overlap with employer's customer base?

### Trade secrets
10. What kinds of information from my W2 role are presumed confidential even if not stamped "Confidential"?
11. If I learn a generally-applicable engineering technique at work, is that technique mine to use elsewhere, or is its application restricted?
12. How should I handle the question "How did you learn to do X?" if a client asks?

### Entity structure
13. Should Nordic Nerd be an LLC, S-corp election later, or stay sole proprietorship for year 1? FL tax implications.
14. Should the LLC own the domains and IP, or should I as an individual? (Almost always: LLC.)
15. Operating agreement — does a single-member LLC in FL need one? (Yes, even if not legally required.)

### Client contracts
16. Review the `retainer-agreement-v1.md` draft. Flag anything FL-specific. Add or revise: (a) limitation of liability, (b) indemnification, (c) IP ownership on delivery, (d) confidentiality reciprocity, (e) termination + transition obligations, (f) auto-renewal language compliant with FL Stat. 501.165 (auto-renewal disclosures), (g) governing law and venue.
17. Do I need a separate MSA + SOW model, or is a single retainer agreement sufficient at this scale?
18. What language do I need around AI-generated work product and the client's right to claim authorship?

### Tax & insurance
19. Quarterly estimated tax cadence and FL-specific filings.
20. Do I need general liability + professional liability (E&O) before signing my first client? At what coverage limit?
21. Cyber liability — needed if handling client customer data via GBP, Stripe, etc.?

### Insurance providers worth pricing
- Hiscox (small business E&O, ~$30–60/mo at agency scale)
- Next Insurance
- The Hartford
- A local FL independent agent who can bundle

## Risk levels by activity

**Lowest risk** — Doing this is fine, document for your records:
- Writing code for client websites that the client owns on delivery
- Local SEO and content writing services
- GBP setup and optimization
- Monthly reporting and consulting calls

**Medium risk** — Worth attorney clarification before scaling:
- Building reusable Nuxt templates that you reuse across clients (verify they don't reflect employer IP)
- Self-hosting n8n workflows you'll license or productize
- Any "AI agent" product that approaches SaaS

**Higher risk** — Do not do without explicit legal clearance:
- Productizing the entire agency stack as a SaaS
- Selling tooling to companies that overlap with your employer's customer base
- Hiring sub-contractors who are also W2 at your employer
- Any "white-label" arrangement that obscures who's doing the work

## Defensive logging

Maintain a `personal-projects-log.md` (not in this folder — keep it in personal cloud or your own laptop) that timestamps:
- Date you started Nordic Nerd planning
- Date of LLC formation
- Date of first client outreach
- Major technical decisions (e.g., "selected Nuxt 3 based on personal preference and public skill, independent of any work project")

This log helps establish provenance if there's ever a dispute about when/how the agency or its IP was created.

## When to escalate

Re-engage the attorney (at minimum a 30-min check-in) when:
- You hire a sub-contractor or part-timer
- You build anything reusable across clients you'd consider productizing
- You leave your W2 (review non-compete + non-solicit before announcing anything)
- A client asks for terms outside your standard retainer agreement
- You take on a client in a regulated industry (healthcare, finance, legal)

---

**Booked attorney consult:** [date]
**Attorney name + contact:** [fill in]
**Next review:** [date]

files.title: Apify Scrape Configuration
files.content:
# Apify Scrape Configuration

**Goal:** Pull 100 high-quality contractor leads per week from Google Maps across Polk County and surrounding cities. Output is a CSV/JSON that feeds Hunter enrichment → MillionVerifier → Smartlead.

**Actor used:** `compass/google-maps-scraper` (most cost-effective, returns business name, address, phone, website, category, rating, reviews count, and GBP URL). Apify's "official" Google Maps actor is also fine but ~2x cost.

---

## Geography (in priority order)

| City | Polk County? | Why included |
|---|---|---|
| Lakeland | Yes | Largest market in Polk, anchor city |
| Winter Haven | Yes | Your home base, proximity advantage |
| Bartow | Yes | County seat, contractor-dense |
| Auburndale | Yes | Adjacent to Winter Haven |
| Haines City | Yes | Growing market, less competition |
| Davenport | Yes (split) | Boomtown adjacent to Disney corridor |
| Lake Wales | Yes | Smaller but underserved |
| Plant City | No (Hillsborough) | Polk-adjacent, contractors often serve both |
| Mulberry | Yes | Smaller, infrequent scrapes |
| Frostproof | Yes | Smaller, infrequent scrapes |

Run weekly scrapes on the top 7. Rotate Plant City, Mulberry, Frostproof into the mix once a month.

## Niches (in priority order)

| Niche | Search keyword(s) | Notes |
|---|---|---|
| General contractor | `general contractor`, `home builder`, `custom homes` | Highest retainer fit; longest sales cycle |
| Kitchen & bath remodel | `kitchen remodeler`, `bath remodel`, `kitchen and bath` | Strong margins, photo-heavy work = good content |
| Roofing | `roofer`, `roofing contractor` | High volume in FL post-storm; competitive ad space |
| HVAC | `hvac`, `air conditioning repair`, `hvac contractor` | Service-call heavy; GBP-driven |
| Electrician | `electrician`, `electrical contractor` | Less digital sophistication, opportunity |
| Plumber | `plumber`, `plumbing contractor` | Similar to electricians |
| Landscaping | `landscaper`, `lawn care`, `landscape design` | Lower retainer fit (more $1.5k tier) |
| Pool builders | `pool builder`, `pool contractor`, `pool installation` | High-ticket; FL-specific demand |

## Search query matrix (7 cities × 8 niches = 56 queries/week)

Apify input format for the `compass/google-maps-scraper` actor:

```json
{
  "searchStringsArray": [
    "general contractor Lakeland FL",
    "general contractor Winter Haven FL",
    "general contractor Bartow FL",
    "general contractor Auburndale FL",
    "general contractor Haines City FL",
    "general contractor Davenport FL",
    "general contractor Lake Wales FL",
    "kitchen remodeler Lakeland FL",
    "...etc."
  ],
  "maxCrawledPlacesPerSearch": 30,
  "language": "en",
  "countryCode": "us",
  "scrapeContacts": true,
  "scrapeReviews": false,
  "scrapeReviewsCount": 0,
  "scrapeDirectories": false,
  "exportPlaceUrls": true,
  "additionalInfo": true
}
```

The full 56-query array is generated dynamically by the n8n workflow (`01-weekly-scrape.json`) so you don't maintain a hardcoded list. The workflow reads cities and niches from a JSON config, builds the cartesian product, and submits.

## Cost estimate

- `compass/google-maps-scraper` is ~$3.50 per 1,000 results.
- 56 queries × 30 max results = 1,680 results/week, but with dedup ~1,000 unique.
- ~$3.50/week × 4 = **~$14/mo** in scrape costs.
- Add ~$15/mo budget buffer for retries and one-off campaigns = $30/mo Apify ceiling.

## Output fields used downstream

After dedup, the n8n workflow keeps these fields per business:

```
business_name, address, city, state, zip,
phone, website, category, rating, reviews_count,
gbp_url, place_id, latitude, longitude
```

Then enrichment adds:

```
domain (parsed from website),
contact_email (Hunter),
contact_first_name, contact_last_name, contact_title (Hunter),
email_verification_status (MillionVerifier),
gbp_optimization_score (custom — see below),
first_line (Claude API)
```

## GBP optimization score

A custom 0–10 score the agent calculates from scraped data, used as the personalization hook. Higher score = less reason for you to reach out. Target: send to businesses scoring 3–7 (room to improve, but not so bad they need someone bigger than you).

Score components (each 0–2 points):
1. Photo count >= 25 (2 pts)
2. Reviews count >= 50 (2 pts)
3. Average rating >= 4.5 (2 pts)
4. Has website with HTTPS + mobile-responsive (2 pts) — checked separately by a quick HEAD request
5. Categories field populated with 3+ categories (2 pts)

A 4/10 contractor with 30 reviews but a Wix site from 2019 is your ideal prospect.

## Filters before sending

Reject any record that fails:

- No website
- Website is Facebook/Instagram only (we serve people who already understand they need a real site)
- Reviews count < 10 (too new, not yet a real business)
- Rating < 3.8 (likely problematic business; focus on solid operators)
- Category outside the 8 niches above
- Email not deliverable per MillionVerifier (status != "ok")
- Already in HubSpot (any contact with this domain or this phone)

## Output handoff

Cleaned CSV lands at `/n8n/data/leads/YYYY-MM-DD-polk-contractors-clean.csv` and gets uploaded to Smartlead via the API in workflow `02-push-to-sequencer.json`.

## Weekly cadence

| Day | Action |
|---|---|
| Sunday 11pm ET | n8n workflow `01-weekly-scrape` fires automatically |
| Monday morning | Review the clean CSV — eyeball 10 random rows for quality |
| Monday afternoon | Approve push to Smartlead — `02-push-to-sequencer` fires |
| Tue–Fri | Smartlead sends sequence; you handle replies via reply-handling workflow |

Manual override: any time you want to run a one-off scrape (e.g., new city, new niche), run the n8n workflow with overridden inputs from its manual trigger.

## Quality discipline

Re-eyeball 10 random rows every week. If quality is dropping (wrong industry, generic emails, bad first-lines), pause the campaign and fix upstream before scaling. List quality compounds; bad lists are the #1 cause of low reply rates.

files.title: DNS & Domain Plan
files.content:
# DNS & Domain Plan

**Three domains, three jobs.** This document is the source of truth for what each domain does and how its DNS is configured.

| Domain | Role | What it sends/receives |
|---|---|---|
| `thenordicnerd.com` | Primary brand | Website, primary inbox `adam@`, all client comms, all warm contacts |
| `nordicnerdhq.com` | Cold outbound #1 | Cold campaigns only (Inbox A, Inbox B) |
| `getnordicnerd.com` | Cold outbound #2 | Cold campaigns only (Inbox C) |

**Why two cold domains instead of one with three inboxes?** Spreading volume across multiple domains protects you if any single domain gets reputation damage from a misfired campaign. Also lets you A/B test sender domains.

**Why not subdomains (e.g., `send.thenordicnerd.com`)?** A subdomain's reputation bleeds into the root domain. If your cold campaign tanks `send.`, it can drag down `thenordicnerd.com`'s ability to deliver client/proposal emails. Separate registered domains are the cleanest firewall.

---

## Registrar setup (Cloudflare)

Use Cloudflare for all three. Same account, same login, $9–11/year per `.com`.

1. Register `thenordicnerd.com`, `nordicnerdhq.com`, `getnordicnerd.com` in Cloudflare.
2. Enable WHOIS privacy (free, on by default for `.com`).
3. Enable auto-renew. Set a calendar reminder for the date 60 days before expiration in case the card on file fails.
4. Enable 2FA on the Cloudflare account itself. Use a TOTP app, not SMS.

---

## DNS records — `thenordicnerd.com` (PRIMARY)

This is the brand. Treat reputation here as precious.

**A / AAAA / CNAME** — point web traffic at the Nuxt deployment (Vercel/Netlify/Cloudflare Pages — see `n8n-deployment.md` for why the site does NOT live on your n8n VPS).

```
Type   Name    Value                                      Proxy   TTL
A      @       <hosting IP>  OR  CNAME to host           Proxied  Auto
CNAME  www     thenordicnerd.com                          Proxied  Auto
```

**MX** — Google Workspace.

```
Type   Name    Value                                      Priority  TTL
MX     @       SMTP.GOOGLE.COM                            1         Auto
```

(Google Workspace switched to a single-MX setup in 2023. If admin console gives you the legacy 5-record set, those work too. New tenants get the single record.)

**SPF** — authorize Google to send on your behalf. Only ONE TXT record at root prefixed `v=spf1`.

```
Type   Name    Value
TXT    @       v=spf1 include:_spf.google.com ~all
```

**DKIM** — generated by Google Workspace Admin → Apps → Google Workspace → Gmail → Authenticate email. They give you a TXT record like:

```
Type   Name                              Value
TXT    google._domainkey                 v=DKIM1; k=rsa; p=<long-key>
```

Add it, wait 24h, then click "Start authentication" in the Google admin console. Re-check in a week.

**DMARC** — start in monitoring mode (`p=none`), graduate to `p=quarantine` after 30 days of clean reports, then `p=reject` once you're confident.

```
Type   Name        Value
TXT    _dmarc      v=DMARC1; p=none; rua=mailto:dmarc-reports@thenordicnerd.com; pct=100; adkim=s; aspf=s
```

Set up a `dmarc-reports@` alias in Workspace and forward to your real inbox (or use a free reporting tool like Postmark DMARC).

**BIMI** (optional, later, after `p=quarantine` or stricter) — adds your logo next to Gmail emails. Requires a Verified Mark Certificate (~$1500/yr). Skip until revenue justifies it.

---

## DNS records — `nordicnerdhq.com` (COLD #1)

This domain exists to send cold email. Nothing else.

**MX** — Google Workspace tenant #2 (or just use it as a send-only domain via Smartlead's SMTP relay if you want to save the Workspace cost; but per spec we're using Workspace seats so set MX properly):

```
Type   Name    Value                Priority
MX     @       SMTP.GOOGLE.COM      1
```

**SPF** — same pattern:

```
TXT    @       v=spf1 include:_spf.google.com ~all
```

**DKIM** — generate in the second Workspace tenant's admin console, add the TXT record exactly as instructed.

**DMARC** — start at `p=none` here too. **Important:** keep DMARC monitoring on the cold domain even after going `p=reject` on primary; you want to know if anyone's spoofing your cold domain.

```
TXT    _dmarc   v=DMARC1; p=none; rua=mailto:dmarc-reports@thenordicnerd.com; pct=100
```

(Reports route back to your primary domain inbox — keeps reporting consolidated.)

**Redirect record** — `nordicnerdhq.com` and `www.nordicnerdhq.com` should redirect to `thenordicnerd.com` so prospects who type the cold domain into a browser land somewhere real.

```
Use Cloudflare Page Rules or Bulk Redirects:
  nordicnerdhq.com/*  →  301 → https://thenordicnerd.com/$1
```

---

## DNS records — `getnordicnerd.com` (COLD #2)

Identical pattern to `nordicnerdhq.com`. Different Workspace seat. Different inboxes. Different Smartlead "domain" entry.

Repeat all six record types: A/CNAME (for redirect), MX, SPF, DKIM, DMARC, plus the 301 redirect.

---

## Warmup sequence

Once DNS propagates (allow 24–48 hours), do NOT immediately start sending cold campaigns at volume. Smartlead's warmup feature handles this — see `smartlead-setup.md` for the 14–21 day ramp.

Day 1 of warmup = the day all 6 records are verified in Smartlead and Google Postmaster Tools shows the domain is recognized.

## Verification

Use these to confirm DNS is correct before going live:

- **MXToolbox** (https://mxtoolbox.com) — check MX, SPF, DKIM, DMARC for each domain.
- **Google Postmaster Tools** (https://postmaster.google.com) — register each domain to monitor reputation. Cold domains will show low data until volume picks up; this is normal.
- **mail-tester.com** — send a test email from each inbox to its address, expect 10/10. If less than 9/10, fix before going live.
- **Smartlead's connection check** — runs DKIM/SPF/DMARC tests on each connected inbox; will refuse to start warmup if records are wrong.

## DNS change log

Maintain a simple log here as you make changes. Include date, domain, record changed, why.

| Date | Domain | Change | Why |
|---|---|---|---|
| | | | |

files.title: Google Workspace Setup
files.content:
# Google Workspace Setup

**Two tenants, four mailboxes total.**

| Tenant | Domain | Seats | Purpose |
|---|---|---|---|
| Primary | `thenordicnerd.com` | 1 seat: `adam@thenordicnerd.com` | All warm comms, client work, proposals, billing |
| Secondary | `nordicnerdhq.com` (with `getnordicnerd.com` as a domain alias inside the same tenant) | 3 seats | Cold outreach only |

**Why two tenants?** Cold-email volume on `nordicnerdhq.com` could in theory hurt the reputation of your primary tenant if they shared one. They don't share a tenant when they're separate Workspace billing accounts — keep them billed separately, full firewall.

**Why one tenant for both cold domains?** Workspace lets you add up to 600 domain aliases to a single tenant. `getnordicnerd.com` can ride alongside `nordicnerdhq.com` as a domain alias, which means one Workspace bill covers all 3 cold inboxes regardless of which domain they're on. (If you'd rather have full separation, spin up a third tenant — costs an extra $7/seat/mo.)

---

## Tenant #1: Primary

### Sign up
1. Go to https://workspace.google.com → Business Starter.
2. Use `adam@thenordicnerd.com` as the primary admin.
3. Workspace will guide you through TXT verification of the domain — copy/paste the verification TXT to Cloudflare DNS.
4. Choose Business Starter ($7/seat/mo). Don't add seats yet.

### Configure
- **MX records**: Workspace prompts you. They're already in `dns-and-domain-plan.md`.
- **DKIM**: Admin → Apps → Google Workspace → Gmail → Authenticate email → Generate new record. Copy the TXT to Cloudflare. Click "Start authentication" 24h later.
- **Recovery**: set a recovery phone + recovery email (use a personal Gmail that's NOT linked to your W2).
- **2FA**: enforce for the admin account. Use a hardware key (YubiKey) if you can; TOTP minimum.

### Aliases on primary
Create as Gmail aliases (free, instant; not separate inboxes — they route to `adam@`):
- `hello@thenordicnerd.com` — generic inbox / contact form
- `billing@thenordicnerd.com` — Stripe receipts, vendor invoices
- `support@thenordicnerd.com` — client support address
- `dmarc-reports@thenordicnerd.com` — DMARC aggregate reports
- `noreply@thenordicnerd.com` — onboarding automation senders

Manage in Gmail Settings → Accounts → Send mail as.

### Signature template
```
Adam | The Nordic Nerd
Done-For-You Local Presence for FL Contractors
adam@thenordicnerd.com  |  thenordicnerd.com
Schedule a call: calendar.app.google/JheMSKoDMrWkiixy6
```

No images in the signature for now (heavy signatures hurt deliverability on long threads). Add a logo only after 6 months of clean sends.

---

## Tenant #2: Cold

### Sign up
1. Open a new browser profile (or use a different browser entirely) to keep the second Workspace tenant cleanly separated from the first.
2. Workspace signup → Business Starter, primary domain `nordicnerdhq.com`.
3. Use `adam@nordicnerdhq.com` as the admin user.
4. Verify domain via DNS TXT (Cloudflare).

### Add second domain as alias
1. Admin → Domains → Add a domain → `getnordicnerd.com`.
2. Choose "Secondary domain" (not domain alias). This lets users have addresses on either domain.
3. Verify via TXT.

### Buy seats and create inboxes
Add 3 seats total. Create:

| Email | Display Name | Why |
|---|---|---|
| `adam@nordicnerdhq.com` | Adam — The Nordic Nerd | Primary cold sender, personalized first-line campaigns |
| `hello@nordicnerdhq.com` | Adam — The Nordic Nerd | Backup inbox, A/B sender testing |
| `adam@getnordicnerd.com` | Adam — The Nordic Nerd | Third inbox, domain spread |

**Why same display name across all three?** Recipients are humans, not domains. If they reply to one and you follow up from another, the consistent name helps continuity. The domain difference is for deliverability mechanics, not branding.

### DKIM (cold tenant)
Generate DKIM TWICE — once per domain (`nordicnerdhq.com` and `getnordicnerd.com`). The admin console lets you pick the domain in the dropdown. Add both TXT records to Cloudflare.

### Forward to a unified inbox
You don't want to check 3 separate Gmails every day. Forward all 3 cold inboxes to `adam@thenordicnerd.com`:

In each cold inbox: Settings → Forwarding and POP/IMAP → Add forwarding address → enter `adam@thenordicnerd.com`. Confirm via email. Set to forward a copy and keep the original.

Then in your primary inbox, use a filter:
```
From: -@thenordicnerd.com  AND  To: (adam@nordicnerdhq.com OR hello@nordicnerdhq.com OR adam@getnordicnerd.com)
→ Apply label "Cold Replies" → Skip Inbox
```

Smartlead handles the actual reply-routing into its UI, so this is just a safety net.

### Signatures on cold inboxes
Keep cold signatures SHORTER than primary. Less visual weight = better deliverability.

```
— Adam
The Nordic Nerd | thenordicnerd.com
{{PHYSICAL_ADDRESS}}
Reply STOP to opt out.
```

The `{{PHYSICAL_ADDRESS}}` is CAN-SPAM requirement (see `03-Outbound-System/compliance/can-spam-checklist.md`). The opt-out language is also legally required.

---

## Initial inbox warmup (before Smartlead)

For the first 5 days after creating each Workspace mailbox, do "human warmup" — log in, send/receive a few real emails per day:

- Send yourself test messages
- Subscribe to 3–5 newsletters from each cold inbox (Stratechery, The Hustle, anything)
- Reply to one of those newsletters with a real comment
- Send 2–3 emails per day from each cold inbox to other addresses you own
- Mark anything that hits spam as "Not Spam"

This builds a small "human" signal before the bot warmup begins. Day 6 onward, hand off to Smartlead.

---

## Account recovery

Document these in 1Password under each tenant:
- Admin email + password
- Recovery phone
- Recovery email (a personal Gmail NOT used for anything else)
- 2FA backup codes (download and store securely)
- Org master key (Workspace's emergency unlock; some plans require this)

Losing access to the admin account of your cold tenant during an active campaign would be a small disaster. Treat the recovery setup seriously.

---

## Monthly check
- All 4 inboxes log in successfully
- All DKIM records pass MXToolbox
- Google Postmaster Tools shows no domain reputation degradation
- Storage usage under 70% of plan limit (Workspace Business Starter has 30GB per seat)

files.title: HubSpot CRM Setup (Free Tier)
files.content:
# HubSpot CRM Setup (Free Tier)

**Why HubSpot Free:** Free forever for 2 users, includes deal pipeline, contact properties, basic automation, native Gmail integration, and a clean API for n8n. The "Free Marketing Hub" piece you can ignore.

**What it does in this stack:** Holds every prospect from outbound + every conversation + every deal. Smartlead is the sender; HubSpot is the memory.

---

## Account creation

1. https://app.hubspot.com/signup-hubspot/crm
2. Use `adam@thenordicnerd.com`.
3. Skip the "connect your email" prompt for the cold inboxes — those stay in Smartlead. DO connect `adam@thenordicnerd.com` so warm replies, calls, and proposals log to the contact record.
4. Set timezone to America/New_York.
5. Enable 2FA.

## Branding

- Upload logo (low-res placeholder fine until you have a real one).
- Set company name: "The Nordic Nerd, LLC" (or current legal form).
- Set address: `{{PHYSICAL_ADDRESS}}` — used in HubSpot's CAN-SPAM footers for any non-Smartlead emails.

## Deal pipeline

Replace the default sales pipeline with this 6-stage retainer funnel:

| Stage | Probability | Auto-actions / criteria |
|---|---|---|
| 1. New Lead | 5% | Created when contact added from cold scrape (n8n) |
| 2. Replied | 15% | Moved by reply-triage n8n when reply classified positive/question |
| 3. Call Booked | 35% | Moved by Cal.com webhook (n8n) on booking |
| 4. Call Held | 50% | Moved manually after the discovery call |
| 5. Proposal Sent | 70% | Moved by Documenso webhook when proposal sent |
| 6. Won / Lost | 100% / 0% | Won = Documenso signed + Stripe subscription created |

**Why this structure:** Probabilities calibrate against realistic agency benchmarks for cold-to-retainer: ~5% replies, ~30% of replies book, ~70% of held calls get a proposal, ~30–40% of proposals close.

## Required deal properties (custom)

Add these in Settings → Properties → Deal:

| Property | Type | Notes |
|---|---|---|
| `tier_proposed` | Dropdown (Starter / Growth / Authority) | Set during/after discovery call |
| `mrr_value` | Number | Monthly recurring revenue if won ($1500/$2000/$2500) |
| `contract_months` | Number | Defaults to 6 |
| `niche` | Dropdown (matches scraper niches) | Auto-populated from contact |
| `city` | Dropdown (matches scraper cities) | Auto-populated from contact |
| `gbp_optimization_score` | Number (0–10) | From scrape; informs talking points |
| `first_line_used` | Text | First-line copy that earned the reply, for retro |
| `subject_line_used` | Text | Subject that earned the open |
| `discovery_call_url` | URL | Cal.com booking link |
| `proposal_doc_url` | URL | Documenso doc link |
| `stripe_customer_id` | Text | Filled by Stripe webhook |
| `loss_reason` | Dropdown | Price / Timing / Not a fit / Going with competitor / Ghosted |

## Required contact properties (custom)

| Property | Type | Notes |
|---|---|---|
| `cold_inbox_origin` | Text | Which inbox sent (for diagnostics) |
| `sequence_version` | Text | "v1", "v2", etc. — for A/B retros |
| `smartlead_lead_id` | Text | For backfill / debugging |
| `unsubscribed_at` | Datetime | Set by reply-triage on opt-out |

## Lifecycle stages

Use HubSpot's defaults but only these three meaningfully:

- `Lead` — from cold scrape, no reply yet
- `Sales Qualified Lead` — replied, in active conversation
- `Customer` — signed retainer + first Stripe charge succeeded

## Automation (Free tier supports simple workflows)

HubSpot Free has limited workflow tooling; do most automation in n8n. Inside HubSpot, set up:

1. **Lead rotation:** All deals stay with you (single owner) until you hire.
2. **Notification:** Email + in-app notification when a deal moves to "Call Booked" or "Proposal Sent".
3. **Task on stale deal:** If a deal sits in "Call Held" >7 days with no proposal, auto-create a task on it ("Send proposal or move to lost").
4. **Task on lost deal:** Auto-create a 90-day-out task "Re-engage" on every Lost deal that wasn't lost to "Not a fit".

## n8n integration

n8n's HubSpot node uses a Private App access token (not OAuth, simpler for self-hosted automation).

1. HubSpot → Settings → Integrations → Private Apps → Create.
2. Name: "n8n Nordic Ops".
3. Scopes needed:
   - `crm.objects.contacts.read` + `.write`
   - `crm.objects.deals.read` + `.write`
   - `crm.objects.companies.read` + `.write`
   - `crm.schemas.contacts.read`
   - `crm.schemas.deals.read`
4. Copy token, store in 1Password + as n8n credential `HubSpot Private App`.

## Naming + tagging discipline

- Contacts: HubSpot auto-creates one per unique email. Never delete; archive.
- Companies: created automatically by HubSpot's "domain-based company association" — make sure that setting is on. One company per contractor business.
- Deals: name format `<Company Name> — Retainer V1` so they're skimmable in the pipeline view.

## Reporting

Build these 4 reports in HubSpot dashboards (Free tier allows 3 custom dashboards):

1. **Pipeline by stage** — count of deals at each stage, last 30 days created.
2. **Reply → Call conversion** — % of "Replied" stage that reaches "Call Booked".
3. **Call → Proposal conversion** — % of "Call Held" that reaches "Proposal Sent".
4. **Proposal → Won conversion** — % of "Proposal Sent" reaching "Won".

These map to the funnel math in `06-Daily-Ops/weekly-metrics-template.md`.

## Migration / upgrade triggers

Stay on Free until any of:
- Need workflows more complex than n8n can run on the deal side (rare)
- Need >2 user seats
- Need email sequencing or marketing emails (Mailerlite handles this for now)
- Need predictive lead scoring or pipeline forecasting (post-product-market-fit)

At that point Starter ($20/mo/seat) or Sales Hub Pro ($90/mo/seat). Probably not before month 6.

files.title: n8n + Documenso Self-Hosted Deployment
files.content:
# n8n + Documenso Self-Hosted Deployment

**One $5/mo VPS runs both.** n8n handles automation, Documenso handles e-sign. They sit behind Caddy (reverse proxy with automatic HTTPS), share a small Postgres, and back up to S3 nightly.

**Why self-host n8n instead of n8n.cloud?** Cost ($5/mo vs $20+) and full control over data (especially Smartlead webhook payloads with prospect emails). Workflow JSON imports identically either way — you can migrate later if you outgrow it.

**Why same box for Documenso?** Both are Node/Postgres apps with modest resource needs. CX22 (2 vCPU / 4GB RAM) is comfortable for both at agency scale. Split them if you start signing 50+ docs/day.

**Why NOT host the marketing site here?** Single point of failure. Vercel/Netlify/Cloudflare Pages is free for a Nuxt brochure site and won't take your automations down if it gets hugged by HN.

---

## Provision the VPS (Hetzner)

1. https://console.hetzner.cloud → Create Project: "Nordic Nerd Ops".
2. Add Server:
   - Location: Ashburn, VA (US East — lowest latency from FL)
   - Image: Ubuntu 24.04 LTS
   - Type: **CX22** (2 vCPU, 4GB RAM, 40GB SSD) — €4.51/mo
   - SSH Key: paste your ED25519 public key. Don't allow root password login.
   - Firewall: create one, allow only 22, 80, 443 inbound
   - Backups: enable (€0.90/mo) — worth it for set-and-forget DR
3. Note the IPv4 address. Add to Cloudflare DNS:
   ```
   A    n8n          <hetzner-ip>      DNS only (NOT proxied — n8n uses websockets that Cloudflare proxy mangles)
   A    docs         <hetzner-ip>      DNS only
   ```
   `n8n.thenordicnerd.com` → n8n. `docs.thenordicnerd.com` → Documenso.

## First-boot hardening

SSH in as root (one time):

```bash
# Update
apt update && apt upgrade -y

# Create non-root user
adduser nordic
usermod -aG sudo nordic

# Copy SSH keys
rsync --archive --chown=nordic:nordic ~/.ssh /home/nordic

# Disable root login + password auth
sed -i 's/^#*PermitRootLogin.*/PermitRootLogin no/' /etc/ssh/sshd_config
sed -i 's/^#*PasswordAuthentication.*/PasswordAuthentication no/' /etc/ssh/sshd_config
systemctl restart sshd

# UFW firewall (Hetzner's firewall is already filtering, but defense in depth)
apt install -y ufw
ufw default deny incoming
ufw default allow outgoing
ufw allow 22/tcp
ufw allow 80/tcp
ufw allow 443/tcp
ufw --force enable

# Fail2ban
apt install -y fail2ban
systemctl enable --now fail2ban

# Unattended security upgrades
apt install -y unattended-upgrades
dpkg-reconfigure --priority=low unattended-upgrades
```

Log out and back in as `nordic` from here on. Verify root SSH is dead (`ssh root@...` should fail).

## Install Docker + Compose

```bash
# Official docker install
curl -fsSL https://get.docker.com | sh
sudo usermod -aG docker nordic
# Log out / log back in for group change
```

## Directory layout

```
/home/nordic/nordic-ops/
  ├── docker-compose.yml
  ├── .env
  ├── caddy/
  │   └── Caddyfile
  ├── n8n/
  │   └── (data volume)
  ├── documenso/
  │   └── (data volume)
  ├── postgres/
  │   └── (data volume)
  └── backups/
      └── (nightly snapshots, mirrored to S3)
```

## `docker-compose.yml`

```yaml
version: "3.9"

networks:
  nordic-net:
    driver: bridge

volumes:
  caddy_data:
  caddy_config:
  postgres_data:
  n8n_data:
  documenso_data:

services:
  caddy:
    image: caddy:2-alpine
    container_name: caddy
    restart: unless-stopped
    ports:
      - "80:80"
      - "443:443"
    volumes:
      - ./caddy/Caddyfile:/etc/caddy/Caddyfile:ro
      - caddy_data:/data
      - caddy_config:/config
    networks:
      - nordic-net

  postgres:
    image: postgres:16-alpine
    container_name: postgres
    restart: unless-stopped
    environment:
      POSTGRES_USER: ${POSTGRES_USER}
      POSTGRES_PASSWORD: ${POSTGRES_PASSWORD}
      POSTGRES_DB: postgres
    volumes:
      - postgres_data:/var/lib/postgresql/data
      - ./postgres/init.sql:/docker-entrypoint-initdb.d/init.sql:ro
    networks:
      - nordic-net
    healthcheck:
      test: ["CMD-SHELL", "pg_isready -U ${POSTGRES_USER}"]
      interval: 10s
      retries: 5

  n8n:
    image: n8nio/n8n:latest
    container_name: n8n
    restart: unless-stopped
    environment:
      DB_TYPE: postgresdb
      DB_POSTGRESDB_HOST: postgres
      DB_POSTGRESDB_DATABASE: n8n
      DB_POSTGRESDB_USER: ${POSTGRES_USER}
      DB_POSTGRESDB_PASSWORD: ${POSTGRES_PASSWORD}
      N8N_HOST: n8n.thenordicnerd.com
      N8N_PROTOCOL: https
      WEBHOOK_URL: https://n8n.thenordicnerd.com/
      N8N_PORT: 5678
      N8N_ENCRYPTION_KEY: ${N8N_ENCRYPTION_KEY}
      N8N_BASIC_AUTH_ACTIVE: "true"
      N8N_BASIC_AUTH_USER: ${N8N_BASIC_AUTH_USER}
      N8N_BASIC_AUTH_PASSWORD: ${N8N_BASIC_AUTH_PASSWORD}
      GENERIC_TIMEZONE: America/New_York
      TZ: America/New_York
      EXECUTIONS_DATA_PRUNE: "true"
      EXECUTIONS_DATA_MAX_AGE: 168
    volumes:
      - n8n_data:/home/node/.n8n
    depends_on:
      postgres:
        condition: service_healthy
    networks:
      - nordic-net

  documenso:
    image: documenso/documenso:latest
    container_name: documenso
    restart: unless-stopped
    environment:
      NEXTAUTH_URL: https://docs.thenordicnerd.com
      NEXTAUTH_SECRET: ${DOCUMENSO_NEXTAUTH_SECRET}
      NEXT_PRIVATE_ENCRYPTION_KEY: ${DOCUMENSO_ENCRYPTION_KEY}
      NEXT_PRIVATE_ENCRYPTION_SECONDARY_KEY: ${DOCUMENSO_ENCRYPTION_SECONDARY_KEY}
      NEXT_PUBLIC_WEBAPP_URL: https://docs.thenordicnerd.com
      NEXT_PRIVATE_DATABASE_URL: postgres://${POSTGRES_USER}:${POSTGRES_PASSWORD}@postgres:5432/documenso
      NEXT_PRIVATE_DIRECT_DATABASE_URL: postgres://${POSTGRES_USER}:${POSTGRES_PASSWORD}@postgres:5432/documenso
      NEXT_PRIVATE_SMTP_TRANSPORT: smtp
      NEXT_PRIVATE_SMTP_HOST: smtp.gmail.com
      NEXT_PRIVATE_SMTP_PORT: 587
      NEXT_PRIVATE_SMTP_USERNAME: adam@thenordicnerd.com
      NEXT_PRIVATE_SMTP_PASSWORD: ${GMAIL_APP_PASSWORD}
      NEXT_PRIVATE_SMTP_FROM_NAME: "The Nordic Nerd"
      NEXT_PRIVATE_SMTP_FROM_ADDRESS: adam@thenordicnerd.com
      NEXT_PRIVATE_SIGNING_TRANSPORT: local
    volumes:
      - documenso_data:/app/data
    depends_on:
      postgres:
        condition: service_healthy
    networks:
      - nordic-net
```

## `postgres/init.sql`

```sql
CREATE DATABASE n8n;
CREATE DATABASE documenso;
```

## `.env` (chmod 600)

```dotenv
POSTGRES_USER=nordic_db
POSTGRES_PASSWORD=<32-char random>

N8N_ENCRYPTION_KEY=<32-char random>
N8N_BASIC_AUTH_USER=adam
N8N_BASIC_AUTH_PASSWORD=<32-char random>

DOCUMENSO_NEXTAUTH_SECRET=<32-char random>
DOCUMENSO_ENCRYPTION_KEY=<32-char random>
DOCUMENSO_ENCRYPTION_SECONDARY_KEY=<32-char random>

GMAIL_APP_PASSWORD=<App Password from Google Account → Security → App Passwords>
```

Generate randoms with `openssl rand -base64 24` per value. Never commit `.env`.

## `caddy/Caddyfile`

```
n8n.thenordicnerd.com {
    reverse_proxy n8n:5678
    encode gzip
}

docs.thenordicnerd.com {
    reverse_proxy documenso:3000
    encode gzip
}
```

Caddy auto-issues Let's Encrypt certs on first request to each host. No cert management.

## Boot the stack

```bash
cd /home/nordic/nordic-ops
docker compose up -d
docker compose logs -f
```

Wait for "n8n ready on port 5678" and Documenso's startup logs. Hit https://n8n.thenordicnerd.com (basic auth: `adam` + password) and https://docs.thenordicnerd.com.

## Backups

Nightly `pg_dump` of all databases + tarball of Docker volumes, pushed to S3 (or Backblaze B2, cheaper).

`backups/backup.sh`:

```bash
#!/usr/bin/env bash
set -euo pipefail

TS=$(date +%Y%m%d_%H%M%S)
BACKUP_DIR=/home/nordic/nordic-ops/backups/$TS
mkdir -p "$BACKUP_DIR"

# DB dumps
docker exec postgres pg_dump -U nordic_db n8n        | gzip > "$BACKUP_DIR/n8n.sql.gz"
docker exec postgres pg_dump -U nordic_db documenso  | gzip > "$BACKUP_DIR/documenso.sql.gz"

# Volume tarballs (small enough to copy hot for this scale)
docker run --rm -v nordic-ops_n8n_data:/data -v "$BACKUP_DIR":/backup alpine \
  tar czf /backup/n8n_data.tar.gz -C /data .
docker run --rm -v nordic-ops_documenso_data:/data -v "$BACKUP_DIR":/backup alpine \
  tar czf /backup/documenso_data.tar.gz -C /data .

# Upload to B2 (install b2 CLI separately)
b2 sync "$BACKUP_DIR" b2://nordic-ops-backups/$TS

# Retain 14 days locally, 90 days remote
find /home/nordic/nordic-ops/backups -mindepth 1 -maxdepth 1 -mtime +14 -exec rm -rf {} +
```

Cron entry: `0 3 * * * /home/nordic/nordic-ops/backups/backup.sh >> /var/log/nordic-backup.log 2>&1`

## Updates

Monthly cadence (first Sunday of each month):

```bash
cd /home/nordic/nordic-ops
docker compose pull
docker compose up -d
docker image prune -af
```

Always read n8n + Documenso release notes before pulling. n8n breaking changes are rare but happen.

## Monitoring

Lightweight: Uptime Kuma in a tiny additional container, or use a free external monitor (UptimeRobot, Better Stack) hitting `n8n.thenordicnerd.com/healthz` every 5 min. Slack/email on failure.

## Disaster recovery test

Quarterly: spin up a second VPS, restore from latest B2 backup, confirm n8n workflows + Documenso load correctly. If you can't restore in <60 minutes, you don't have backups.

files.title: Smartlead Setup
files.content:
# Smartlead Setup

**One-line:** Connect 3 cold inboxes, run 14–21 day warmup, then launch campaigns. Never skip warmup.

---

## Account creation

1. Sign up at https://www.smartlead.ai — Basic plan at $39/mo covers 3 inboxes and 6,000 emails/mo, plenty for launch volume.
2. Use `adam@thenordicnerd.com` for login (not a cold inbox).
3. Enable 2FA.

## Connect inboxes

In Smartlead → Email Accounts → Add Account. Choose "Google / Gmail OAuth" for each:

1. `adam@nordicnerdhq.com`
2. `hello@nordicnerdhq.com`
3. `adam@getnordicnerd.com`

Smartlead opens a Google OAuth flow for each. Authorize. It auto-runs SPF/DKIM/DMARC checks and refuses to proceed if records are wrong (this is good — it's catching DNS bugs before they cost you reputation).

If any check fails, fix DNS first per `dns-and-domain-plan.md`. Don't override.

---

## Warmup configuration

Per inbox, set the warmup ramp. Smartlead's default is decent; the table below is slightly more conservative.

| Day | Emails sent/day | Reply rate | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1–3 | 5 | 50% | Smartlead's warmup network exchanges real-feeling emails with your inbox |
| 4–6 | 10 | 45% | Stay below 15 sends/day until DMARC reports look clean |
| 7–10 | 15 | 40% | Check Google Postmaster Tools for IP/domain reputation |
| 11–14 | 25 | 35% | Add inbox to a low-volume campaign at end of day 14 |
| 15–21 | 35 | 30% | Ramping toward steady-state |
| 22+ | 40 | 30% (ongoing) | Steady-state. Always keep warmup running in parallel with live campaigns. |

**Why keep warmup running forever?** Even a perfect-reputation inbox benefits from ongoing warmup traffic — it dilutes any individual campaign's reputation damage and keeps reply-rate signals high for inbox providers.

### Per-inbox daily send cap (when live)
- Day 22+: **40 emails/day** per inbox
- 3 inboxes × 40 = **120 cold emails/day**, ~600/week, ~2,400/month
- Plenty for 100 high-quality contractor prospects/week, with room for follow-ups

Don't push past 50/day per inbox without 60+ days of clean reputation data. Gmail starts soft-rate-limiting you and reply rates crater.

---

## Campaign config — first campaign

When warmup hits day 15 and reputation looks clean, create your first campaign.

### Settings

```
Name:                Polk Contractors V1 - {{niche}}
From email rotation: All 3 inboxes (round-robin)
Sending days:        Tue, Wed, Thu, Fri  (skip Mon and weekends — best open rates)
Sending hours:       9am – 4pm America/New_York
Send interval:       300 seconds (5 min) between messages
Daily limit:         Inherits from inbox cap (40/day each)
Stop on reply:       YES — pause sequence for that contact when they reply
Track opens:         OFF  (open tracking pixels hurt deliverability + are unreliable post-MPP)
Track clicks:        ON   (only if you have a link; cold first email shouldn't)
```

**Why opens off, clicks on?** Apple Mail Privacy Protection makes opens unreliable (pre-fetches everything) and the tracking pixel itself flags some spam filters. Clicks are still real signals.

### Sequence

Imported from `03-Outbound-System/sequences/polk-contractor-sequence-v1.md`. 4 emails over 14 days. Smartlead supports the same merge fields as the file: `{{first_name}}`, `{{company_name}}`, `{{city}}`, `{{first_line}}`, `{{niche}}`.

### Pre-launch checklist
- [ ] DNS verified (all 3 inboxes pass Smartlead's check)
- [ ] Warmup at day 15+ for all 3 inboxes
- [ ] First-line generation working in n8n (test on 10 contacts, eyeball each)
- [ ] List verified through MillionVerifier (>=95% deliverable)
- [ ] Physical address filled into footer (CAN-SPAM)
- [ ] Unsubscribe link or "reply STOP" present in every email
- [ ] Reply-handling n8n workflow live and posting to your inbox/Slack

## Reply routing

Smartlead → Settings → Webhooks → add the URL from your n8n reply-triage workflow:

```
https://n8n.thenordicnerd.com/webhook/smartlead-reply
```

Event: `email_reply`. n8n picks it up, classifies it via Claude API, drafts a response, and either auto-pauses + drafts or escalates to you (see `03-Outbound-System/reply-handling/`).

## Bounces and unsubscribes

- Hard bounces: Smartlead auto-suppresses. Verify suppression is on in campaign settings.
- Soft bounces: 3-strikes-and-suppress rule (Smartlead default).
- Unsubscribes: Add a global suppression list. Anyone who replies "STOP", "unsubscribe", or whose email contains a clear opt-out goes here forever. n8n reply-classification handles routing these.

## Daily checks (5 min, every morning)

1. Inbox health dashboard — any inbox showing reputation drop? Pause it, investigate.
2. Reply queue — anything that needs human response?
3. Bounce rate trend — should be <3% rolling 7-day. If higher, pause and re-verify your list.
4. Campaign send rate — is each inbox hitting target volume? If under, something's blocking sends.

This is the morning-review checklist in `06-Daily-Ops/`.

## When to scale

After 60 days of clean sending + 5% reply rate + 1+ booked call/week, options for scaling:
1. Add 2–3 more inboxes to the same Smartlead account, same warmup pattern.
2. Add a fourth cold domain after 90 days.
3. Move to a higher Smartlead plan only if Basic's 6k/mo limit becomes binding.

Don't scale outbound before delivery is solid. A pipeline you can't fulfill is worse than no pipeline.
files.excerpt: 1. Sign up at https://www.smartlead.ai — Basic plan at $39/mo covers 3 inboxes and 6,000 emails/mo, plenty for launch volume.

files.title: Stripe Setup
files.content:
# Stripe Setup

**One Stripe account. Three Products (Starter, Growth, Authority). Subscriptions for the retainers. Customer Portal for clients to manage their own card.**

Stripe's tax/business compliance is solid out of the box for FL service businesses. Don't overthink it.

---

## Account setup

1. https://dashboard.stripe.com/register
2. Use `billing@thenordicnerd.com` (the alias forwarding to your primary inbox).
3. Connect to LLC: business EIN, business bank account (Mercury/Relay). Personal SSN only if LLC isn't filed yet — switch as soon as it is.
4. Set timezone America/New_York.
5. Enable 2FA. Add team backup (just you for now, but consider a personal email as backup for break-glass).
6. Statement descriptor: `THENORDICNERD` (max 22 chars; visible on client card statements).
7. Branding: upload logo + brand color in Settings → Branding (affects Checkout + emails + Customer Portal).
8. Enable Tax: Settings → Tax. For FL service businesses to FL clients, sales tax usually doesn't apply to web services (verify with attorney/CPA). Leave Tax DISABLED until confirmed.

## Products + Prices

In Products → Add Product. Three products, one price each. Subscriptions are billed monthly.

### Product 1: Starter

```
Name:        Done-For-You Local Presence — Starter
Description: Nuxt 3 site + Google Business Profile optimization + monthly reporting. 6-month minimum term.
Pricing:     $1,500.00 USD / month  recurring
Billing:     Monthly
Trial:       0 days
Tax behavior: Exclusive (Stripe adds tax if/when enabled)
Metadata:
  - tier: starter
  - mrr_value: 1500
  - contract_months: 6
```

### Product 2: Growth

```
Name:        Done-For-You Local Presence — Growth
Description: Everything in Starter, plus 4 local SEO city/niche pages, 2 blog posts/month, review automation, monthly 30-min check-in. 6-month minimum term.
Pricing:     $2,000.00 USD / month  recurring
Metadata:
  - tier: growth
  - mrr_value: 2000
  - contract_months: 6
```

### Product 3: Authority

```
Name:        Done-For-You Local Presence — Authority
Description: Everything in Growth, plus 8 local SEO pages/month, 4 blog posts/month, monthly 60-min strategy call, priority support, quarterly competitor audit. 6-month minimum term.
Pricing:     $2,500.00 USD / month  recurring
Metadata:
  - tier: authority
  - mrr_value: 2500
  - contract_months: 6
```

Save the resulting `price_xxx` IDs to 1Password + add as env vars / n8n credentials:

```
STRIPE_PRICE_STARTER=price_xxxxxxxxxxxxxx
STRIPE_PRICE_GROWTH=price_xxxxxxxxxxxxxx
STRIPE_PRICE_AUTHORITY=price_xxxxxxxxxxxxxx
```

## Customer Portal

Settings → Billing → Customer Portal → Activate.

Configuration:

- **Allow customers to update payment methods**: YES
- **Allow customers to update invoices info**: YES
- **Allow customers to view invoice history**: YES
- **Allow customers to update billing address**: YES
- **Cancellation**: **Off** initially (force them to email you to cancel — gives you a chance to save the relationship; also enforces the 6-month minimum). You can revisit at month 12.
- **Subscription updates** (upgrade/downgrade): **Off** initially. Tier changes happen through you so you can re-scope deliverables.

Portal URL: `https://billing.stripe.com/p/login/<id>` (Stripe generates). Include this in onboarding email + monthly reports.

## Checkout / payment collection

Two patterns:

### Pattern A: Documenso-driven (default flow)
1. Client signs retainer in Documenso.
2. Documenso webhook hits n8n.
3. n8n creates a Customer in Stripe + a Subscription with the price ID matching the proposed tier.
4. n8n sends a hosted-invoice link to the client for the first month's charge (Stripe's `invoice.send` endpoint).
5. Client pays via the hosted invoice; future months auto-charge the saved card.

This avoids requiring the client to fill out card info during the signing flow — they sign first, get the invoice, and pay separately. Friendly for contractors who'd rather see paperwork before card.

### Pattern B: Stripe-driven (faster but loses signing step)
Stripe Payment Links → one link per tier. Client clicks, enters card, subscribes. Use only if you've already got a signed contract via a separate flow.

**Use Pattern A for the first 10 clients.** It enforces the contract before money moves.

## Webhooks (n8n endpoints)

Settings → Developers → Webhooks → Add endpoint:

```
URL:          https://n8n.thenordicnerd.com/webhook/stripe
Events:       customer.subscription.created
              customer.subscription.updated
              customer.subscription.deleted
              invoice.paid
              invoice.payment_failed
              charge.refunded
```

Each event maps to an action in `05-signed-to-onboarding.json` and `pipeline-agent.md`:

- `customer.subscription.created` → Move HubSpot deal to Won, start MailerLite onboarding, send welcome email.
- `invoice.paid` → Log revenue to Wave (manual review), Slack ping, increment scoreboard.
- `invoice.payment_failed` → Slack high-priority ping, n8n sends a friendly retry email, HubSpot task.
- `customer.subscription.deleted` → Slack ping, retro task, MailerLite churn list.

Get the webhook signing secret (`whsec_xxx`) and store in n8n.

## Tax handling

For FL-based service businesses selling to FL clients, the default position is no FL sales tax on professional/digital services. **Verify with CPA before invoicing.** If you take out-of-state clients, Stripe Tax can auto-calculate but you'll need to register in states where you exceed nexus thresholds — most agencies never hit this.

For now: keep Stripe Tax OFF, monitor revenue by state quarterly, revisit with CPA at year-end.

## Refund policy

Stated in retainer agreement: month-to-month, no pro-rated refunds within an active month. If client cancels (after 6-month minimum), service ends at end of current billing cycle, no refund. Exceptions are case-by-case and goodwill-based.

Stripe's refund UI: Dashboard → Payment → Refund. Document each refund's reason in a Wave note for clean books.

## Invoicing for one-off services (later)

Some clients may want a one-off Nuxt build (no retainer). Use Stripe Invoicing:

1. Dashboard → Invoices → New invoice.
2. Customer (existing or new).
3. Add line item (custom amount + description).
4. Send.

Don't build a custom flow for this until you've done 5+ of them.

## Reconciliation

Monthly close (15 min, last business day of month):

1. Stripe Dashboard → Reports → Balance summary for month.
2. Compare to Wave's revenue total.
3. Compare to HubSpot's "Won this month" deal sum.
4. Mismatches >$50 → investigate that day.

## Failure modes to watch

- **Payment failed retry exhaustion** — Stripe retries 4 times over 3 weeks then gives up. Your n8n flow should ping you on the first failure so you can call the client.
- **Card update needed but customer not engaged** — Customer Portal email goes to spam. Always follow up by direct email after a failed payment.
- **Subscription created but proposal not signed** — Bug somewhere in your automation. Reconcile monthly.
- **Disputes / chargebacks** — Notify immediately. Respond within 7 days. Document everything from the original conversation in HubSpot.

## API keys

`sk_test_xxx` for the test mode, `sk_live_xxx` for production. Store both in 1Password. Never commit. n8n stores them as credentials, referenced by name only in workflow JSON.

## Going live

Stripe accounts start in test mode. Once you've completed the activation form (business info, ID, banking), Stripe flips you to live. Don't accept the first real client until live mode is fully active and you've test-charged $1 to yourself and refunded it.

files.title: Tools & Accounts — Master Inventory
files.content:
# Tools & Accounts — Master Inventory

**Total fixed monthly burn at launch:** ~$220/mo (excludes per-use APIs, contractor scrapes, paid ads if added later)
**Account principle:** Every login uses `adam@thenordicnerd.com` once Google Workspace is live. Cold-inbox logins use their respective addresses.

Order of operations is in `SUMMARY.md`; this file is the inventory.

---

## Tier 1 — Foundation (set up week 1)

| Tool | Purpose | Plan | Cost/mo | Signup |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Cloudflare** | Domain registrar, DNS, email routing | Free | $0 | https://dash.cloudflare.com/sign-up |
| **Google Workspace** | Primary inbox + Drive + Docs on `thenordicnerd.com` | Business Starter | $7 | https://workspace.google.com |
| **Google Workspace** | 3 cold inboxes on cold domain | Business Starter × 3 seats on secondary | $21 | (same, on `nordicnerdhq.com`) |
| **Hetzner Cloud** | VPS for self-hosted n8n + Documenso | CX22 (2 vCPU, 4GB RAM) | €4.51 (~$5) | https://www.hetzner.com/cloud |
| **Stripe** | Payments | Pay-as-you-go (2.9% + 30¢) | $0 fixed | https://stripe.com (after LLC + EIN) |
| **Mercury** or **Relay** | Business banking | Free | $0 | https://mercury.com  or  https://relayfi.com |
| **Wave** | Bookkeeping | Free | $0 | https://www.waveapps.com |

## Tier 2 — Outbound (set up weeks 2–3, after DNS warm)

| Tool | Purpose | Plan | Cost/mo | Signup |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Smartlead** | Cold email sequencer | Basic (3 inboxes, 6k/mo) | $39 | https://www.smartlead.ai |
| **Apify** | Scraping Google Maps for contractor leads | Pay-as-you-go starter | ~$30 (variable) | https://apify.com |
| **Hunter.io** | Email finding by domain | Starter | $49 | https://hunter.io |
| **MillionVerifier** | Email verification before sending | Pay-as-you-go (1000 verifies/$5) | ~$10 | https://www.millionverifier.com |

## Tier 3 — Pipeline & Delivery (set up week 3)

| Tool | Purpose | Plan | Cost/mo | Signup |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **HubSpot CRM** | Deal pipeline, contact mgmt, lightweight automation | Free | $0 | https://www.hubspot.com/products/crm |
| **Cal.com** | Discovery call booking, embedded on site | Free (self-hosted optional) | $0 | https://cal.com |
| **Documenso** | Self-hosted e-sign, same VPS as n8n | Self-hosted | $0 (in VPS cost) | https://documenso.com |
| **MailerLite** | Client onboarding email automations + ad-hoc newsletter | Free up to 1k subs | $0 | https://www.mailerlite.com |

## Tier 4 — AI & Operations

| Tool | Purpose | Plan | Cost/mo | Signup |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Anthropic API** | Claude API for first-lines, classification, drafts, proposals | Pay-as-you-go | ~$25 est. | https://console.anthropic.com |
| **Notion** | Internal SOPs, client wikis, content calendar | Free (personal) | $0 | https://www.notion.so |
| **n8n** | Self-hosted automation (on VPS) | OSS edition | $0 (in VPS cost) | https://n8n.io |

## Tier 5 — Optional / later

| Tool | Purpose | When to add |
|---|---|---|
| **Loom** | Async client demos | After first 2 clients |
| **Slack** or **Discord** | Client comms if they prefer over email | When a client asks |
| **Plausible / Fathom** | Privacy-friendly analytics on `thenordicnerd.com` | Pre-launch site |
| **Sentry** | Error tracking on Nuxt builds | When you ship the first client site |
| **GitHub** (personal) | Code hosting | Already have; just confirm personal account |

---

## Monthly summary

| Phase | Tools live | ~Monthly cost |
|---|---|---|
| Week 1 | Foundation only | ~$35 (Workspace primary + VPS + cold domain reg amortized) |
| Week 3 | + Outbound + Pipeline | ~$180 |
| Steady-state | All Tier 1–4 | ~$220 |
| With paid ads (if added) | + ad spend | varies |

## Account creation order

1. Cloudflare → register `thenordicnerd.com`, `nordicnerdhq.com`, `getnordicnerd.com`
2. Google Workspace → primary on `thenordicnerd.com`
3. SunBiz.org → file FL LLC
4. IRS → EIN (free, online, instant)
5. Mercury/Relay → business banking (needs EIN)
6. Stripe → connect to LLC
7. Hetzner → spin up VPS (see `n8n-deployment.md`)
8. Anthropic Console → create API key, store in 1Password
9. Google Workspace → second tenant for cold inboxes
10. Smartlead → connect cold inboxes, start warmup
11. Apify, Hunter, MillionVerifier → connect via n8n
12. HubSpot, Cal.com, MailerLite, Documenso, Notion, Wave — order doesn't matter much, do in one block

---

## Credential storage

Every credential goes in 1Password under a single "Nordic Nerd" vault. Tag with the tool name. Never reuse a password. Use 1Password's generator (24-char, mixed) by default. Enable 2FA on every tool that supports it (most do).

For API keys consumed by n8n: store as n8n credentials (encrypted at rest) and reference by name in workflow JSON — never hardcode.

files.title: Client Delivery Agent — Rules
files.content:
# Client Delivery Agent — Rules

**Identity:** You are the Client Delivery Agent for The Nordic Nerd. From the moment a client signs and pays, you own the work: Nuxt 3 site builds, local SEO city/niche pages, Google Business Profile optimization, monthly content (Growth+/Authority tiers), and monthly reporting.

**One-line scope:** Deliver what's promised in the retainer, every month, on time, and prove it with a report.

---

## What you own

### Day 0–30 (onboarding)
1. Welcome email (within 1 hour of signed proposal).
2. Discovery questionnaire sent (within 24 hours; due back within 7 days).
3. Brand assets intake (logo, photos, color preferences, fonts) — chase if not received.
4. GBP access acquired (manager role on their listing).
5. Site build kickoff: Nuxt 3 starter scaffolded from template, dev env shared with client preview link.
6. Domain handling: client owns domain; you set up Cloudflare DNS + Nuxt deployment.
7. Site v1 shipped by day 21 (for Starter tier).
8. GBP audit + optimization complete by day 14.
9. Day-30 check-in call (Growth+/Authority tiers).

### Monthly recurring
1. **GBP maintenance**: new photos posted (3+/mo), services updated, posts published weekly.
2. **Local SEO pages**: per-tier counts (Starter: 0/mo after initial 3, Growth: 4/mo, Authority: 8/mo).
3. **Blog posts**: Starter: 0, Growth: 2/mo, Authority: 4/mo.
4. **Review requests**: automated workflow + monthly nudge for clients to enable it post-job.
5. **Monthly report**: sent by 5th of the following month. Format in `04-Client-Templates/monthly-reporting-template.md`.
6. **Monthly check-in call**: Growth (30 min) and Authority (60 min) tiers only.

## What you do NOT own

- Sales or proposals — Pipeline Agent.
- Outbound — Outbound Ops / Copywriter.
- Bookkeeping or invoicing — Pipeline Agent → Stripe.
- Anything that would require licensure (legal, accounting, code-required structural work).

## Tier-specific deliverable matrix

| Deliverable | Starter ($1.5K) | Growth ($2K) | Authority ($2.5K) |
|---|---|---|---|
| Nuxt 3 site | Yes (5 pages) | Yes (5 + 4 SEO pages) | Yes (5 + 8 SEO pages) |
| GBP setup + audit | Yes | Yes | Yes |
| Monthly GBP maintenance | Yes | Yes | Yes |
| Local SEO pages/mo (after launch) | 0 | 4 | 8 |
| Blog posts/mo | 0 | 2 | 4 |
| Review request automation | Setup only | Setup + monitor | Setup + monitor + respond |
| Monthly report | Yes | Yes | Yes |
| Monthly check-in call | No | 30 min | 60 min |
| Quarterly competitor audit | No | No | Yes |
| Priority support (response SLA) | 48h | 24h | 24h |
| Revisions/round per month | 1 | 2 | Unlimited (reasonable) |

## Site build playbook (every client)

1. **Template**: start from internal `nordic-nuxt-contractor` template (Nuxt 3, Tailwind, Nuxt Content for blog/SEO pages, Nuxt Image for photo optimization, Schema.org `LocalBusiness` JSON-LD on every page).
2. **Page set (standard 5)**:
   - Home (hero, services, recent work, reviews, CTA)
   - About (story, team, service area)
   - Services (with links to per-service deep pages)
   - Recent Work / Gallery (pulls from GBP photos + client-provided)
   - Contact (form to client's email + Cal.com embed if they take calls)
3. **Per-page checklist**:
   - H1 reflects primary keyword for the location ("Kitchen remodelers in Lakeland, FL")
   - Meta title under 60 chars, includes city + niche
   - Meta description 140–160 chars, includes phone or CTA
   - JSON-LD LocalBusiness with NAP (name/address/phone) consistent with GBP
   - Internal link to at least 2 other pages
   - At least one real photo (no stock photos on production pages)
4. **Performance budget**: Lighthouse Performance ≥90, Accessibility ≥95, SEO 100. Block deploy if below.
5. **Forms**: every contact form posts to client's email AND a HubSpot contact (separate from agency HubSpot — this lives in client's deliverable infra).
6. **Analytics**: Plausible/Fathom installed (privacy-friendly, no cookie banner needed). Goal events: form submit, phone-tap, GBP click.

## Local SEO page playbook (Growth+/Authority)

Use the template in `05-Content/blog/city-niche-page-template.md`. Each page targets a `[niche] in [city], FL` keyword cluster. Schema:

```
H1: [Niche] in [City], FL — [Client Brand]
Hero: 50-word value prop
Section 1: Who we serve in [City] (link to neighborhood mentions)
Section 2: Common [niche] projects in [City] (specific to local building styles, climate)
Section 3: Process (4-step graphic)
Section 4: Recent work in [City] (gallery)
Section 5: FAQ (5 long-tail keywords)
Section 6: Reviews from [City] clients
CTA: Get a quote (form + phone + Cal.com)
```

Word count target: 1200–1800. AI-assisted writing is fine, but every page must be edited by hand for local specificity (building codes, climate references, neighborhood names) — generic AI text gets demoted.

## GBP optimization checklist (day 1 audit)

- [ ] Categories: primary + 3–5 secondary, all relevant
- [ ] Services: every service listed with description and price range (if client OK)
- [ ] Hours: accurate, including special hours for holidays
- [ ] Photos: 25+ photos (interior, exterior, work in progress, team, finished projects)
- [ ] Logo + cover photo
- [ ] Description (750 chars max, use 600–700)
- [ ] Q&A section: 5+ Qs proactively added with answers
- [ ] Booking link, if relevant
- [ ] Attributes (women-owned, veteran-owned, etc.) where applicable
- [ ] First post published (weekly cadence going forward)
- [ ] Service area set (specific cities, not just radius)

## Review request automation

Setup (one-time per client):
1. Identify their CRM/job-management tool (HCSS, JobNimbus, Buildertrend, Markate, simple Google Sheet).
2. Configure n8n integration to trigger on "job complete" event.
3. Trigger sends an SMS (Twilio) + email to customer 2–3 days post-completion with a friendly review-request link to their GBP.
4. If no reply in 7 days, one polite reminder. Then stop.

Ongoing: monthly report shows review count delta + new reviews summary + flag any 1–3 star reviews for client to respond to.

## Monthly report template

See `04-Client-Templates/monthly-reporting-template.md`. The report includes:
- GBP metrics (views, calls, directions, photos viewed) — month-over-month
- Site metrics (visitors, top pages, conversion events)
- Content delivered this month (pages shipped, posts published)
- Reviews added (count + average rating delta)
- Next month's plan
- One "you should know" recommendation (could be a Q&A topic to add, a service to feature, a competitor move)

Report is sent as a PDF + a 2-min Loom walkthrough for Growth+/Authority. Starter gets PDF only.

## Hard rules

1. **Never publish client content without their approval** during first 30 days. After 30 days, you can publish blog posts and SEO pages on schedule unless client opts into pre-review.
2. **Never reuse copy across clients.** Each page is unique. Re-using boilerplate gets pages demoted by Google.
3. **Never make claims you can't verify** in client copy (best in Polk County, #1 contractor, etc.).
4. **Never deploy on Fridays** unless emergency.
5. **Never miss the monthly report deadline.** If material is missing, send the report with what you have + a note about what's coming.
6. **Never set up DNS or domain transfers without explicit client confirmation in writing.**

## Escalations to Adam

- Client asks for work outside scope (Adam decides: do it, scope-creep it, or escalate to tier upgrade).
- Client gives bad feedback on content/site that you can't easily address.
- Client missed a payment (Pipeline Agent already flagged; you're the relationship person).
- Client mentions cancellation or downgrade.
- Any GBP suspension or review-against-policy issue.
- Anything requiring legal language (Terms, Privacy Policy specifics).

## Identity / voice

You are competent, organized, and slightly proud of your work without being needy. When you send a report, you let the numbers speak. You don't beg for thanks. You're proactive in suggesting next month's improvements based on what you saw this month.
