files.title: Sequence Variants by Niche
files.content:
# Sequence Variants by Niche

**Same structure. Different specifics.** All 8 niches use the same 4-email cadence and the same merge-field pattern. What changes is the niche-specific hook in email 2 (when `{{gbp_specific_gap}}` can't be auto-generated) and the credibility examples in email 3.

This file holds the niche-specific blocks. The base sequence in `polk-contractor-sequence-v1.md` is the canonical template; this is the variation library.

---

## General contractors / home builders

**Email 2 fallback gap text:**
> "Your site has 8 project photos. The general contractors ranking on page 1 of `general contractor in {{city}} FL` average 28. Half of them have a separate page per project, with the address-and-photos format that Google's local algorithm explicitly rewards."

**Email 3 credibility example:**
> "Top-ranking GCs in Lakeland and Bartow have one thing in common: 4+ neighborhood-specific landing pages (e.g., `lakeland-highlands-custom-homes`). Yours has one homepage and a services page. That's the gap."

**Tone notes:** GCs are often the most digitally cautious. Avoid jargon. Talk about "leads" and "calls", not "conversion rate optimization".

---

## Kitchen / bath remodelers

**Email 2 fallback gap text:**
> "Remodelers live and die by photos. Yours has 14 on the GBP, mostly exteriors. The remodelers winning {{city}} are posting 30–50 in-progress + finished kitchen shots, with neighborhood mentions in the captions. That's not aesthetic — it's how Google decides who shows up."

**Email 3 credibility example:**
> "The top kitchen remodeler in {{city}} on Google? They started in 2019. You've been in business since [verify and reference]. The only thing they're beating you on is digital — and digital's the part that's mostly mechanical."

**Tone notes:** Remodelers tend to be more design-aware. You can lean slightly more "aesthetic" without losing them. Still no SaaS-speak.

---

## Roofers

**Email 2 fallback gap text:**
> "Roofing is the most competitive Google Ads space in Polk County — CPCs hit $40 in storm season. The roofers who don't pay $40/click are the ones with strong organic rankings + solid GBP. Yours is at {{gbp_optimization_score}}/10. Fix it and you stop paying for clicks you should rank for free."

**Email 3 credibility example:**
> "I'm not going to pretend to know roofing. I know the algorithm side. Look at your top three competitors in {{city}} — the difference between them and you isn't roofing quality, it's whether their digital house is in order."

**Tone notes:** Roofers get the most cold email of any FL contractor niche. Lead with respect for their time, signal you know they're getting blasted.

---

## HVAC

**Email 2 fallback gap text:**
> "HVAC is GBP-dominated. 80% of `ac repair in {{city}}` traffic clicks the map pack, not the organic results. Your GBP photo count, post cadence, and Q&A section are the difference between showing up there or not. Two of those three are blank on yours."

**Email 3 credibility example:**
> "Top HVAC in Lakeland posts to GBP twice a week and has 60+ Q&A entries. It looks like busywork. It's not — Google's local ranking factor for service businesses weights post recency and Q&A density heavily."

**Tone notes:** HVAC owners often work in the field, not at a desk. Email may be checked in evenings. Send in the 5–7pm ET window for this niche specifically.

---

## Electricians

**Email 2 fallback gap text:**
> "Electricians in {{city}} mostly compete on referrals and the GBP map pack. Your site is fine but it's an afterthought to the GBP. The electricians I'd point to as winning this market have 40+ photos, 75+ reviews, and they respond to every review — including the bad ones. You've got [verify] reviews."

**Email 3 credibility example:**
> "Same playbook as the top licensed electricians in Bartow — and they're getting 2–3 calls a day from Google alone. Service-business GBP optimization is genuinely the highest-ROI hour you can spend per month."

**Tone notes:** Electricians appreciate accuracy. Don't generalize. Reference specific GBP attributes.

---

## Plumbers

**Email 2 fallback gap text:**
> "Plumbing is emergency-driven — when a pipe bursts at 11pm Tuesday in Auburndale, the GBP map pack decides who gets that call. Hours, 24/7 service flag, photos of trucks/team, and review velocity are what tip the algorithm. Your listing is missing [specific gap]."

**Email 3 credibility example:**
> "The plumbers booking the most after-hours calls in {{city}} all have: 24/7 hours flagged, 'emergency plumber' as a secondary GBP category, and weekly posts about recent jobs. Three things. Each takes 10 minutes."

**Tone notes:** Plumbers respond well to direct, tactical, "here's the lever" copy. Skip metaphors.

---

## Landscapers / lawn care

**Email 2 fallback gap text:**
> "Landscaping in FL is seasonal but Google search isn't. The landscapers ranking in {{city}} have year-round content — drought-tolerant Florida plant guides, hurricane prep landscaping, etc. — that drags in organic traffic when their physical work slows. Yours doesn't have that lever pulled."

**Email 3 credibility example:**
> "A landscaper I'd benchmark against in Lakeland publishes one 'Florida-yard-specific' post a month. Two years in, that single habit pulls 30% of their leads. Slow burn, real ROI."

**Tone notes:** Landscapers are often smaller, more solo-operator. May be on the Starter tier specifically. Don't oversell — match their scale.

---

## Pool builders

**Email 2 fallback gap text:**
> "Pool builds are high-ticket, low-volume — you only need 8–12 builds a year to be set, but each one is a $40K+ decision the buyer agonizes over for months. The pool builders winning {{city}} have project galleries with full backstories per pool. Yours has a generic grid. Buyers want the build journey, not the result."

**Email 3 credibility example:**
> "Top pool builder GBP listings in Davenport and Lake Wales each have 4+ video walkthroughs of finished projects on the listing. Video is the #1 differentiator in pool-build conversion. You don't need a videographer — phone footage with a short voiceover works."

**Tone notes:** Pool builders often have the most marketing sophistication of the 8 niches. Don't talk down. Lean technical.

---

## Cross-niche notes

**Send-time recommendations** (best reply rates observed in this market):

| Niche | Best send window (ET) |
|---|---|
| GCs / home builders | Tue–Thu, 9–11am |
| Kitchen / bath | Tue–Wed, 9–11am or Thu 1–3pm |
| Roofing | Tue–Thu, 10am–noon |
| HVAC | Tue–Thu, 5–7pm |
| Electricians | Tue–Thu, 5–7pm |
| Plumbers | Tue–Thu, 5–7pm |
| Landscapers | Tue–Wed, 7–9am |
| Pool builders | Tue–Wed, 9–11am |

(Service-trade niches that work outdoors all day check email evenings. Pre-9am pulls landscapers.)

**Geographic note:** Davenport and Haines City have heavy Disney-corridor traffic. Some contractors there serve both Polk and the I-4 corridor. Their digital sophistication tends to be higher; expect lower reply rate but higher close rate.

## When to spin off a new sequence

If a niche shows reply rate >2x the average AND you have enough flow there, build a niche-dedicated sequence (e.g., `pool-builders-sequence-v1.md`). Niche-dedicated sequences can be 5–6 emails because the audience is narrower; you can afford more touches.
files.excerpt: This file holds the niche-specific blocks. The base sequence in `polk-contractor-sequence-v1.md` is the canonical template; this is the variation library.

files.title: Subject Line Bank
files.content:
# Subject Line Bank

**30 subjects. Test in cohorts of 5. Always lowercase. Always under 50 chars. Always specific to either the city, the niche, or a concrete number.**

Subject lines are the single highest-leverage variable in cold email. A bad subject means the email is unread, so the body doesn't matter. Test ruthlessly, kill losers fast, keep winners moving forward.

---

## How to A/B test

1. Pick 5 from below.
2. Split 100 sends across them (20 each). Same list, same body, same time of day.
3. After 100 sends per variant (500 total), look at reply rate (not open rate — opens are unreliable post-MPP).
4. Keep top 2. Kill bottom 3. Add 3 fresh ones. Repeat.
5. Significance bar: 30% relative lift = signal. Less = noise.

A new winner becomes the v1 default in the sequence file.

---

## 30 subject lines — categorized

### Category A: city-specific (lean on geographic relevance)

```
1. quick question about your {{city}} site
2. {{city}} {{niche}} — one thing worth fixing
3. your {{city}} listing vs your competitors
4. saw you in the {{city}} map pack
5. {{city}} GBP audit — anything useful here?
```

### Category B: niche-specific (lean on category insight)

```
6. one thing most {{niche}} miss
7. {{niche}} sites in Polk County
8. why {{niche}} are losing to Google ads
9. {{niche}} GBP photos question
10. how your {{niche}} listing compares
```

### Category C: numbers + specificity (concrete = credible)

```
11. your GBP scored {{gbp_optimization_score}}/10
12. 5 things separating top {{city}} {{niche}}
13. 3 things I'd change about {{company_name}}'s site
14. {{gbp_optimization_score}}/10 — quick audit
15. {{company_name}} ranks #{{rank_proxy}} for "{{primary_keyword}}"
```

### Category D: contrarian / interrupt (high-risk, high-reward)

```
16. don't buy a website yet
17. ignore your marketing agency
18. SEO is mostly nonsense, except this
19. stop posting on Facebook
20. why I'm not pitching you
```

### Category E: peer-to-peer / casual (the "fellow tradesperson" angle)

```
21. fellow Winter Haven nerd here
22. quick note from another local
23. neighbor in Winter Haven
24. another small business in Polk
25. you and I have the same problem
```

### Category F: question-first (forces brain engagement)

```
26. who handles your website?
27. is your GBP getting calls?
28. how many leads from Google this month?
29. own the domain, or does someone else?
30. seen your competitor's GBP?
```

---

## Recommended initial test cohorts

**Cohort 1 (week 1):** 1, 6, 11, 16, 21 — one from each category to find the strongest *category*.

**Cohort 2 (week 2):** Keep the top 2 winners from Cohort 1. Replace bottom 3 with 2, 7, 12.

**Cohort 3 (week 3):** Iterate. By now you should know which category wins; switch testing to within-category variants.

---

## Known anti-patterns (do not test)

| Bad subject | Why |
|---|---|
| "Boost your business in Lakeland" | Generic. Verb-driven. Reads like an ad. |
| "Free SEO audit" | Triggers spam filters + reads cheap. |
| "Hi {{first_name}}" | Lazy. Many spam filters catch this exact pattern. |
| "Are you ready to scale?" | Cliche. Question that has no real answer. |
| "{{Company}} — Important" | False urgency. Erodes trust. |
| ALL CAPS or "!!" | Spam signal. |
| Emojis in subject | Tanks deliverability on cold sends. |
| "Re:" without "Re:" being legitimate (i.e., on email 1) | Dishonest. Filters catch the inconsistency anyway. |

`Re:` IS legitimate on email 2+ when you're literally following up on the same thread — that's why the sequence file uses it on email 2.

---

## Tracking template

Maintain a log of every variant's performance.

| Subject | Niche | City | Sends | Replies | Reply % | Positive % | Status |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| quick question about your lakeland site | GC | Lakeland | 100 | 7 | 7.0% | 50% | Active |
| your GBP scored 4/10 | GC | Lakeland | 100 | 4 | 4.0% | 75% | Active |
| ... | | | | | | | |

Auto-generated by `weekly-metrics` n8n workflow. Sort by reply % to find winners.

## Subjects vs. body interaction

A subject that gets a high open but low reply means the body's not delivering on the subject's promise. Track both.

A subject like "5 things separating top Lakeland kitchen remodelers" sets up a body that better deliver 5 specific things — or at least one specific thing with a promise to share the rest on a call. Mismatches kill trust fast.

files.title: Discovery Call Script
files.content:
# Discovery Call Script

**Goal:** 15–20 minute call. By the end: you know what tier they fit, they know whether you can help, both decide whether to send a proposal.

**Mindset:** This isn't a sales call. It's a fit call. Half the time you'll decide they're not a fit (wrong stage, wrong niche, wrong budget). Saying no on the call is faster and cleaner than chasing them later.

---

## Before the call (auto-generated prep brief)

The n8n booking workflow generates and Slacks you a prep brief 24h before. Re-read it 5 min before the call. The brief contains:
- Their company, city, niche, GBP score
- The first-line that earned the reply
- Their reply text + thread history
- Three suggested angles to probe
- Tier fit hypothesis

If the brief didn't generate, manually pull from HubSpot.

## Opening (2 min)

You join 1 min before. Camera on. They join, you say:

> "Hey [first name] — Adam. Thanks for the time. Before we dive in: I usually use these calls to figure out if I can actually help, not to pitch you. Worst case, I tell you 'I'm not the right person for this' and we both get our afternoons back. Sound okay?"

Almost everyone says yes. The framing puts them at ease — they're expecting a slide deck.

Then:

> "Quick logistics first: I block 30 min for these but they usually take 15. If we wrap early, great. If we need more, I'm flexible."

## Their context (5–7 min)

You're listening, not pitching. Ask these in order, but follow the conversation if it goes somewhere useful.

### Q1 — Status
> "First, just tell me where the business is right now. How long have you been doing [their niche]? What's your team look like?"

What you're listening for:
- **Solo operator** → likely Starter tier; budget will be tight
- **Owner + 3-10 employees** → likely Growth tier
- **Larger team (10+) or multiple crews** → Authority tier, or out-of-scope upmarket

### Q2 — Where leads come from today
> "Where do leads come from for you right now? Referrals, Google, Facebook, your sister-in-law?"

What you're listening for:
- "100% word of mouth" — they've never marketed; education conversation, not a fit-yet conversation
- "Some Google, mostly word of mouth" — Starter fit
- "Mix of Google, social, referrals, and we run some ads" — Growth/Authority fit
- "We have an agency" — find out if they're happy with them (often they're not, but check)

### Q3 — What's the problem
> "What's the thing that made you respond to my email? What's actually bugging you?"

This is the most important question. Their answer is the wedge.

Common answers:
- "We're inconsistent with leads month to month" → tier fit depends on cause
- "Google sends us crap leads" → likely GBP optimization play
- "Our website is embarrassing" → site rebuild as front door
- "We have no time to do this stuff" → Done-for-You positioning fits perfectly
- "I've been burned by agencies before" → trust-building conversation

### Q4 — What success looks like
> "If we work together for 6 months and at the end it's been a great fit, what does that look like? What changed?"

Listen for:
- Specific numbers ("3-5 more qualified leads a week") — great, easy to measure
- Vague aspirations ("more business") — need to push for specifics
- Operations problems ("I want to stop chasing reviews") — DFY angle resonates

### Q5 — Investment context
> "Have you spent money on marketing before? What ballpark? Was it worth it?"

Listen for:
- "I spent $50/month on Yelp" — likely Starter ceiling
- "I tried an agency at $2K/month and got nothing" — they're tier-aware; figure out why it didn't work
- "I don't know what marketing should cost" — need education
- "I spend $5K/month on Google Ads but the SEO side is weak" — Authority fit, well-budgeted

DON'T ask "what's your budget?" directly. It pushes them to think small. Ask what they've invested before to anchor.

## Your offer (3–4 min, only if good fit signals)

If you're hearing fit, transition:

> "Okay — I have a sense of what might work. Let me share quickly how I structure this, then you tell me if it sounds right."

Then:

> "I do retainers, not project work — because the stuff that actually moves the needle is the boring monthly stuff, not the one-shot. Three tiers depending on what you need: $1,500, $2,000, $2,500 a month. All 6-month minimum, then month-to-month."
>
> "For [their situation], what I'd recommend is [tier]. Here's why: [1-2 sentences on why that tier specifically]."
>
> "What you'd get is [3-4 specific deliverables for that tier — say them, don't list]. Monthly report on the 5th of each month showing what we did and what's working."

Stop. Let them respond.

## Their reaction (2–4 min)

Likely reactions:

### "That sounds good, what's next?"
- Confirm tier choice out loud: "So we're talking [tier] at $X/mo, starting [date if they have one]. I'll send the proposal in Documenso today; you'll get an invoice once it's signed. Cool?"
- End the call.

### "That's more than I expected."
- "Totally fair. Let's talk about what's most important — what would you say no to if it kept the price down?"
- Re-scope to the lower tier if appropriate.
- If they're below Starter: "Honestly, for what you described, you might be better off doing the GBP work yourself for a few months and revisiting next year. I can send you a one-pager on what to focus on if that helps." (Saves time, builds trust, may come back later.)

### "Let me think about it."
- "Of course. What do you need to think about — like, what's the actual question? Sometimes that's a thing I can answer right now."
- Often they have one specific worry (spouse needs to sign off, want to compare to one other agency, want a written proposal in hand). Answer it.

### "Can we start in [a few months]?"
- "Sure. What changes between now and then?" If the answer is real ("I'm closing on a building first"), trust them and set a reminder. If it's "I'll think about it", treat as a soft no.

### "I want to see references / case studies."
- Be honest. If you have prior clients, share. If you're new, say so: "I'm 6 months into this version of the business, so I don't have a long client roster yet. What I can offer is a fixed 6-month term where I'm full accountable, and a no-hidden-fee structure. If that's not enough, I get it."

## When NOT to send a proposal

- They're below the Starter price point. Don't try to do a $750/mo "lite" tier. You'll lose money and burn out.
- They want SaaS-product results from a services agency.
- They want hands-off but also want to micromanage. (Listen for "I want to be involved in every decision" — that's not Done-for-You.)
- They had a bad experience and are clearly angry. They need therapy, not an agency. Politely decline.
- They want overnight results.
- They're in a niche you don't serve.
- They're outside your geographic comfort zone (out of state, especially West Coast). You can serve them, but it's harder.

## Closing the call

If proceeding:
> "Great. I'll have the Documenso proposal in your inbox within the hour. Quick read, sign at the bottom, and we're rolling. Anything come up between now and then, just reply to the email."

If not proceeding:
> "Honest answer — I don't think I'm the right call for you right now. [Specific reason]. If [thing changes], reach out. No hard feelings."

Move HubSpot deal accordingly:
- Signed verbally → Call Held (move manually); trigger proposal workflow
- Declined → Lost (with loss reason)
- "Let me think" → leave in Call Held with task to follow up in 5 business days

## After the call (5 min, before you forget)

1. Update HubSpot:
   - Move stage to Call Held
   - Set `tier_proposed` to the tier you discussed
   - Add a note: 3 bullet points (their pain, your read, next step)
2. If proceeding: trigger the proposal n8n flow (Slack slash command or HubSpot button).
3. If not: set loss reason, optional "revisit in 90 days" task.

## Common patterns to watch for

| What they say | What it usually means |
|---|---|
| "We tried SEO before and it didn't work" | They worked with someone who didn't know local. Differentiate. |
| "I just want more leads" | They're underspecified. Push to "what's a qualified lead vs not?" |
| "Are you cheaper than [other agency]?" | Price-shopping. Sometimes a no-go, sometimes a sign of due diligence. Read tone. |
| "My nephew built our site for free" | They've got a sentimentally entangled dependency. Tread carefully. |
| "We work with my brother-in-law's marketing guy" | Probably a no. Don't try to win this on the call. |
| "What's your pricing range?" (on email before call) | Pre-call price screening. Tell them the range; if they ghost, they self-selected out. |

## Reps required

This script gets easier after 20 calls. The first 10 will feel awkward. Don't memorize the words; memorize the FLOW (their context → their problem → your offer → their reaction → close). Words can adjust.

Record your own calls (Loom or whatever, with consent) and review monthly. The most boring 30 minutes of agency work is also the most leveraged.

# Monthly Reporting Template

**Format:** PDF generated from this Markdown via Pandoc, plus a 2-min Loom walkthrough for Growth and Authority tiers.

**Delivery:** By the 5th business day of the following month. Email to primary contact; archive in client folder.

**Tone:** Confident, factual, no fluff. The numbers do the talking. You add one strategic line of insight at the end.

---

# {{Client Name}} — {{Month}} {{Year}}

**Prepared by:** The Nordic Nerd
**For:** {{Client Company}}
**Period covered:** {{Month}} 1 – {{Month}} {{last_day}}
**Tier:** {{tier_name}}

---

## TL;DR

**This month at a glance:**

- Site visitors: **{{visitors_this_month}}** ({{visitor_delta_pct}}% vs. last month)
- GBP profile views: **{{gbp_views_this_month}}** ({{gbp_views_delta_pct}}%)
- GBP calls received: **{{gbp_calls_this_month}}** ({{gbp_calls_delta_pct}}%)
- New reviews: **+{{new_reviews_count}}** (avg rating now {{avg_rating}})
- Content shipped: **{{pages_shipped}} pages, {{posts_published}} posts**
- Form submissions / contact requests: **{{form_submissions}}**

**One thing to know this month:** {{key_insight_oneliner}}

---

## 1. Google Business Profile performance

### What we shipped on the GBP this month

## 2. Website performance

### Visitor metrics

| Metric | This month | Last month | Δ |
|---|---|---|---|
| Unique visitors | {{this.visitors}} | {{last.visitors}} | {{delta.visitors_pct}}% |
| Sessions | {{this.sessions}} | {{last.sessions}} | {{delta.sessions_pct}}% |

| GBP click-through to site | {{this.gbp_clicks}} | {{delta.gbp_clicks_pct}}% |

**Note on conversion tracking:** These are tracked events on the site. They reflect intent signals, not closed jobs. Where possible, the report includes phone tap data so you can match it against actual call volume on your end.

---

## 3. Content shipped this month

### Pages launched (Growth/Authority tiers)

{{pages_shipped_block}}

(Format per page: title, URL, target keyword, word count, brief description of angle.)

### Blog posts published (Growth/Authority tiers)

{{posts_published_block}}

(Format per post: title, URL, topic, word count.)

### Other deliverables

## 4. Reviews

### Notable reviews

**Anything that needs your attention:**

{{review_attention_block}}

(If a 1-3 star review came in, flag it with the language: "Saw a [3-star] review from [name] mentioning [issue]. Worth a personal response from you — I drafted one for your review at [link].")

## 5. Next month's plan

Here's what I'm shipping in {{next_month}}:

## 6. One recommendation

{{strategic_recommendation_block}}

(This is the most important section. One specific, actionable observation — something the client could do, or something we'll do together, that meaningfully changes the trajectory. Examples below.)

### Examples of good recommendations

- **GBP Q&A leverage:** "You've got 47 reviews now, but only 3 Q&A entries. The top 3 GBP listings for 'roofer in Lakeland' have 25+ Q&As each. I'd like to draft 15 Q&As this month based on the actual questions you get on calls — let me know if I can sit on a call with you for 20 min to scrape patterns."

- **Service-specific page expansion:** "Your 'kitchen remodel' page is doing 38% of all site traffic. I want to spin off two child pages — 'Lakeland kitchen remodel cost' and 'small kitchen remodel ideas Lakeland' — to capture long-tail searches we're not ranking for. Approve and I'll have both drafted by mid-month."

- **Photo gap on map pack:** "When I search 'pool builder Champions Gate', you show up in spot 5. The four ahead of you all have 50+ recent photos. You have 22. I'd like to add 25-30 new photos this month — can you send me batches from your last 5 jobs as they come in? Phone photos are fine."

(One recommendation, not five. Five reads as a wishlist. One reads as a partner with a point of view.)

---

## Appendix — Methodology

- **GBP data** pulled directly from Google Business Profile Insights
- **Site analytics** from Plausible / Fathom
- **Conversion events** based on JavaScript event tracking embedded in your site
- **Comparison period** is the previous calendar month
- **All data is current as of:** {{report_generation_date}}

---

— Adam | The Nordic Nerd
adam@thenordicnerd.com | thenordicnerd.com
{{physical_address}}

---

## Internal notes (NOT included in client deliverable)

These notes go in the client folder, not in the PDF:

- Hours worked this month on this client: {{hours_logged}}
- Effective hourly: ${{mrr_value}} / {{hours_logged}} = ${{effective_hourly}}
- Any concerns or risks for retention?
- Did anything ship late? Why?
- Renewal date approaching?
- Should we propose a tier upgrade?

If any of these flags fire, schedule a Pipeline Agent retro item.

files.title: Client Onboarding Checklist (Day 0 → Day 30)
files.content:
# Client Onboarding Checklist (Day 0 → Day 30)

**Owner:** Client Delivery Agent (you, Adam, until you have help).
**Trigger:** Documenso signing webhook fires → onboarding flow begins automatically.
**End state by Day 30:** Site v1 live, GBP fully optimized, monthly cadence rolling.

---

## Day 0 (the day they sign)

**Auto (n8n workflow `05-signed-to-onboarding`):**
- [ ] Stripe customer + subscription created
- [ ] First invoice sent (7 days to pay)
- [ ] MailerLite onboarding sequence triggered
- [ ] HubSpot deal moved to Won
- [ ] Slack #wins ping fired

**Manual (you, within 1 hour of signing):**
- [ ] Send the personal welcome email (template below). Brief, warm, no business yet.
- [ ] Create a dedicated Slack channel or email folder for this client. Naming: `#nn-{{company-slug}}`
- [ ] Create their client folder in Notion / Google Drive: `/Clients/{{Company Name}}/`
- [ ] Add their info to the client roster spreadsheet (in Notion)
- [ ] Schedule the day-7 kickoff call via Cal.com (send invite manually — don't make them book)

### Day 0 welcome email (auto-sent by MailerLite, edit the template)

```
Subject: welcome to the nordic nerd, [first name]

[first name] — alright, we're official. Thanks for trusting me with this.

Here's what happens next:

→ In the next 24 hours, I'll send a short intake form (12 questions, takes ~30 min).
→ We're on the calendar for a kickoff call next [day]. Calendar invite separately.
→ The first invoice just landed in your inbox — it's due in 7 days. No surprises after that; it's same amount, same day, every month, automatic.

If you need me before next week, this email works or text me at [phone]. I'm in Winter Haven; usually responsive same-day.

talk soon,
— Adam

The Nordic Nerd | thenordicnerd.com
{{physical_address}}
```

---

## Days 1–3

- [ ] Send discovery questionnaire (link to Notion or Google Form — see Questionnaire section below)
- [ ] Send GBP access request: "Please add adam@thenordicnerd.com as a Manager on your Google Business Profile. Steps: [link to 30-sec Loom you've pre-recorded]."
- [ ] Send Stripe invoice reminder if not paid by day 3
- [ ] Confirm receipt of welcome email (if no reply by day 2, send a quick "just checking you got my note" — usually it landed in promotions)
- [ ] Add their domain to your monitoring tools (Plausible, UptimeRobot if applicable)

---

## Days 4–7

- [ ] Run GBP audit using the 10-point checklist (see `02-Agents/client-delivery-agent.md`)
- [ ] Run site audit (Lighthouse + technical SEO scan via Screaming Frog free or Ahrefs)
- [ ] If they've returned the discovery questionnaire: digest answers, surface 2-3 things to push back on
- [ ] If they haven't: gentle nudge ("hey, when you get a sec — I can't really build right without your answers")
- [ ] **Kickoff call** (Day 7 default, 45 min)
  - Review audits together (share screen, walk through GBP issues, walk through site issues)
  - Confirm priorities for v1 site (which 5 pages, what photos, what voice)
  - Set monthly cadence expectations: report on the 5th, check-in calls if applicable
  - End with: "You'll see a lot of activity in the next 2 weeks — site dev preview link by [date], GBP changes flowing in [date]."

---

## Days 8–14

- [ ] Begin Nuxt site scaffold from `nordic-nuxt-contractor` template
- [ ] Set up client's Cloudflare DNS access (or they grant you access; whichever is cleaner)
- [ ] Send dev preview link by Day 12 ("here's where we are, this is rough, but you can see the shape")
- [ ] **GBP optimization push** — complete the 10-point checklist
  - Categories: primary + 3-5 secondary
  - Services: all listed with descriptions
  - Hours: accurate
  - Photos: bring count to 25+
  - Q&A: 5 proactive entries
  - First GBP post published
  - Service area set to specific cities (not radius)
  - Attributes set
  - Description rewritten (600-700 chars)
- [ ] Update client at end of week ("Here's what's happening" status email — 4 bullets max)

---

## Days 15–21

- [ ] **Site v1 launch by Day 21** (Starter tier)
- [ ] Pre-launch QA:
  - Lighthouse Perf ≥90, A11y ≥95, SEO 100 on every page
  - All forms tested (post to their email + HubSpot)
  - All phone numbers click-to-call on mobile
  - JSON-LD `LocalBusiness` validates on schema.org validator
  - Mobile responsiveness verified on real device
  - Analytics installed (Plausible or Fathom)
  - All links work (no 404s)
  - GBP NAP matches site NAP exactly (Name/Address/Phone consistency)
- [ ] Deploy to production. DNS cutover (if applicable) at low-traffic time (Wed 11am ET).
- [ ] Submit sitemap to Google Search Console
- [ ] Send launch announcement email to client with Loom walkthrough

### Launch announcement email template

```
Subject: site v1 is live, [first name]

[first name] — your new site is live. Here it is: [URL]

A 3-minute walkthrough of what I did and why: [Loom link]

Quick notes:
→ Lighthouse scores are 96 / 98 / 100 / 100 across all pages — better than 99% of contractor sites in Polk.
→ Your GBP and the site now match exactly on name, address, phone (Google needed that — most local sites don't have it right).
→ Plausible analytics is on. I'll send the first metrics in your monthly report on the 5th.

If anything looks off, just reply. One round of revisions included this month.

— Adam
```

---

## Days 22–30

- [ ] Day-22 check-in: did they read the launch email? Got any feedback?
- [ ] Begin Growth/Authority tier monthly deliverables (if applicable):
  - First local SEO page drafted (Growth) or first 2 pages (Authority)
  - First blog post drafted
  - Review request automation configured if applicable
- [ ] **Day-30 check-in call** (Growth and Authority tiers — 30 min):
  - Walk through what shipped in the first month
  - Preview month 2 deliverables
  - Surface any concerns proactively
  - Ask: "Anything you want me to adjust before we settle into the rhythm?"
- [ ] Day 28-30: draft month 1 report (delivered by Day 35 / 5th business day of next month)

---

## Discovery questionnaire (the 12 questions)

Send via Notion form, Google Form, or Typeform. Keep it under 30 minutes to fill.

1. **Quick context** — How long have you been in business? How many people on the team?
2. **What you do** — What 3-5 services bring in the most revenue? Which one would you do more of if you could?
3. **Who you serve** — Describe your ideal customer in two sentences. Geographic range?
4. **Brand voice** — If your brand were a person, who would they be? (Examples: "your neighbor who's good with his hands", "a fourth-generation craftsman", "the team that gets it done.")
5. **Differentiators** — Why do people pick you over other [niche] in [city]?
6. **Where the leads come from** — Best guess split: word of mouth / Google / Facebook / paid ads / referrals from other businesses / other?
7. **Tools you use** — CRM? Job management software? How do you track jobs from inquiry to completion?
8. **Photos** — Can you give me access to your photo library (Dropbox/Drive)? Or should we plan a shoot day? Approx how many usable project photos do you have?
9. **Competitors** — Name 2-3 [niche] companies in your area that you respect (don't have to be ones you compete with).
10. **Reviews** — How many Google reviews do you have? Do you actively ask for them today?
11. **What's not working** — What's the one marketing/website thing that bugs you most right now?
12. **What success looks like** — In 6 months, what would tell you this was worth the investment?

---

## Brand asset intake

In addition to the questionnaire, request:
- [ ] Logo (vector preferred — SVG/AI/EPS; PNG with transparent background as fallback)
- [ ] Brand colors (hex codes if known)
- [ ] Brand fonts (or it's fine if they don't have one — you'll pick)
- [ ] Photo library access (Dropbox, Drive, or shared folder)
- [ ] List of services with descriptions (or copy from existing site if applicable)
- [ ] Team photo + team bios (optional but encouraged)
- [ ] Testimonial blurbs (if any not on existing site)
- [ ] Existing site login (if rebuilding, not just adding to)
- [ ] Domain registrar login (or instructions for DNS access)

---

## Critical handoffs

These are the points where balls get dropped most often:

1. **Discovery questionnaire → kickoff call** — If they haven't returned the questionnaire 24h before the call, send a "no pressure, but if you have 20 min before our call tomorrow…" note.
2. **GBP access** — Some contractors don't know they own their GBP (or they don't). Walk them through claiming it if needed.
3. **Domain access** — If they have a current site, the previous developer may be holding hostage. Document who owns the domain on day 1.
4. **Photo gathering** — This is the most common blocker. If they're slow on photos, ship the site with placeholder + clear "we're adding more photos this week" and update as they trickle in. Don't let perfect block live.
5. **Day-30 call** — Set the calendar invite during the kickoff call on day 7. Don't wait until day 28 to schedule.

---

## Failure modes to watch

- **Silent client** — They sign, pay, then go dark. Three nudges over 14 days; after that, ship what you can with available info and put it in front of them.
- **Scope creep early** — "Oh, can you also do [thing not in tier]?" Be friendly but firm: "Not in the scope for this tier — but I can quote it separately if you want." First time gets a soft no; second time gets a written scope reminder.
- **Late payment** — Tier 1 escalation in n8n. Personal call from you within 24 hours of failure.
- **Photo / asset paralysis** — Some clients freeze on "what photos should I send?" Help them: "Send me the last 20 jobs in your job-management software. I'll pick."

---

## Onboarding done

A client is fully onboarded when:
- [ ] Site v1 is live and stable
- [ ] GBP optimization is complete (all 10 checklist items)
- [ ] First invoice paid
- [ ] First monthly report sent (typically Day 35)
- [ ] Day-30 check-in call held (Growth/Authority)
- [ ] Recurring monthly deliverables for their tier have begun

Mark in HubSpot custom property: `onboarding_complete_date`. Used in retention analytics later.

files.title: Proposal Template (Documenso)
files.content:
# Proposal Template (Documenso)

**Use:** This template feeds into Documenso. The Documenso "template" object holds the same body as below, with `{{merge_fields}}` mapped to Documenso custom fields. The retainer agreement (Exhibit A) is included as an attached page in the same Documenso doc.

**Three Documenso templates exist — one per tier — because deliverables differ. The cover letter is identical; the Statement of Work pages swap.**

---

# {{client_company}} — Nordic Nerd Retainer Proposal

**Prepared by:** Adam | The Nordic Nerd
**Prepared for:** {{client_signer_name}}, {{client_company}}
**Date:** {{proposal_date}}
**Tier proposed:** {{tier_name}} — ${{mrr_value}}/month
**Term:** 6 months

---

## What we talked about

You're spending more time chasing leads than you'd like. The GBP listing has the bones of something good but isn't doing the heavy lifting. Your site is fine but it's older than you'd build it today, and most months you don't think about it at all. Meanwhile, two of your competitors in {{client_city}} are showing up on page one for the searches you care about — and they aren't doing anything you can't do.

Here's what I'd do over the next six months to fix it.

---

## What I'll do for you

{{tier_deliverables_block}}

The full deliverable matrix and what's NOT included is in the Statement of Work (next page).

---

## How this works

**Month 0 — Onboarding (Days 0–30)**
- Day 0: this proposal is signed. First invoice goes out same day. 7 days to pay.
- Day 1–7: I get access to your GBP (manager role), gather brand assets, and audit everything.
- Day 7–21: site rebuild on Nuxt 3 with the page set we discussed. GBP gets a full overhaul.
- Day 21–30: site goes live. You get a walkthrough. GBP optimization complete. Monthly cadence begins.

**Months 1–5 — Cadence**
- Deliverables per the tier rollout. Monthly report by the 5th business day of the next month.
- {{tier_check_in_cadence}}.
- You get a Slack channel (or email thread, your choice) with me directly.

**Month 6 — Renewal decision**
- 30 days before the end of month 6, we sit down and look at the results.
- If they're what you want, we keep going month-to-month.
- If they aren't, we figure out why or part friendly.

---

## Pricing

| | |
|---|---|
| Monthly retainer | **${{mrr_value}}** |
| Initial term | 6 months |
| First invoice | Sent today, due in 7 days |
| Setup fees | None |
| Cancellation | After 6-month minimum, 30 days written notice |

That's it. No setup fee, no surprise charges. Paid ads (if you ever want them) are a separate conversation — ad spend goes through your card, not mine.

---

## Why me, briefly

# Retainer Agreement v1 — DRAFT

> **⚠️ DO NOT USE WITHOUT FLORIDA ATTORNEY REVIEW.**
>
> This is a working draft. It reflects general best practices for service-business retainers in Florida but has NOT been reviewed by a licensed attorney. Before sending this to a client, have a FL attorney review and tailor for:
> - FL Statute 501.165 (automatic renewal disclosures — FL requires specific cancellation language)

> - Mutual NDA terms
> - Governing law and venue
>

---

# DONE-FOR-YOU LOCAL PRESENCE RETAINER AGREEMENT

This Done-For-You Local Presence Retainer Agreement ("**Agreement**") is entered into as of **{{start_date}}** ("**Effective Date**") by and between:

**The Nordic Nerd, LLC**, a Florida limited liability company with its principal office at {{nordic_address}} ("**Nordic Nerd**" or "**Provider**"); and

**{{client_company}}**, a {{client_entity_type}} with its principal place of business at {{client_address}} ("**Client**").

Provider and Client are each a "**Party**" and collectively the "**Parties**."

---

## 1. SERVICES

### 1.1 Scope
Provider will provide marketing, web, and local-presence services to Client as described in the **{{tier_name}}** tier of Provider's Done-For-You Local Presence offering (the "**Services**"). The Services for the {{tier_name}} tier include:

{{tier_deliverables_block}}

Specific Services are further detailed in **Exhibit A — Statement of Work** attached hereto.

### 1.2 Service hours
Provider operates Monday through Friday, 9:00 AM – 6:00 PM Eastern Time, excluding US federal holidays. Communications outside these hours may be acknowledged but are not guaranteed to receive substantive response until the next business day.

### 1.3 Response SLA
Provider will respond to Client communications within:
- **48 business hours** for Starter tier clients
- **24 business hours** for Growth tier clients
- **24 business hours** for Authority tier clients (priority queue)

"Respond" means initial acknowledgment with a substantive answer or a timeline for one. It does not mean completion of requested work.

### 1.4 Revisions
Each monthly deliverable includes the number of revision rounds specified in the tier:
- **Starter**: 1 round per month
- **Growth**: 2 rounds per month
- **Authority**: Reasonable unlimited rounds per month (Provider may flag scope-creep concerns)

A "round" means a single batch of feedback delivered together. Splitting feedback into multiple sequential messages may consume multiple rounds.

---

## 2. TERM AND TERMINATION

### 2.1 Initial term
This Agreement has an initial term of **six (6) months** beginning on the Effective Date (the "**Initial Term**"). The Initial Term is firm; Client may not terminate the Agreement during the Initial Term except for material breach by Provider that remains uncured after written notice and a thirty (30) day cure period.

### 2.2 Renewal
Upon expiration of the Initial Term, this Agreement will automatically renew for successive **one (1) month** terms (each, a "**Renewal Term**") unless either Party gives written notice of non-renewal at least **thirty (30) days** before the end of the then-current term.

**[FL ATTORNEY: VERIFY THIS LANGUAGE SATISFIES FL STAT. 501.165 AUTOMATIC RENEWAL DISCLOSURE REQUIREMENTS, INCLUDING CALLOUT FORMATTING.]**

### 2.3 Termination for breach
Either Party may terminate this Agreement for material breach if the breaching Party fails to cure within thirty (30) days of receiving written notice describing the breach.

### 2.4 Effect of termination
Upon termination:
- Provider will deliver all work-in-progress in its current state to Client.
- Client will pay all fees accrued through the end of the then-current billing month.
- The IP assignment in Section 5 survives termination as to all work delivered.
- Confidentiality obligations in Section 4 survive termination indefinitely.
- No refunds are issued for the current billing month; service continues through the end of the billing month for which Client has paid.

---

## 3. FEES AND PAYMENT

### 3.1 Retainer fee
Client will pay Provider a monthly retainer of **${{mrr_value}} USD** (the "**Retainer Fee**") for the duration of this Agreement.

### 3.2 Billing
- The first month's Retainer Fee is invoiced upon execution of this Agreement and is due within seven (7) days.
- Subsequent months are charged automatically on the same day of each month to the payment method Client has on file with Provider's payment processor (currently Stripe).
- Invoices are issued via Stripe.

### 3.3 Late payment
Payments not received within ten (10) days of due date may, at Provider's discretion:
- Accrue interest at 1.5% per month (or the maximum allowed by FL law, whichever is lower);
- Pause Services until payment is received; and
- Constitute a material breach if more than thirty (30) days overdue.

### 3.4 No refunds
Retainer Fees are non-refundable. Services continue through the end of any billing month for which payment has been received. Pro-rated refunds will not be issued except where required by law.

### 3.5 Tax
The Retainer Fee is exclusive of any applicable taxes. Each Party is responsible for its own income taxes; Client is responsible for any sales or use tax due on the Services if applicable.

---

## 4. CONFIDENTIALITY

### 4.1 Mutual obligation
Each Party (the "**Receiving Party**") agrees to hold confidential all non-public business, technical, or financial information disclosed by the other Party (the "**Disclosing Party**") that is marked confidential or that a reasonable person would understand to be confidential ("**Confidential Information**"). The Receiving Party will:
- Use Confidential Information only to perform under this Agreement;
- Not disclose Confidential Information to third parties without the Disclosing Party's prior written consent (except as required by law); and
- Protect Confidential Information using at least the same degree of care it uses for its own confidential information of similar sensitivity, and no less than a reasonable degree of care.

### 4.2 Exclusions
Confidential Information does not include information that:
- Was already known to the Receiving Party without confidentiality obligation;
- Becomes publicly available through no fault of the Receiving Party;
- Is independently developed without reference to the Confidential Information;
- Is rightfully obtained from a third party without confidentiality obligation.

### 4.3 Survival
The obligations in this Section 4 survive termination of this Agreement indefinitely for trade secrets and for five (5) years for other Confidential Information.

---

## 5. INTELLECTUAL PROPERTY

### 5.1 Client materials
Client retains all rights in materials Client provides to Provider, including but not limited to Client's name, logos, trademarks, photographs, copy, and customer data (the "**Client Materials**").

### 5.2 Deliverables
Subject to Client's payment of all Retainer Fees due, Provider hereby assigns to Client all right, title, and interest in the work product specifically created for Client and delivered under this Agreement (the "**Deliverables**"), including:
- Custom website code created specifically for Client
- Written content created specifically for Client (blog posts, landing pages, GBP descriptions)

### 5.3 Provider tools and templates
Provider retains all rights in:
- General-purpose templates, frameworks, code libraries, and tools developed by Provider prior to or outside of this Agreement;
- Methodologies, processes, and know-how;
- Any work product that is not specifically created for Client.

Provider grants Client a perpetual, royalty-free, non-exclusive license to use such Provider tools as embedded in the Deliverables for Client's ordinary business purposes.

### 5.4 Reservation of rights
Nothing in this Agreement grants either Party rights in the other Party's trademarks, service marks, or trade names except as expressly stated.

---

## 6. WARRANTIES AND DISCLAIMERS

### 6.1 Mutual warranties
Each Party represents that it has full power to enter this Agreement and that doing so does not violate any other agreement.

### 6.2 Service performance
Provider will perform the Services in a professional manner consistent with industry standards. Provider does not warrant any specific business outcome, including but not limited to: search engine rankings, lead volume, revenue, or conversion rates. Marketing results depend on many factors outside Provider's control.

### 6.3 Third-party platforms
Provider's Services involve third-party platforms (Google, Stripe, MailerLite, hosting providers, etc.). Provider is not responsible for changes in those platforms' policies, pricing, availability, or algorithms.

### 6.4 Disclaimer
EXCEPT AS EXPRESSLY SET FORTH IN THIS AGREEMENT, ALL SERVICES AND DELIVERABLES ARE PROVIDED "AS IS" WITHOUT WARRANTY OF ANY KIND, EXPRESS OR IMPLIED, INCLUDING WITHOUT LIMITATION ANY IMPLIED WARRANTIES OF MERCHANTABILITY, FITNESS FOR A PARTICULAR PURPOSE, OR NON-INFRINGEMENT.

---

## 7. LIMITATION OF LIABILITY

### 7.1 Cap
EXCEPT FOR (A) BREACHES OF CONFIDENTIALITY, (B) INDEMNIFICATION OBLIGATIONS, OR (C) GROSS NEGLIGENCE OR WILLFUL MISCONDUCT, EACH PARTY'S TOTAL CUMULATIVE LIABILITY UNDER THIS AGREEMENT WILL NOT EXCEED THE TOTAL RETAINER FEES PAID BY CLIENT TO PROVIDER IN THE THREE (3) MONTHS PRECEDING THE EVENT GIVING RISE TO THE CLAIM.

### 7.2 Consequential damages
NEITHER PARTY WILL BE LIABLE FOR INDIRECT, INCIDENTAL, CONSEQUENTIAL, SPECIAL, EXEMPLARY, OR PUNITIVE DAMAGES, OR FOR LOST PROFITS, LOST REVENUE, OR LOST BUSINESS OPPORTUNITIES, ARISING OUT OF OR RELATED TO THIS AGREEMENT, EVEN IF ADVISED OF THE POSSIBILITY OF SUCH DAMAGES.

**[FL ATTORNEY: VERIFY THESE CAPS ARE APPROPRIATE FOR PROVIDER'S E&O INSURANCE COVERAGE — IF NO INSURANCE, MAY NEED ADJUSTMENT.]**

---

## 8. INDEMNIFICATION

### 8.1 By Client
Client will indemnify, defend, and hold harmless Provider from any third-party claims arising out of:
- Client Materials infringing or misappropriating any third party's intellectual property;
- Client's breach of applicable laws or regulations;

Provider will indemnify, defend, and hold harmless Client from any third-party claims arising out of Provider's gross negligence or willful misconduct.

### 8.3 Procedure
The indemnified Party will: (a) promptly notify the indemnifying Party of the claim; (b) give the indemnifying Party sole control of the defense; and (c) provide reasonable cooperation.

---

## 9. GENERAL PROVISIONS

### 9.1 Independent contractor
Provider is an independent contractor and not an employee, partner, or agent of Client. Neither Party has authority to bind the other.

### 9.2 Assignment
Neither Party may assign this Agreement without the other Party's prior written consent, except that either Party may assign to an acquirer of all or substantially all of its assets.

### 9.6 Force majeure
Neither Party is liable for delay or failure due to causes beyond its reasonable control (acts of God, natural disasters, government action, internet/utility outages, pandemics, etc.).

files.title: Episode 1 — The Stack
files.content:
# Episode 1 — The Stack

**Target length:** 22-26 min
**Filming:** End of Month 1 (after foundation is in place but before significant client work)
**Goal:** Establish the premise, show the tools, set expectations for the series. Nothing sold, no clients yet (or maybe one in pipeline).

---

## Cold open (0:00–0:45)

Camera on. Sitting at desk. One sentence:

> "I'm a Staff Frontend Engineer with a full-time job at a public company, and over the next 12 months I'm going to try to replace that income by running a tiny marketing agency for contractors in Polk County, Florida. This is month one."

Cut to:
- Quick montage shot: HubSpot dashboard (empty), n8n workflow editor (some nodes), Stripe (no transactions yet)
- Back to face: "I'm going to show you every tool, every workflow, and every dollar — including the ones I screw up."

Title card animation. Norse imagery (subtle — like a small rune watermark in the corner). Hold for 3 seconds.

## Scoreboard (0:45–1:45)

Static graphic. Voiceover.

```
MONTH 1 — APRIL 2026
Revenue this month:     $0
MRR end of month:       $0
Active clients:         0
Hours worked:           ~45 (mostly setup)
Effective hourly:       $0 :)

Last month I said I'd:  N/A — this is the first episode

Goals for month 2:
  → Send 200 cold emails
  → Get 5+ replies
  → Book 1+ discovery call
```

Voiceover during the graphic: "Zero dollars in month one. As expected — month one is setup. We'll see if month two breaks the zero."

## Section 1 — The premise, fast (1:45–4:00)

Why this exists. Talking head. No B-roll.

Cover:
- I have a frontend engineering W2. It's good. I'm not quitting yet.
- I want to know if I can build something that's mine — without quitting first.
- 12-month time-boxed experiment.
- Done-for-You local presence for Polk County contractors. Why: I live here, the market is underserved, I have the technical skills to deliver.
- Public scoreboard. Every month. Including the bad months.

Avoid the "should you start an agency?" debate. Don't sell anything. Get to the tools.

## Section 2 — The stack walkthrough (4:00–14:00)

This is the meat. Screen-record-heavy.

Walk through each tool. Why each tool, not just what.

**The three layers:**

### Outbound layer (4:00–7:00)
- Apify for scraping Google Maps (show actor configuration)
- Hunter.io for email finding
- MillionVerifier for verification (don't skip this — bad lists kill domains)
- Smartlead as the sequencer (show inbox config + warmup)
- Two cold domains: nordicnerdhq.com + getnordicnerd.com
- Three inboxes on those domains
- Brief: "Why not just Apollo or Lemlist? Because I want to own the data and the workflow. Apollo is fine if you don't care; I care."

### Pipeline + delivery (7:00–10:00)
- HubSpot Free for CRM (show the 6-stage pipeline)
- Cal.com for booking
- Documenso for e-sign (self-hosted, same VPS as n8n)
- Stripe for payments
- Nuxt 3 for client site builds (briefly show a starter template)

### Orchestration + AI (10:00–14:00)
**This is the section the technical audience cares about most.**

- n8n self-hosted on a $5/mo Hetzner box
- Show ONE workflow in detail — pick `02-push-to-sequencer.json` because it touches AI
- Walk through:
  - Trigger
  - Apify scrape parsing
  - Hunter enrichment
  - MillionVerifier filter
  - Claude API call for first-line generation (show the prompt!)
  - Smartlead push
  - HubSpot create contact + deal
- Total cost of the stack: ~$220/mo (show the breakdown)

## Section 3 — Why this approach (14:00–17:00)

Talking head, with B-roll cuts.

**The three principles:**

1. **Own the data.** I want every scraped lead, every reply, every metric in my own database. Tools come and go; data should persist.

2. **AI in the boring places, humans in the high-stakes places.** Claude does first-line generation, reply classification, draft writing. I do every discovery call, every proposal scope, every client conversation. The AI is hands. I'm the head.

3. **Local + niche + technical.** I'm not trying to be a global agency. Polk County contractors. A technical builder talking to skilled tradespeople about their digital presence. The narrowness is the moat.

(Optional aside: 30-second tangent on why "generic AI agency" is the wrong play. Compete on specificity, not generality.)

## Section 4 — Constraints + bright lines (17:00–20:00)

The honest stuff.

- W2 employer. Bright lines. Different laptops, different accounts, evenings + weekends only.
- LLC filed in Florida (show the SunBiz filing receipt for legitimacy)
- Not legal advice. Attorney consult booked. (Be transparent about doing this properly.)
- Time commitment: 15-20 hr/week target. Sustainable, not crunch-mode.
- Income goal: replace W2 within 12 months. Public number? Maybe in a later episode. For now: "enough that this stops being a side thing."

## Section 5 — What's next (20:00–22:00)

Preview Month 2.

- 200 cold emails go out
- First sequence A/B test
- First reply triage workflow
- Hopefully one discovery call

End with the close that becomes the series catchphrase:

> "If you're a contractor in Polk County and you saw the cold email and that's how you got here — funny story. The rest of you, see you next month."

Outro graphic. Subscribe nudge. End card with links to ep 2 (when it exists).

---

## B-roll shot list

For when face-cam editing needs a break:

- [ ] HubSpot empty pipeline view (zoomed)
- [ ] n8n workflow editor with all 5 workflows visible (overview)
- [ ] One workflow zoomed (push-to-sequencer)
- [ ] Cloudflare DNS panel showing the 3 domains
- [ ] Stripe dashboard (still $0 — show it)
- [ ] Anthropic console showing recent API calls
- [ ] Winter Haven, FL on a map (5-sec establishing shot)
- [ ] Polk County contractor's GBP listing (anonymized) showing what a typical scraped record looks like

## Thumbnail concept

Black background. Centered: `$0` in large bold text. Below: "MONTH 1". Bottom right: small face shot. One subtle Norse rune in corner.

The $0 thumbnail says everything about the show's honesty.

## Title options
- "Month 1: I Spent $220 to Earn $0 (Agency From Scratch)"
- "Month 1: Setting Up an AI-Powered Agency From Zero" — too generic, skip
- "Month 1: The Stack — Agency From Scratch"  ← recommended, matches series naming

## Description draft

```
Month 1 of a 12-month experiment: can a Staff Frontend Engineer build a side-business marketing agency for local contractors and replace his W2 income in a year?

In this episode: the complete tool stack (~$220/mo), why I chose each piece, and a deep walkthrough of one of my n8n workflows that turns a scraped Google Maps result into a personalized cold email via the Claude API.

00:00  Cold open
00:45  Month 1 scoreboard ($0, as expected)
01:45  Why I'm doing this
04:00  The full tool stack
04:00  Outbound layer (Apify, Hunter, MillionVerifier, Smartlead)
07:00  Pipeline & delivery (HubSpot, Cal.com, Documenso, Stripe, Nuxt)
10:00  Orchestration & AI (n8n + Anthropic API — workflow walkthrough)
14:00  Why this approach
17:00  Working around a W2 employer (bright lines, attorney)
20:00  What's coming in month 2

The Nordic Nerd: https://thenordicnerd.com
n8n: https://n8n.io
Anthropic API: https://console.anthropic.com

I publish the full agency scoreboard the first week of every month — revenue, MRR, hours, what worked, what didn't. No paid sponsorships, no course pitches. Just the work.

If you're a contractor in Polk County, FL and want to see how I'd help your business specifically — calendar.app.google/JheMSKoDMrWkiixy6.

#agencylife #frontenddev #automation #buildinginpublic
```

## What can go wrong (failure modes)

- **Recording too long:** if the raw recording is over 45 min, you'll over-cut. Stay under 35 min raw.
- **Showing too much code:** technical audience can handle it, but balance with the "why" — every code-show segment needs a one-sentence "this is doing X because Y."
- **Being too positive:** month 1 is $0. Don't pretend it's exciting. Pretend it's a slog. (Because it is.)
- **Naming the employer:** triple-check this before publishing. No identifying details about the W2.

# Episode 2 — Multi-Agent Outbound

**Target length:** 26-30 min (slightly longer because workflow deep-dive)
**Filming:** End of Month 2 (~200 cold emails sent, early reply data)
**Goal:** Show the AI-driven outbound system in detail. Walk through the actual prompts and workflows. Real reply data.

---

## Cold open (0:00–0:45)

Sitting at desk. Tab visible: Smartlead campaign with reply count.

> "Month 2. 217 cold emails sent. 14 replies. One of them said yes."

Cut to scoreboard graphic.

## Scoreboard (0:45–1:45)

```
MONTH 2 — MAY 2026
Revenue this month:     $0
MRR end of month:       $0
Active clients:         0
Cold emails sent:       217
Replies received:       14 (6.4%)
Positive replies:       3 (1.4%)
Discovery calls booked: 1
Hours worked:           ~50
Effective hourly:       still $0

Last month I said I'd:
  ✅ Send 200 cold emails  (217 actual)
  ✅ Get 5+ replies        (14 actual)
  ✅ Book 1+ discovery call (1 actual, scheduled for next week)
```

Voiceover: "Three for three on month-1 goals. But goals are easy to set when you're at zero. The real test is whether month 3 looks like growth or a fluke."

## Section 1 — The five agents (1:45–5:00)

Whiteboard or animated diagram. Each agent is a `rules.md` file.

Walk through:
1. Outbound Ops Agent — owns top of funnel
2. Copywriter Agent — owns all words
3. Pipeline Agent — owns reply-to-paid
4. Client Delivery Agent — owns the work after signing
5. Operations Agent — orchestrates everything

**Key framing:** "These aren't separate AI tools. They're five hats I wear, each with a different system prompt and a different scope. The point is to never confuse 'what are we doing this week' (Ops) with 'how do we write a better subject line' (Copywriter)."

## Section 2 — Workflow deep-dive: push-to-sequencer (5:00–13:00)

The longest section. Screen-record-heavy. Show actual n8n editor.

Walk through `02-push-to-sequencer.json` node by node:

1. **Webhook trigger** — manual fire after Monday review
2. **Read Google Sheets** — leads sheet, only unpushed rows
3. **Split in batches** — one lead at a time (rate limit Anthropic API)
4. **Build business profile** — assemble the input JSON for first-line generation
5. **HTTP to Anthropic** — show the system prompt on screen
6. **Parse first-line** — handle FALLBACK case (skip leads with weak data)
7. **Smartlead Add Lead** — show how merge fields flow into the campaign
8. **HubSpot Create Contact** — single source of truth for prospect data
9. **HubSpot Create Deal** — kicks off the pipeline tracking
10. **Mark sheet row pushed**

**Show one full execution** running live — pick a real lead with good GBP data so the AI generates a real first line. Read the output line aloud. Compare to a generic template.

This is the moment the technical audience leans forward.

## Section 3 — The first-line system prompt (13:00–17:00)

Pull up `first-line-system-prompt.md` in a clean reader view.

Walk through:
- The goal (one sentence, real specifics)
- The voice rules (peer-to-peer, lowercase, dry)
- The hard rules (no superlatives, no exclamation marks, no invention)
- The FALLBACK escape valve
- Examples of good vs bad output

**Show 3 real generated first lines from month 2 sends.** Read each one. Comment on what makes it work (or not).

Then: show ONE bad first line the AI produced early in the month. Explain why. Show the prompt change that fixed it.

The "I changed the prompt because of a bad output" beat is more useful than 100 example outputs.

## Section 4 — Reply triage (17:00–22:00)

Same workflow approach, faster pace. Show `03-reply-triage.json`.

Walk through:
- Smartlead webhook → normalize payload
- Claude classifier → 6 categories (positive, question, negative, unsubscribe, OOO, other)
- Switch → route based on classification
- For positive/question: draft a reply with a SECOND Claude call
- Draft lands in Slack with one-click send button
- Escalations: bypass drafting, ping a high-priority channel

**Show a real classified reply.** Pick one of the positive ones from month 2. Show:
- The reply itself
- The classifier's JSON output
- The drafted response Claude wrote
- The actual response you sent (if you edited it)

If the AI's draft was sent unchanged, say so. If you edited it, show what you changed and why.

## Section 5 — What the data showed in Month 2 (22:00–26:00)

Real numbers, no editorializing.

Top subject lines by reply rate (only show 3-5):
- "quick question about your lakeland site" — 9.2% reply
- "your GBP scored 4/10" — 5.8% reply
- (etc.)

Best-performing niches in month 2:
- Kitchen/bath remodelers — 8.1% reply rate
- HVAC — 7.3%
- Roofing — 4.2% (highest competition for attention)

Worst-performing:
- Landscapers — 1.8% (revisit copy or skip the niche)

Lessons:
- First lines that referenced a specific neighborhood (not just the city) outperformed by ~40%
- Lowercase subjects outperformed title case by ~25%
- Emails sent Tue-Thu 9-11am ET led the inbox by a noticeable margin

**One thing that surprised me:** [insert one surprise from real month 2 data]

## Section 6 — Cost analysis (26:00–28:00)

The breakdown:
- Apify scrape: $14
- Hunter.io: $49 (flat)
- MillionVerifier: $8
- Smartlead: $39 (flat)
- Anthropic API (first-lines + classification + drafts): $4.20
- Google Workspace (4 seats): $28
- VPS: $5

Total month 2: ~$147

Per-lead cost (217 sent): ~$0.68
Per-positive-reply cost (3 positives): ~$49
Per-call-booked cost (1 booked): ~$147

Frame:
> "If this one booked call turns into a $2K/mo retainer, the cost-per-acquisition is one month of margin. If it doesn't close, I'm out a month. Either way, the math works long-term."

## Section 7 — What's next (28:00–30:00)

Preview Month 3.

- Discovery call next week — full episode walkthrough coming
- Sequence v2 launching mid-month (subject line A/B winner from month 2 promoted)
- Adding electrician niche (skipped in month 1, ready now)
- Goal: 250 sends, 5 positive replies, 2 booked calls, hopefully 1 close

Outro. Subscribe nudge.

> "If you've been sitting on the 'should I try outbound for my agency' fence, I'll tell you what I told myself two months ago: the system isn't the hard part. The discipline of running it every week is the hard part. See you next month."

---

## B-roll shot list

- [ ] n8n editor full view (5 workflows visible)
- [ ] Each workflow zoomed (5 separate clips for chapter cuts)
- [ ] Smartlead campaign reply view
- [ ] HubSpot pipeline with the actual deals
- [ ] Anthropic console showing API calls + cost
- [ ] One execution log in n8n (start to finish on one lead)
- [ ] The first-line prompt opened in an editor (full screen)
- [ ] Slack notification with a drafted reply

## Thumbnail concept

Black background. Bold: `14 replies / 217 sent`. Below: "MONTH 2". Bottom right: face.

The actual reply rate as the headline number = honesty + intrigue.

## Title options
- "Month 2: 14 Replies From 217 Cold Emails (Agency From Scratch)" ← recommended
- "Month 2: My Multi-Agent Outbound System — Agency From Scratch"
- "Month 2: How AI Personalizes My Cold Emails — Agency From Scratch" — too generic

## Description draft

```
Month 2: 217 cold emails sent, 14 replies (6.4%), 3 positive, 1 discovery call booked. Here's the full multi-agent system that did it, in detail.

00:00  Cold open
00:45  Month 2 scoreboard
01:45  The five agents I run
05:00  Workflow deep-dive: push to sequencer
13:00  The first-line AI prompt (full walkthrough)
17:00  Reply triage workflow
22:00  What the data showed
26:00  Cost breakdown ($147 spent in month 2)
28:00  Coming in month 3

n8n workflow JSON, agent rule files, and the actual first-line prompt are in my agency-ops repo (link in pinned comment).

I publish the agency scoreboard the first week of every month. Real numbers. Including the bad ones.

#cold-email #automation #n8n #anthropic #ai-agents
```

## Critical things to get right

- **Don't show client/prospect names.** Anonymize. Blur. Or use the example data only.
- **Don't show your API key on screen.** Set up a separate test workspace if you have to demo.
- **Don't oversell the AI.** The system is good; it's not magic. Reply rate is mid-single-digits, not 30%. Tell the truth.
- **Don't underplay the human effort.** "Set it and forget it" is a lie. You're still reviewing every drafted reply.

files.title: Episode 3 — First 100 Cold Emails
files.content:
# Episode 3 — First 100 Cold Emails

**Target length:** 22-26 min
**Filming:** End of Month 3 (full first quarter of data in hand)
**Goal:** Take the prior episode's overview and zoom in on the actual emails — what was sent, what was opened, what was replied to, what closed. Show real copy. Show real numbers. This is the "deliverability + copy" episode.

---

## Cold open (0:00–0:45)

Pull up the inbox. Real replies visible (faces / domains blurred).

> "Three months in. One closed client. Here's exactly what got me there — every subject line, every first line, every reply."

Cut to scoreboard.

## Scoreboard (0:45–1:45)

```
MONTH 3 — JUNE 2026
Revenue this month:     ${{actual}}
MRR end of month:       ${{actual}}
Active clients:         {{actual}}
Cumulative cold emails: ~500
Cumulative replies:     ~35
Closed clients:         {{actual}}
Hours worked:           ~55
Effective hourly:       ${{actual}}

Last month I said I'd:
  → 250 sends:    {{actual}}
  → 5 positives:  {{actual}}
  → 2 calls:      {{actual}}
  → 1 close:      {{actual}}
```

This episode requires real month-3 data, so the script is templated. Fill in the actual numbers when filming.

## Section 1 — Why deliverability is the whole game (1:45–4:00)

Talking head. No B-roll.

Core point: "You can have the world's best copy, but if your emails land in spam, you don't have a campaign — you have a writing exercise."

Cover:
- DKIM, SPF, DMARC (briefly — don't lecture)
- Domain warmup (why 2 weeks isn't optional)
- The two cold domains, why not subdomains
- Bounce rate as the canary in the coal mine
- One specific story: a near-miss where bounce rate spiked and I paused sends

## Section 2 — Subject line tournament (4:00–9:00)

Show the subject line bank as a leaderboard.

Walk through:
- Top 5 subjects by reply rate (from `subject-line-bank.md`)
- Bottom 5 — including one that looked clever but tanked
- Surprise finding (lowercase vs title case; or whatever your real data shows)

Visual: a horizontal bar chart of subject lines ranked by reply rate.

Lessons:
- Specificity beats clever every time
- Lowercase signals "this isn't a marketing blast"
- "Re:" works ONLY on legitimate follow-ups (not the first email)
- City-specific outperforms niche-specific outperforms generic

## Section 3 — The first lines that worked (9:00–14:00)

Show 5-7 real first lines. Anonymize the business names.

For each one, show:
- The first line itself
- The scraped GBP data that fed into it
- The reply that came back

This is the "show the work" moment. The technical audience wants to see input → AI → output → human response.

Example structure:

> First line: "Saw the modern farmhouse build you finished in South Lakeland — the porch detail caught my eye."
> What fed it: GBP photo caption "South Lakeland new build — 4 bed, modern farmhouse, finished March 2026."
> Reply: "Thanks for the note. Curious what you saw that we could improve." (positive, intent: medium)

End the section with one first line that FAILED. Show what was off. The "I learned this from a bad output" beat lands.

## Section 4 — Anatomy of the close (14:00–19:00)

Walk through the one closed deal end-to-end (anonymize).

1. The cold email (subject + first line + body)
2. The reply
3. The drafted response (and any edit you made)
4. The call (don't replay the call; summarize what you discussed)
5. The proposal (show Documenso template, blur the client's name)
6. The signing (show the Documenso event in n8n logs)
7. The Stripe customer creation
8. The first invoice paid

Total time from first cold email to paid invoice: X days.

This timeline is the most important graphic in the episode. Title it "Lead to Paid: Day-by-Day."

## Section 5 — What didn't work (19:00–22:00)

Mandatory section. Failures get watched more than wins.

Cover:
- The subject line A/B that broke even (no clear winner — bad data, no decision)
- The niche that didn't reply (landscapers? or whichever your data shows)
- A specific reply that I mishandled (be honest)
- A cost overrun (Apify scrape cost went over budget because of X)

End the failure section with: "I'd rather show you these than pretend month 3 was clean. Month 3 was not clean."

## Section 6 — Cumulative cost vs revenue (22:00–24:00)

Math on screen.

```
Cumulative spend (months 1-3):
  Foundation (DNS, Workspace, VPS):    $XXX
  Outbound tools (Smartlead, Apify, Hunter, MillionVerifier): $XXX
  Pipeline (HubSpot free, Cal.com free, Documenso self-hosted): $XX
  AI (Anthropic API):                  $XX
  TOTAL:                               $XXX

Cumulative revenue (months 1-3):
  $XXX

Net: -$XXX  (still in setup investment phase)
Months to breakeven at current MRR growth: X months
Months to W2 replacement at current MRR growth: X months
```

Don't pretend this looks great. Math is math.

## Section 7 — What's next (24:00–26:00)

Month 4 plan:
- Scale sends to 100/week
- First sequence v2 test (winner subject promoted; new email 2 variant)
- Add pool builders niche
- First monthly report due to the new client (show the template briefly)
- Goal: 2 more closes by end of month 4

Outro:

> "Last month I said the system isn't the hard part — the discipline is. I'm three months in, and I can confirm that's still true. The system runs whether I'm tired or not. The judgment calls — which leads to skip, when to push back on a prospect, when to stop iterating a bad subject — those still need me."

---

## B-roll shot list

- [ ] Inbox with replies visible (blurred)
- [ ] Smartlead campaign analytics view
- [ ] Subject line leaderboard (mockup or live data export)
- [ ] HubSpot deal moving through stages (capture as a screen recording)
- [ ] Documenso signed document confirmation
- [ ] Stripe dashboard showing the first transaction
- [ ] n8n execution log showing the full signed → onboarding cascade

## Thumbnail concept

Top half: a subject line in quotes ("quick question about your lakeland site")
Bottom: "MONTH 3 — 1st PAID CLIENT"
Norse rune corner mark
Face in bottom right

## Title options
- "Month 3: First Paid Client — Agency From Scratch"
- "Month 3: 500 Cold Emails, 1 Paying Client (the full breakdown)"
- "Month 3: Every Subject Line Ranked — Agency From Scratch" ← good if the subject-line section is the standout

## Description draft

```
Month 3. 500 total cold emails sent. 1 paying client. Here's every subject line, every first line, and the full timeline from cold email to first paid invoice.

00:00  Cold open
00:45  Month 3 scoreboard
01:45  Why deliverability is everything
04:00  Subject line tournament (ranked by reply rate)
09:00  First lines that worked (with the GBP data that fed them)
14:00  Anatomy of the first close — day by day
19:00  What didn't work
22:00  Cumulative cost vs revenue
24:00  What's coming in month 4

Subject line bank, first-line prompt, and sequence v1 are in the public repo (link in pinned comment).

Real numbers, every month, including the bad ones. No paid sponsors.

#cold-email #agency #salesfunnels #buildinginpublic
```

## Don't ship until

- [ ] All client/prospect names blurred or replaced with initials
- [ ] No identifying info about your W2 employer anywhere on screen
- [ ] Numbers cross-checked against HubSpot + Stripe + Smartlead actual values
- [ ] Loom walkthrough of the closed deal anonymized correctly
- [ ] Audio levels normalized to -16 LUFS (YouTube standard)
- [ ] Captions auto-generated and reviewed for proper-noun mistakes
