files.title: Agency From Scratch — 12-Episode YouTube Series
files.content:
# Agency From Scratch — 12-Episode YouTube Series

**Premise:** Build a one-person agency from zero to W2-replacement MRR in 12 months. Document every step. Show actual revenue. Show the workflows. Show what doesn't work. Aimed at frontend engineers and technical builders who are agency-curious but allergic to the typical "make $10K/mo selling courses" YouTube ecosystem.

**Why this exists** (besides marketing):
1. Forcing transparency keeps me honest with myself
2. Public scoreboard is the cheapest accountability mechanism
3. The audience who'd find this interesting (technical builders considering agency life) is also a tertiary audience for the agency itself — if any of you happen to be a contractor or know one, that's a bonus, not the point

**Why this works as content** (the cold strategy):
- Specific (not generic agency advice)
- Show-the-numbers (not "here's how to think about it")
- Technical (the audience can see the workflow code, not just hear about it)
- Geographic (Polk County FL grounds it in reality)
- Long arc (12 months, not 7 days to $10K)

**Frequency:** Monthly, ~25 min each. The cadence doubles as a monthly retro deadline.

---

## Voice & tone

- First-person, conversational, lightly self-deprecating
- Norse imagery used at most 1-2 times per episode (intro/outro graphic, maybe one tagline)
- Show actual screens: HubSpot, n8n workflows, Stripe dashboard, the inbox
- Don't hide losses — losses are the most-viewed content in transparent series
- No clickbait thumbnails. Thumbnails are: month number + headline number ("MONTH 4 — $3,200 MRR") + maybe a face
- No "the secret to..." or "the ONE trick" subtitles. Ever.

---

## The Scoreboard (recurring segment)

Every episode opens with the same 60-second segment:

```
Month {{n}}
Revenue this month:     ${{n}}
MRR end of month:       ${{n}}
Active clients:         {{n}}
Hours worked:           {{n}}
Effective hourly:       ${{n}}

Last month I said I'd:  [3 bullets]
Did I?                  [yes / partial / no per bullet]
```

Template lives in `scoreboard-template.md`. Don't skip this segment even on bad months. ESPECIALLY don't skip on bad months.

---

## The 12-episode arc

### Episode 1 — The Stack (Month 1)
Why these tools, why this approach. Skip the "should you start an agency" debate. Address the W2 elephant directly.

### Episode 2 — Multi-Agent Outbound (Month 2)
The Cowork + Claude Code + n8n system. Walk through actual workflow JSON. Show the AI prompts.

### Episode 3 — First 100 Cold Emails (Month 3)
What was sent, what worked, what didn't. Real reply rates. Real subject lines. Real first lines. Real numbers.

### Episode 4 — First Discovery Call (Month 3 or 4)
Recording of a real discovery call (with client consent), or anonymized walkthrough if not. What I said wrong. What I'd change.

### Episode 5 — First Signed Client (target Month 4-5)
The proposal, the negotiation, the close. Show the Stripe dashboard with the actual transaction. This is the inflection-point episode.

### Episode 6 — The Onboarding Sprint (Month 5)
First 30 days of a real client. Site build from scratch. GBP optimization. The boring details that nobody else shows.

### Episode 7 — Three Clients Deep (Month 6)
What happens when delivery volume meets your remaining hours in the day. The first time you have to say no.

### Episode 8 — The Iteration Pass (Month 7)
What's worked, what hasn't. Sequence v2. New niches. Sub-contracting trials.

### Episode 9 — Renewal Decision (Month 8 or 9)
First client's 6-month contract ends. Did they renew? Why or why not? What it means.

### Episode 10 — The Math Check (Month 9)
Real margins. Real hourly. Comparison to the W2. Lessons that only show up after 9 months of data.

### Episode 11 — Scaling Decision (Month 10-11)
Stay solo? Hire? Outsource? VA? The fork in the road.

### Episode 12 — The 12-Month Retro (Month 12)
Did I replace the W2? If yes: what's next. If no: why, and what changes. Either way, the most honest episode of the series.

---

## Production approach (cheap, fast, repeatable)

**Format:** Screen recordings + face cam, edited in DaVinci Resolve (free). 16:9 horizontal. 1080p.

**Equipment:**
- USB condenser mic (one-time $80)
- Logitech webcam or iPhone-as-webcam (free if you have an iPhone)
- That's it

**Workflow per episode:**
1. Day 1-2 of the month: outline draft (markdown file like `epXX-name.md` here)
2. Day 3-4: record screen segments
3. Day 5: record face cam intros and bridges
4. Day 6-7: edit
5. Day 8-9: thumbnail + title + description
6. Day 10: publish

If recording takes more than 4 hours total, the episode is too ambitious. Cut.

**Thumbnail format (consistent across series):**
- Top-left: "MONTH X" in bold
- Center: a single key number or phrase
- Bottom-right: your face (small)
- Color: black background with one Norse accent color (deep gold or muted ice-blue, sparingly)

---

## SEO + distribution

- **YouTube title format:** `Month [N]: [One concrete thing from the episode] — Agency From Scratch`
  - Good: "Month 3: First 5 Replies From 100 Cold Emails — Agency From Scratch"
  - Bad: "How I Built My Marketing Agency Side Hustle (You Won't BELIEVE!)"

- **YouTube description format:**
  - First paragraph: what this episode covers (3 sentences max)
  - Numbered timestamps
  - Links to: the agency site, the GitHub repo (if any code is shared), the previous episode
  - A short PS at the bottom for the cold viewer who happens to be a contractor

- **Repurpose:** every episode gets:
  - A blog post on `thenordicnerd.com/blog/` (~800 words, scoreboard table + key takeaways)
  - 3-4 LinkedIn posts (one per major section)
  - 1-2 Twitter/X threads
  - A short clip for Shorts/Reels (the most-shareable 30 seconds)

The repurpose is mechanical, not creative — done same-day as the publish to avoid the "I'll repurpose it later" trap.

---

## What to NOT do

- **Don't fake numbers.** Once. If you fake one scoreboard, the credibility's gone and the series is worthless.
- **Don't promise specific results.** "$10K MRR in 12 months" is a goal, not a marketing claim.
- **Don't name your W2 employer.** Ever. Even casually. Even on a private call. (See `00-Account-Hygiene/bright-line-rules.md`.)
- **Don't name clients without explicit written permission.** Default to anonymous ("a roofer in Bartow") or initials.
- **Don't film on the work laptop. Don't sign into the work Slack mid-episode. Don't have any work tabs visible.**
- **Don't optimize for virality.** This is a slow-burn audience-building play. 200 views from the right people > 20,000 views from the wrong ones.

---

## Engagement loop

People who actually engage (comment, email back, DM) are the seed audience for:
- Cohort 2 of the agency (if you decide to take on overflow / partners)
- The eventual product (if there is one — don't plan it, but stay aware)
- Job referrals (frontend engineers who consume technical-builder content tend to know other frontend engineers)

Respond to every comment in the first 90 days. After that, scale however you can.

---

## The honest disclaimer

Public scoreboard means public losses. If month 6 ends at $1,200 MRR instead of $5,000, that episode still ships. The series is worthless without the failure data — and frankly the failure data is what your target audience actually wants.

If you can't commit to publishing a bad-numbers month, don't start the series.

files.title: City × Niche Page Template (Nuxt Content)
files.content:
# City × Niche Page Template (Nuxt Content)

**Purpose:** Reusable structure for local SEO pages. Each page targets a `[niche] in [city] FL` keyword cluster. These pages are the workhorses of local SEO — boring, high-value, evergreen.

**File location in Nuxt project:** `content/locations/[niche]-[city]-fl.md`

**URL pattern:** `/[niche]-[city]-fl` (handled by Nuxt Content's automatic routing or a custom slug rule).

---

## Frontmatter (every page)

```yaml
---
title: "[Niche] in [City], FL — [Client Brand]"
description: "[140-160 char meta description with city + niche + phone or CTA]"
city: "[City]"
state: "FL"
niche: "[niche-slug]"
client: "[client-slug]"
publishedAt: 2026-MM-DD
schema:
  "@context": "https://schema.org"
  "@type": "LocalBusiness"
  name: "[Client Brand]"
  address:
    "@type": "PostalAddress"
    streetAddress: "[street]"
    addressLocality: "[city]"
    addressRegion: "FL"
    postalCode: "[zip]"
  telephone: "[phone]"
  url: "https://[client-domain]/[niche]-[city]-fl"
  areaServed:
    "@type": "City"
    name: "[City]"
  priceRange: "$$"
  openingHours: "Mo-Fr 08:00-17:00"
---
```

JSON-LD gets injected by the Nuxt page wrapper into a `<script type="application/ld+json">` tag in the page head.

---

## Page structure (Markdown body, ~1,200–1,800 words)

### H1
```
# [Niche] in [City], FL — [Client Brand]
```

Exact match for primary keyword. Don't get cute with synonyms in the H1; this is the page's job.

### Hero paragraph (50-80 words)

```
[One sentence value prop tied to city.] [One sentence credibility — years in business, certifications, or a specific local fact.] [One sentence call to action — call, book, or visit.]
```

Example:
> When you're looking for kitchen remodelers in Lakeland, FL, you want a team that knows the difference between a Lake Hollingsworth bungalow and a Hallam Preserve new-build — and finishes both on time. [Client Brand] has been remodeling Polk County kitchens since 1998 with a 4.9-star average across 247 Google reviews. Call us at [phone] or book a free in-home consult.

### Service area (Section 2, ~100 words)

H2: `Who we serve in [City] and surrounding Polk County`

Mention specific neighborhoods (3-5). This is critical for hyper-local ranking signals.

Example:
> [Client Brand] regularly works with homeowners across Lakeland — South Lake Morton, Cleveland Heights, Beacon Hill, Lake Hollingsworth, and the newer Hallam Preserve and Whittington developments. We also serve adjacent communities including Mulberry, Highland City, and Combee Settlement.

### Common projects (Section 3, ~250 words)

H2: `Common [niche] projects in [City]`

Tie to local realities — climate, housing stock, building codes. This is where AI-generated pages fall apart and hand-edited pages win.

For Florida specifics:
- Hurricane considerations (impact windows, roof tie-downs, shutters)
- Florida-friendly drought-tolerant landscaping
- Insurance carrier requirements (4-point inspections, wind mitigation)
- Salt-air corrosion (for coastal Polk areas — not all)
- Older homes vs new developments

3-5 concrete project types, each 50-100 words. Real specifics — not generic ("we do kitchens").

### Process (Section 4, ~150 words)

H2: `How we work`

A clear 4-step process. Bullets here are fine if it's a process flow.

```
1. **Free in-home consult** — usually within 5 business days of your call.
2. **Detailed quote** — within 7 days, broken out by line item, no surprise fees.
3. **Materials selection** — we walk you through trusted local suppliers, or honor your selections.
4. **Build phase** — with weekly progress updates and a single point of contact.
```

### Recent work (Section 5, ~150 words)

H2: `Recent [niche] projects in [City]`

Photo gallery with captions tying to neighborhoods or project types. 4-6 photos.

Markdown:
```markdown
::project-gallery
- src: /img/projects/lakeland-modern-farmhouse-1.jpg
  caption: "South Lakeland modern farmhouse kitchen reno — quartz waterfall, white oak"
  neighborhood: "South Lakeland"
- src: /img/projects/lakeland-bungalow-bath.jpg
  caption: "Historic Cleveland Heights bath remodel — preserved original tile pattern"
  neighborhood: "Cleveland Heights"
::
```

If photos don't exist yet, omit this section rather than using stock photos. Stock photos hurt local SEO.

### Reviews (Section 6, ~150 words)

H2: `What [City] customers say`

3-5 real review snippets. Authentic > polished. Cite by first name + neighborhood (with permission).

```markdown
> "Bob and his team rebuilt our kitchen in 6 weeks flat. Lived through demo, drywall dust, and tile-cutting — they cleaned every night. Worth every dollar."
> — **Sarah K., Hallam Preserve**

> "We'd been quoted $48K by two other Lakeland contractors. [Client Brand] came in at $36K with the same materials list and finished early. No idea how, but we're not complaining."
> — **Mike R., South Lakeland**
```

Reviews must be:
- Real (with written permission — keep records)
- City-specific (mention the neighborhood)
- Believable (one mildly-mixed review actually helps trust)

### FAQ (Section 7, ~250 words)

H2: `[Niche] questions [City] homeowners ask`

5-7 questions targeting long-tail keywords. Format as proper FAQ schema (FAQPage JSON-LD).

```markdown
### How much does a kitchen remodel cost in Lakeland, FL?
A full kitchen remodel in Lakeland typically runs $35,000-$75,000 depending on cabinet quality, countertop material, and whether the layout changes. Cosmetic refreshes (paint, hardware, lighting) can be done for $8,000-$15,000.

### How long does a kitchen remodel take?
Most full kitchen remodels in Polk County take 6-10 weeks from demo to walk-through. Cosmetic refreshes take 2-4 weeks. Major layout changes that require permitting can stretch to 12 weeks.

### Do I need a permit for kitchen work in Lakeland?
Cosmetic work (paint, cabinet refacing, countertop replacement) usually doesn't require a permit in Lakeland. Anything that touches plumbing, electrical, or structural elements does. We pull all required permits as part of our standard process.

[...]
```

The FAQ schema markup should mirror these Q&As exactly.

### CTA (Section 8, ~50 words)

H2: `Get a free quote`

Two CTAs — visible phone number + Cal.com booking link + a form embed.

```markdown
Ready to talk about your [niche] project in [City]? Three ways to reach us:

- **Call:** [phone] (Mon-Fri 8a-5p)
- **Book a free consult:** [Cal.com link]
- **Or fill out the form below and we'll respond within one business day.**

::contact-form
client: [client-slug]
location: [City]
::
```

---

## Internal linking

Each page must link to:
- The client's home page (`[/]`)
- The client's services page (`[/services]`)
- 2-3 other city/niche pages where natural (e.g., a Lakeland kitchen page links to Bartow kitchens and to Lakeland bath remodel)
- The client's about/contact page

This builds the internal link graph that helps Google understand site topical authority.

## Image requirements

- All images use Nuxt Image (`<NuxtImg>`) with `format=webp` + `loading=lazy`
- Alt text describes the project specifically (e.g., "White oak kitchen with quartz waterfall island in South Lakeland modern farmhouse" — NOT "kitchen renovation").
- File names use the same pattern: `[city]-[niche]-[project-type]-[number].jpg`
- Hero photo aspect ratio: 16:9 minimum 1920×1080
- Gallery photos: 4:3 ratio, 1200×900 minimum

## Performance budget (Lighthouse, blocking deploy)

- Performance: ≥90
- Accessibility: ≥95
- Best Practices: ≥95
- SEO: 100

If a page comes in below, fix before deploy. Most common offenders: too-heavy hero images, third-party scripts, missing alt text.

## Schema markup checklist

Each page gets:
- [ ] `LocalBusiness` schema (page-level, in head)
- [ ] `BreadcrumbList` schema
- [ ] `FAQPage` schema if FAQ section exists
- [ ] `Service` schema with `areaServed` set to the city
- [ ] Validates on https://validator.schema.org

## Content quality bar

Bad version (don't ship):
> "We are a leading kitchen remodeling company in Lakeland, FL. Our team of experienced professionals delivers high-quality kitchen remodels that exceed expectations."

Good version (ship):
> "We've remodeled 47 kitchens in South Lakeland since 2019. Half of them were in homes built before 1995, which means we deal with the same things every time: lath-and-plaster walls, undersized 100-amp panels, and cabinet boxes that aren't square. We bring the right tools."

The Bad version was generated by an LLM in 2 seconds. The Good version was edited by a human who knows what an old Florida home looks like. The Good version ranks; the Bad version doesn't.

## Refresh cadence

Every 6 months:
- Update review snippets if newer/better ones exist
- Refresh photo gallery with latest work
- Update FAQ section if pricing or process has shifted
- Re-validate schema
- Re-run Lighthouse audit

Google rewards freshness for local queries.

files.title: Q1 Content Calendar
files.content:
# Q1 Content Calendar

**Period:** First 3 months of agency operation (Months 1–3 of the W2-replacement timeline).
**Output target:** 1 long-form blog post per week on `thenordicnerd.com/blog/` + 1 city/niche SEO page per week.

**Two streams running in parallel:**

1. **Top-of-funnel SEO pages** — keyword-driven. Goal: rank for "[niche] in [city] FL" cluster. Pulls in organic search traffic that doesn't know who you are yet.
2. **Authority blog posts** — point-of-view content. Goal: build credibility with the cold-email audience who Googles your name after reading your email.

The SEO pages get the city/niche template (see `city-niche-page-template.md`). The blog posts are stand-alone essays.

---

## Month 1 — Foundation (4 posts + 4 pages)

### Week 1
- **Blog:** "What I built before I sent a single cold email"
  - Inside: the actual stack inventory, why each tool, costs. The accompanying YouTube ep1 lives alongside it.
  - Target keyword: none specific — branded search defense
  - Word count: 1,500

- **SEO page:** `/general-contractors-lakeland-fl`
  - Target keyword: "general contractor lakeland fl"
  - Word count: 1,500

### Week 2
- **Blog:** "Why I'm building a marketing agency for contractors instead of a SaaS"
  - Inside: the strategic case for services over product. Audience: technical builders who'd otherwise default to SaaS.
  - Target keyword: long-tail / organic discovery
  - Word count: 1,800

- **SEO page:** `/kitchen-remodelers-lakeland-fl`
  - Target keyword: "kitchen remodelers lakeland fl"
  - Word count: 1,500

### Week 3
- **Blog:** "Self-hosted n8n for a one-person agency: complete setup ($5/mo)"
  - Inside: walk through the deployment doc as a public guide. Brings in technical audience.
  - Target keyword: "n8n self host" + adjacencies
  - Word count: 2,500 (technical posts get more length)

- **SEO page:** `/roofers-winter-haven-fl`
  - Target keyword: "roofers winter haven fl"
  - Word count: 1,500

### Week 4
- **Blog:** "The Florida contractor's Google Business Profile checklist"
  - Inside: 10-point GBP audit. Audience: contractors who read your blog from cold email. Doubles as lead magnet.
  - Target keyword: "Google Business Profile checklist contractors"
  - Word count: 1,200

- **SEO page:** `/hvac-contractors-bartow-fl`
  - Target keyword: "hvac contractors bartow fl"
  - Word count: 1,500

---

## Month 2 — Outbound deep-dives (4 posts + 4 pages)

### Week 5
- **Blog:** "AI-personalized cold email: the actual prompt I use"
  - Inside: paste the first-line system prompt, walk through it, show real outputs. Audience: technical builders running outbound.
  - Word count: 2,000

- **SEO page:** `/plumbers-auburndale-fl`

### Week 6
- **Blog:** "Subject line tournament: 30 cold-email subjects ranked by reply rate"
  - Inside: real data from month 1-2 sends. Highly shareable.
  - Word count: 1,600

- **SEO page:** `/electricians-haines-city-fl`

### Week 7
- **Blog:** "How I classify and respond to cold-email replies with Claude (full workflow)"
  - Inside: reply triage workflow, classification prompt, draft prompt. Show actual examples.
  - Word count: 2,200

- **SEO page:** `/landscapers-davenport-fl`

### Week 8
- **Blog:** "What a $5/mo Hetzner box can host: n8n + Documenso + Postgres + Caddy"
  - Inside: technical post for the self-host-curious. Republished from the deployment doc with extra context.
  - Word count: 2,500

- **SEO page:** `/pool-builders-lake-wales-fl`

---

## Month 3 — Real numbers (4 posts + 4 pages)

### Week 9
- **Blog:** "First 100 cold emails: subject lines, first lines, replies, results"
  - Inside: full month-2 and month-3 data, accompanies YouTube ep3.
  - Word count: 2,000

- **SEO page:** `/general-contractors-winter-haven-fl`

### Week 10
- **Blog:** "Anatomy of a $2K/month retainer: from cold email to signed contract"
  - Inside: anonymized walkthrough of the first close.
  - Word count: 1,800

- **SEO page:** `/kitchen-remodelers-winter-haven-fl`

### Week 11
- **Blog:** "Why most Florida contractors lose to Google: 5 things the top-ranking businesses do"
  - Inside: contractor-audience-focused content. Strong distribution potential.
  - Word count: 1,400

- **SEO page:** `/roofers-bartow-fl`

### Week 12 (Quarter retro)
- **Blog:** "90 days, 500 cold emails, 1 paying client: the Q1 retro"
  - Inside: cumulative numbers, what worked, what to change. Sets up Q2.
  - Word count: 1,800

- **SEO page:** `/hvac-contractors-lakeland-fl`

---

## Distribution per post (mechanical, not optional)

Every blog post gets:
1. Published to `thenordicnerd.com/blog/` (Nuxt Content)
2. Cross-posted as a LinkedIn article (full text)
3. Broken into 3-5 LinkedIn standalone posts (one per major section)
4. Twitter/X thread (8-12 tweets)
5. Email to MailerLite list (when list exists)

Every SEO page:
1. Published to `thenordicnerd.com/[niche]-[city]-fl`
2. Submitted to Google Search Console
3. Internal links from at least 2 other pages (especially the blog post that mentions the niche)
4. JSON-LD `Article` + `LocalBusiness` schema

## Time budget

- Blog post: 4 hours total (90 min outline + 2 hours draft + 30 min edit)
- SEO page: 3 hours total (60 min outline + 2 hours draft + 30 min schema/QA)
- Distribution: 1 hour per blog (LinkedIn, Twitter, etc.)

Total content time per week: ~8 hours.

This is sustainable for a few months while building. If it bumps up against client delivery, prioritize:
1. Client delivery (paying)
2. Blog post (long-term audience, slow burn)
3. SEO page (rankings compound over time)
4. Distribution (immediate but lower compounding)

## What's NOT in the Q1 plan

- Guest posts on other sites (Q2 priority once you have track record)
- Podcast appearances (Q2-Q3)
- Conference talks (year 2)
- Paid content distribution (never, unless A/B testing organic)
- Generic listicle SEO ("10 best CRMs for contractors") — done to death, no differentiation

## Editorial guardrails

- **No AI-only content.** Every post is hand-edited. AI is for first drafts and research; humans for voice and judgment.
- **No clickbait headlines.** "How I 10x'd my MRR with this ONE prompt" — never.
- **No fake authority.** If a topic is outside your expertise, don't write about it.
- **Always tie back to specifics** (Polk County, FL, contractor niches, real numbers).
- **Internal linking** — every new post links to 2-3 older posts where natural.
- **One CTA per post.** Either Cal.com link, or email signup, or "read the next post." Not all three.

## Measurement

Weekly check (in `06-Daily-Ops/weekly-metrics-template.md`):
- Total blog/SEO page traffic (Plausible)
- Top 3 pages by traffic
- Conversion events from organic (any Cal.com bookings or email signups attributed to a blog post)

If a post hits 1,000+ visitors in its first 30 days, double down on the topic with a follow-up.
If a post gets <50 visitors after 90 days, the topic was wrong. Don't write more like it.

## Q2 preview (do NOT plan yet — but flag the direction)

- Case studies from real clients (once 2-3 are 90+ days in)
- Niche deep-dives (a roofing-specific guide, a pool-builder-specific guide)
- Tooling reviews ("we tried 4 cold-email sequencers; here's what we kept")
- The "build in public" milestone posts (50k MRR, 10 clients, etc.)

files.title: Monthly Retro Template
files.content:
# Monthly Retro Template

**When:** Last Friday of each month, 90 minutes. Block the calendar like a meeting with someone important — because you are.

**Where:** Away from the desk. Coffee shop, park, even just a different room. Different physical context shakes loose different thinking.

**Output:**
1. The internal scoreboard fully filled (per `05-Content/agency-from-scratch-youtube/scoreboard-template.md`)
2. This retro doc completed
3. Three priorities for the new month (the same three become Operations Agent's anchors)
4. Any agent rule updates queued (in `02-Agents/`)
5. The public-facing version: blog post + YouTube episode outline

**Honesty bar:** This is the place to be most honest with yourself. No spin. The audience for this is you-in-12-months.

---

## Part 1: The scoreboard (15 min)

Fill in the internal scoreboard (template in `05-Content/agency-from-scratch-youtube/scoreboard-template.md`).

This is mechanical — most numbers are auto-populated by Operations Agent. You're filling the qualitative gaps:
- Time allocation breakdown (estimate by category)
- Loss reason summary
- Cohort retention if applicable
- W2-replacement tracker

Don't analyze yet. Just get the numbers down.

---

## Part 2: What happened (20 min)

Free-form recap. Bullet-list whatever happened. Don't filter. Don't categorize yet.

Examples of things to include:
- Major wins (deals closed, milestones hit, content that landed)
- Major losses (deals lost, sequence experiments that failed, missed deadlines)
- New people (clients, leads, advisors)
- New things tried (new tools, new processes, new niches)
- Emotional/energetic moments — when you were lit up, when you wanted to quit
- External events that affected the month (conference, family thing, W2 demands)

Aim for 15-25 bullets. Don't edit.

---

## Part 3: The math check (15 min)

Now look at the scoreboard like a stranger would.

**Question 1: Is MRR growing?**
- If yes: by how much? Is the growth rate accelerating, steady, or decelerating?
- If no: is this a flat month, or a real concern?
- 4-week trailing MRR growth: ${{}}/month average

**Question 2: Is the funnel math improving?**
- Reply rate: trending up, flat, or down?
- Reply → call → proposal → close conversion: any specific stage degrading?
- Cost per booked call: trending in the right direction?

**Question 3: Is delivery sustainable?**
- Hours worked this month: ___
- If you tripled current MRR, would current hours support it? At what point does it break?
- Are any clients at risk of churn? Why?

**Question 4: Is the W2 replacement timeline tracking?**
- Projected months to target at current growth: {{}}
- Acceptable: <12 months by month 6
- Worrying: >18 months by month 6
- If worrying: what's the bottleneck — top of funnel (need more leads), conversion (close rate too low), or scope (deals too small)?

---

## Part 4: What worked, what didn't (20 min)

### What worked — keep doing

(5 specific things, not categories. Each gets a one-line "why I think it worked" — gut + data.)

1. {{thing 1}} — why: {{}}
2. {{thing 2}} — why: {{}}
3. {{thing 3}} — why: {{}}
4. {{thing 4}} — why: {{}}
5. {{thing 5}} — why: {{}}

### What didn't work — stop or change

(5 specific things. For each: stop entirely, or modify how?)

1. {{thing 1}} — action: {{stop / modify / iterate}}: {{specifics}}
2. {{thing 2}} — action: {{}}
3. {{thing 3}} — action: {{}}
4. {{thing 4}} — action: {{}}
5. {{thing 5}} — action: {{}}

### What was a surprise

(2-3 things you didn't see coming, good or bad. These often point to the most important lessons.)

1. {{surprise 1}} — what does this mean for next month?
2. {{surprise 2}} — what does this mean?

---

## Part 5: System & agent updates (15 min)

Review the five agent rule files (`02-Agents/*.md`). For each:

**Outbound Ops Agent:** any thresholds to update? Any new failure mode to add to escalations?
**Copywriter Agent:** any banned words to add? Any new positive examples? Voice drift to correct?
**Pipeline Agent:** any new automation gap? Any deal-state transition not handled cleanly?
**Client Delivery Agent:** any tier deliverable that needs adjusting? Any client expectation that needs better framing?
**Operations Agent:** anything Operations is missing or doing wrong? Daily briefing format adjust?

Write proposed changes here, then apply them to the rule files this weekend.

```
Proposed agent updates:
- Outbound Ops: {{change}}
- Copywriter: {{change}}
- Pipeline: {{change}}
- Client Delivery: {{change}}
- Operations: {{change}}
```

Also review the n8n workflows for proposed changes:
- Any workflow throwing errors regularly?
- Any new automation worth adding?
- Any manual task being done weekly that could become a workflow?

---

## Part 6: Three priorities for next month (15 min)

This is the most important section. Don't rush.

Three things. Each one has:
- A specific outcome (not a category)
- A clear "done" definition
- A "why" — why this and not something else
- A leading indicator you can check weekly

Example bad: "Get more clients." (vague)
Example good: "Close 2 new clients by end of month X by sending 250 emails/week and converting 2/4 booked calls — leading indicator: positive replies per week ≥4."

```
Priority 1: {{}}
  Outcome: {{}}
  Done when: {{}}
  Why: {{}}
  Leading indicator: {{}}

Priority 2: {{}}
  Outcome: {{}}
  Done when: {{}}
  Why: {{}}
  Leading indicator: {{}}

Priority 3: {{}}
  Outcome: {{}}
  Done when: {{}}
  Why: {{}}
  Leading indicator: {{}}
```

The Operations Agent uses these to anchor the next month's daily briefings. The YouTube episode preview at the end of this month's video states these three.

---

## Part 7: One question to sit with (5 min)

This is reflective, not strategic. One question to carry into next month.

Examples:
- "Why am I avoiding [specific task]?"
- "Would I refer my own service to a friend right now? If not, why?"
- "What's the most expensive thing I'm not doing?"
- "Am I building the agency I want to run in 5 years, or the one that feels safest now?"
- "What would I change if I had to triple delivery overnight?"

No answer required today. Just the question, written down.

```
This month's question:
{{}}
```

---

## Part 8: Public version (15 min)

Translate this retro into:

1. **YouTube episode outline** — go to `05-Content/agency-from-scratch-youtube/`, create `epXX-month-name.md` from the template structure (see existing ep01-03 outlines as reference). Use the scoreboard, the three priorities, the "what worked / didn't" sections.

2. **Blog post draft** — same content, written form. Around 1,500 words. Lives at `thenordicnerd.com/blog/month-X-retro`.

3. **3-5 LinkedIn posts** — one per major theme from this retro.

Don't ship today. Draft today, edit Saturday or Sunday, publish Monday with the new month's first episode.

---

## After the retro

By Monday morning:
- [ ] Public scoreboard graphic generated
- [ ] YouTube episode outline complete
- [ ] Blog post draft complete
- [ ] Agent rule files updated with proposed changes
- [ ] Three priorities loaded into Operations Agent's anchor list
- [ ] This retro doc saved to `06-Daily-Ops/monthly-archives/YYYY-MM.md`

By Wednesday:
- [ ] YouTube episode filmed
- [ ] Blog post finalized and published
- [ ] LinkedIn posts queued

By Friday:
- [ ] YouTube episode published
- [ ] First week of new month's three priorities is in motion

---

## Anti-patterns

- **Skipping it.** The hardest month to do this is the month you most need to do it (bad month).
- **Making it all positive.** Real retros find ugly things. Lean in.
- **Making it all negative.** You're not the only stakeholder in your own happiness. Acknowledge the wins.
- **5+ priorities.** You'll do none. Three.
- **Vague priorities.** "Improve outbound" is a category. "Test sequence v2 head-to-head with v1, 200 sends each, decision by month-end" is a priority.
- **Treating the retro as a YouTube episode planning meeting.** The content output is downstream. The retro is for you.

---

## The retro discipline compounds

A year of monthly retros gives you 12 data points and 12 priority cycles. The pattern recognition that emerges (e.g., "I always over-estimate sales velocity in month 1 of a quarter") is the kind of self-knowledge that takes most operators 5+ years to develop. The retro is the artifact that makes that compounding possible.

Don't skip it.

files.title: Morning Review — 15 minutes, every weekday
files.content:
# Morning Review — 15 minutes, every weekday

**When:** First 15 min of agency time. Coffee in hand. Phone face-down.

**Where:** The Operations Agent's auto-generated daily briefing arrives in your inbox/Slack at 6:30am ET. Read THAT first. This checklist is the manual layer on top.

**Goal:** By 7am you know exactly what you're working on today and what's broken.

---

## The order

Do these in order. Don't skip.

### 1. Deliverability (2 min)

Open Smartlead → Email Health dashboard.

- [ ] All 3 cold inboxes green?
- [ ] Bounce rate trend looks normal (under 3% rolling 7-day)?
- [ ] Spam complaint rate under 0.1%?

If anything is red:
- **Pause the affected inbox immediately.** It's almost always cheaper than letting a bad day continue.
- Then investigate. Don't try to investigate-then-pause; you'll second-guess yourself.

### 2. Reply queue (5 min)

Open `#replies-pending` in Slack (or your equivalent).

- [ ] Any drafts waiting for you to review and send?
- [ ] Any Tier 1 escalations in `#critical-replies`?

For each pending draft:
- Read their reply (15 seconds)
- Read the AI's draft (15 seconds)
- Either: click "Send as-is", click "Edit in Gmail" and tweak, or click "Don't send" with a reason

For escalations: don't try to handle in 5 minutes. Acknowledge it, schedule 30 min later today.

**Hard rule:** every positive reply gets a response within 24 business hours. If you're in danger of missing SLA on something, drop the daily review and handle it right now.

### 3. Pipeline glance (3 min)

Open HubSpot Deals → Pipeline view.

- [ ] Any deals in "Call Booked" with a call today?
- [ ] Any deals in "Call Held" still waiting for proposal? (>2 business days = should have already triggered the proposal workflow)
- [ ] Any deals in "Proposal Sent" >5 days unsigned?
- [ ] Any new deals at "Replied" that need a quick personal note before the AI takes over?

For each: either act now (if <60 sec) or add to today's task list.

### 4. Client check (3 min)

- [ ] Any client messages overnight? (Slack, email, SMS)
- [ ] Any active site builds with QA needed today?
- [ ] Any monthly reports due this week?
- [ ] Any GBP suspensions or weird notifications?

Active clients get priority over outbound for the rest of the day. If a paying client is waiting on something and there are 4 pending cold-email drafts, the client wins every time.

### 5. Infrastructure (1 min)

- [ ] VPS still up? (Uptime Kuma green, or no overnight email from UptimeRobot)
- [ ] n8n workflows green? (Open n8n → Executions → today's run history; any failed?)
- [ ] Anthropic API spend MTD reasonable? (Click through if briefing flagged anything)

If n8n has failed executions, look at them now. They get worse if left.

### 6. Set today's three (1 min)

Out loud or in writing: name the three things that, if done today, would make the day a win.

Format:
- One outbound thing (could be: send proposal, run scrape, reply to X)
- One delivery thing (could be: ship page X, write report Y, GBP update Z)
- One ops/strategic thing (could be: outline blog post, fix that broken n8n node, attorney follow-up)

Three. Not five. Not ten. Three.

Write them at the top of today's notes. Cross off as you go.

---

## What to do if there's nothing in the queues

This will happen on quiet mornings, especially early. Two options:

**Option A: Move the long-burning thing.** The content calendar. The retainer agreement attorney review. The improvement to a sequence variant. The thing that's never urgent but always important.

**Option B: Read for 15 minutes.** Not Twitter. Not LinkedIn. A book, a long-form article, an essay on something adjacent. The brain ROI of one good read is high.

What NOT to do: stare at HubSpot waiting for a new lead. Watching the kettle doesn't boil it.

---

## What to do if everything is on fire

Almost never happens, but the protocol:

1. Pause Smartlead sends. Stops new fires from starting.
2. Identify the single most-damaging issue (legal escalation? client churn risk? VPS down?).
3. Spend the full morning on that one. Cancel meetings if you have to.
4. Update Operations Agent with what you're skipping today so it doesn't double-ping.

Triage on a bad day looks like: 1 thing fixed > 5 things half-touched.

---

## What this isn't

- Not a strategy session — that's the monthly retro
- Not a long-form planning exercise — Sunday night for that
- Not optional — the discipline of every-weekday-15-min is the point
- Not a 60-minute drift session disguised as a "review"

If you're regularly going over 15 minutes, either the agency has grown enough to need a real ops layer (good problem), or you're avoiding the actual work (bad pattern). Self-diagnose.

---

## Habit anchors

The behaviors that make this stick:
- **Same time every weekday** — drift kills the habit
- **Same physical location** — same chair, same desk, same view
- **Coffee or tea as a Pavlovian cue** — works even on bad mornings
- **Phone face-down across the room** — no exceptions
- **Briefing email in a dedicated label** — easy to skim history

After 60 days this is automatic. Before 60 days it's effortful. Plan accordingly.

files.title: Weekly Metrics Template
files.content:
# Weekly Metrics Template

**When:** Monday morning, after the daily review. Takes 30 min the first few times, 15 min once you're in rhythm.

**Source data:** Operations Agent's automated weekly rollup (from Smartlead + HubSpot + Stripe + Wave). Your job is to read it, interpret it, and decide the week's three priorities.

**Output:** Filled-in version of the template below, saved as `06-Daily-Ops/weekly-archives/YYYY-MM-DD.md`. These accumulate into the data trail for monthly retros and the YouTube series.

---

## Week of {{monday_date}} — {{sunday_date}}

### Top line

| Metric | This week | Last week | 4-week avg | Direction |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| New MRR closed | ${{}} | ${{}} | ${{}} | ↑ ↓ → |
| Cumulative MRR | ${{}} | ${{}} | n/a | |
| Total clients (active) | {{}} | {{}} | n/a | |
| Hours worked on agency | {{}} | {{}} | {{}} | |
| Effective hourly (this week's MRR / hours) | ${{}} | ${{}} | ${{}} | |

**One-sentence read on the week:** {{what_kind_of_week_was_this}}.

---

### Outbound

| Metric | This week | Last week | 4-week avg |
|---|---|---|---|
| Cold emails sent | {{}} | {{}} | {{}} |
| Replies | {{}} | {{}} | {{}} |
| Reply rate | {{%}} | {{%}} | {{%}} |
| Positive replies | {{}} | {{}} | {{}} |
| Positive % | {{%}} | {{%}} | {{%}} |
| Bounce rate (7d rolling) | {{%}} | {{%}} | {{%}} |
| Unsubscribes | {{}} | {{}} | {{}} |
| Spam complaints | {{}} | {{}} | {{}} |

1. Operations Agent auto-fills the top-line numbers, outbound, pipeline, delivery, infra. Just paste them.
2. The qualitative sections (client health, what worked, what didn't, surprises) are 5 minutes of honest reflection. Don't overthink.
3. Three priorities is the highest-value 5 minutes — it sets the week. Don't rush it.

If a week's Monday is hectic and you can't do this until Tuesday, do it Tuesday. The discipline matters more than the day.

- **"Everything was fine"** — almost never true. If you can't list anything that didn't work, you weren't paying attention.

# 07 — Brand Assets

The staging ground for everything visual. Files here get copied into the code repo (`~/Code/NordicNerd/apps/agency-site/public/`) by the brand-assets Claude Code prompt — see `~/Personal/NordicNerd-Ops/CLAUDE-CODE-PROMPT-02-brand-assets.md`.

**Source of truth for file names + destinations:** `asset-manifest.md` (this folder).

---

## Subfolders

| Folder | Contents |
|---|---|
| `logo/` | Primary brand mark — Higgsfield-generated raster + hand-crafted SVG fallback |
| `video/` | Scroll-scrubbed hero video — Higgsfield-generated MP4 + ffmpeg re-encode notes |

---

## What you need to do manually

Two files have to come from the Higgsfield widget (I can't pipe them directly to disk from the MCP side):

1. **Logo** — the one you picked from the 4 variations. Right-click → save → rename to `nordic-nerd-mark.png` → drop in `logo/`.
2. **Video** — the 8-sec liquid-gold-on-black clip. When the generation finishes in the widget, save → rename to `scroll-hero-source.mp4` → drop in `video/`. The re-encode command in `video/README.md` produces the production `scroll-hero.mp4` from it.

Once both files are in place, the Claude Code prompt at the repo root will wire them into the Nuxt site.

---

## What's already here (code-controlled)

- `logo/nordic-nerd-mark.svg` — a hand-drawn SVG approximation of the Nauthiz-N mark. Uses `currentColor` so it inherits whatever text color the parent has. Stable, version-controlled, and works as the inline logo in headers / footers regardless of what happens to the raster.
- `asset-manifest.md` — the single list of every brand asset, its canonical filename, and where it lives in the code repo.

---

## Naming discipline

- All asset filenames use lowercase-kebab-case.
- No spaces, no version numbers in filenames (use git for versioning).
- The asset manifest is the only place where filename → destination mapping lives. Update it when you add or rename a file.

files.title: Logo
files.content:
# Logo

Two formats, two jobs.

| File | Format | Source | Use for |
|---|---|---|---|
| `nordic-nerd-mark.png` | Raster (PNG) | Higgsfield — `nano_banana_pro`, the variation you picked | Social profile avatars, OG images, situations where a richer visual treatment matters |
| `nordic-nerd-mark.svg` | Vector (SVG) | Hand-coded geometric Nauthiz-N inside a circular badge | Inline logo in site header/footer, favicons, anywhere it needs to scale infinitely or recolor via CSS |

---

## To save the PNG

The PNG isn't here yet — you need to grab it from the Higgsfield widget output:

1. Open the chat where Cowork rendered the 4 logo variations.
2. Right-click the one you liked → **Save image as…**
3. Name it exactly: `nordic-nerd-mark.png`
4. Drop it in this folder.

Optional bonus: while you're saving, also save a **black background** version if Higgsfield generated one with the gold-accent prompt. Name it `nordic-nerd-mark-inverse.png`.

---

## About the SVG

`nordic-nerd-mark.svg` is hand-coded geometry. It is NOT a tracing of the Higgsfield raster — it's a deliberate code-controlled brand mark that approximates the same idea:

- A capital N built from two vertical strokes + one diagonal
- Inscribed in a circle for stamp/badge presence
- Single-weight strokes, sharp square caps, rune-influenced angularity
- `currentColor` fill/stroke so it inherits the parent's text color

Why both formats: the Higgsfield raster has texture and richness AI generation excels at, but it can't be infinitely recolored, can't scale to favicon size cleanly, and isn't truly source-controlled (regenerating it gives you a different image). The SVG is the reliable, predictable mark for in-code use. Use the right one for the right job.

---

## Recommended exports (do once, after PNG is saved)

For social and OG images, export the PNG at:
- 512×512 (square avatar)
- 1200×630 (Open Graph default)
- 1024×1024 (high-res App Store / iOS icon source)

These are best generated from the PNG source using a quick ImageMagick or any image editor. Names:
- `nordic-nerd-mark-512.png`
- `nordic-nerd-mark-og-1200x630.png`
- `nordic-nerd-mark-1024.png`

Drop them in this folder. The asset-manifest knows where they go.

files.title: Scroll-Scrubbed Hero Video
files.content:
# Scroll-Scrubbed Hero Video

The premium effect: video timeline maps 1:1 to scroll position. Source clip is the Higgsfield `cinematic_studio_3_0` generation — liquid gold ink on matte black, 8 seconds, 16:9.

| File | Use |
|---|---|
| `scroll-hero-source.mp4` | Raw Higgsfield output — keep as the regenerate-from source |
| `scroll-hero.mp4` | Re-encoded production file with every frame keyed for jitter-free scrubbing — what the site actually serves |
| `scroll-hero-sprite.jpg` *(optional, follow-up)* | JPEG sprite sheet for iOS Safari low-power fallback |

---

## To save the source

1. Open the chat where the Cowork widget rendered the cinematic_studio_3_0 video.
2. Right-click the video → **Save video as…** (or use the widget's download button if present).
3. Name it exactly: `scroll-hero-source.mp4`
4. Drop it in this folder.

---

## To create the production file

Scrubbed video needs every frame to be a keyframe — otherwise the browser stalls and frame-seeks badly during scroll. Run this once after dropping the source:

```bash
cd ~/Personal/NordicNerd-Ops/07-Brand-Assets/video

ffmpeg -i scroll-hero-source.mp4 \
  -c:v libx264 -preset slow -crf 20 \
  -g 1 -keyint_min 1 \
  -movflags +faststart \
  -pix_fmt yuv420p \
  -an \
  scroll-hero.mp4
```

What each flag does:
- `-c:v libx264 -preset slow -crf 20` — H.264 video, slow preset (better quality at the same size), CRF 20 (visually lossless for this kind of content)
- `-g 1 -keyint_min 1` — every frame is a keyframe → seamless scrubbing
- `-movflags +faststart` — moves the moov atom to the front so the browser can start playing before fully downloading
- `-pix_fmt yuv420p` — broad browser compatibility
- `-an` — drop audio (video is muted in the page anyway)

Expected output file size: ~3–6 MB for an 8-second 1080p clip. If it's bigger than 8 MB, bump CRF up to 23 and re-run.

---

## Optional iOS Safari fallback (sprite sheet)

iOS Safari throttles inline video scrubbing in low-power mode. If QA shows jank on iPhones, generate a sprite-sheet of JPEGs and swap the `<video>` for a canvas painter. The Claude Code prompt has the component-level switch ready.

```bash
# 240 frames at 30fps = exactly 8 seconds, arranged 15 columns × 16 rows
ffmpeg -i scroll-hero-source.mp4 \
  -vf "fps=30,scale=480:-1,tile=15x16" \
  -q:v 4 \
  scroll-hero-sprite.jpg
```

Output: a single large JPEG (~1.5–3 MB) containing all 240 frames. The canvas painter in `ScrollScrubVideo.vue` (`mode="sprite"`) handles the math.

---

## Regeneration

If you want a new variant of the source (different motion, tighter convergence, etc.), regenerate via Higgsfield with a tweaked prompt, save the new source as `scroll-hero-source-v2.mp4`, re-encode to `scroll-hero-v2.mp4`, and update the `asset-manifest.md` to point at the new file. Keep the prior version — never overwrite.

# Claude Code Prompt — Phase 02: Brand Assets + Motion Library > **Paste this into Claude Code at the root of `~/Code/NordicNerd/` after Phase 01 (the scaffolding prompt) has been run, or alongside it as the follow-up turn.** --- You are working in the Nordic Nerd monorepo at `~/Code/NordicNerd/`. The Phase 01 scaffolding prompt has been (or is being) executed — `pnpm` monorepo with `apps/agency-site`, `apps/client-template`, `packages/ui`, and `infrastructure/`. The artifact library at `~/Personal/NordicNerd-Ops/` is the source of truth for business context. **Two focused jobs this phase:** 1. Wire the Higgsfield-generated logo + scroll-scrub video into the agency site 2. Stand up `@vueuse/motion` (already canonical per the Phase 01 `CLAUDE.md`) for the page's enter / stagger animations, wrapped in a small `AnimatedSection` primitive --- ## Motion library Use **`@vueuse/motion`** as the Phase 01 `CLAUDE.md` rule already states. No need to update the rule. The scroll-scrub video uses native `requestAnimationFrame` + video `currentTime` directly (not `@vueuse/motion` — scrubbing needs frame-accurate control); `@vueuse/motion` handles everything else (in-view fades, stagger, headline reveals). --- ## Asset manifest **Read this first:** `~/Personal/NordicNerd-Ops/07-Brand-Assets/asset-manifest.md` It's the authoritative list of every brand asset, its current status (✅ ready / ⏳ needs manual save / ⏳ generated), and its destination path in the code repo. Don't invent paths — read the manifest. The manifest shows two assets that may still be marked ⏳ at the time you run: - `logo/nordic-nerd-mark.png` — user saves manually from Higgsfield widget - `video/scroll-hero-source.mp4` → re-encoded to `video/scroll-hero.mp4` Handle both states gracefully: - If the file exists in the staging folder → copy it to the destination - If it doesn't → write a clearly-marked placeholder + log it in `REPO_STATUS.md` under "blocked on user" --- ## Job 1 — Asset placement 1. **Logo SVG** (always present): copy `~/Personal/NordicNerd-Ops/07-Brand-Assets/logo/nordic-nerd-mark.svg` → `apps/agency-site/public/img/nordic-nerd-mark.svg`. 2. **Logo PNG** (conditional): if present, copy to `apps/agency-site/public/img/nordic-nerd-mark.png`. If missing, log in `REPO_STATUS.md`. 3. **Favicon set**: generate from the SVG using `sharp` or `@resvg/resvg-js` in a one-shot Node script at `infrastructure/scripts/generate-favicons.ts`: - `favicon.ico` (multi-size: 16, 32, 48) - `favicon-16.png`, `favicon-32.png` - `apple-touch-icon.png` (180×180) - `web-app-manifest-512.png` Run the script once; commit the outputs to `apps/agency-site/public/`. Don't ship the script as a dev dependency if `sharp` is the only use — just run it ad-hoc with `pnpm dlx`. 4. **Video source**: if `~/Personal/NordicNerd-Ops/07-Brand-Assets/video/scroll-hero.mp4` exists (the user has re-encoded), copy to `apps/agency-site/public/video/scroll-hero.mp4`. If only `scroll-hero-source.mp4` exists, log in `REPO_STATUS.md` with the exact ffmpeg command from `~/Personal/NordicNerd-Ops/07-Brand-Assets/video/README.md` for the user to run. 5. **`.gitattributes`**: ensure `*.mp4 filter=lfs` is set up if the video will exceed 50 MB (it shouldn't at the expected 3-6 MB encode, but check). Otherwise standard binary commit is fine. --- ## Job 2 — `ScrollScrubVideo` component Build `packages/ui/components/ScrollScrubVideo.vue` with this contract: ```ts interface Props { src: string // path to the MP4 height?: string // wrapper height in vh or px (default '300vh' — gives ~3 screens of scroll for the 8s clip) preload?: 'auto' | 'metadata' // default 'auto' mode?: 'video' | 'sprite' // default 'video'; 'sprite' uses a canvas painter for iOS fallback spriteSrc?: string // required when mode='sprite' spriteCols?: number // default 15 spriteRows?: number // default 16 spriteFrameCount?: number // default 240 } ``` Behavior: - Sticky inner container at `top-0 h-screen w-full overflow-hidden bg-[#0a0a0a]`. - The outer section is `height` tall; as user scrolls through it, video `currentTime` advances 1:1. - Use `requestAnimationFrame` to throttle scroll handlers. No GSAP. - On `loadedmetadata`, set initial currentTime to match initial scroll position. - Tear down listeners in `onUnmounted`. - Default slot for overlay content (headline, CTA) — absolutely positioned over the video. - TS strict, no `any`. Use `Ref` etc. For `mode='sprite'` (iOS fallback path), draw the corresponding sprite tile to a `` matching the viewport instead of seeking the video. Math: ``` frameIndex = Math.floor(progress * (spriteFrameCount - 1)) tileX = (frameIndex % spriteCols) * tileWidth tileY = Math.floor(frameIndex / spriteCols) * tileHeight ``` Don't auto-detect iOS; expose `mode` as a prop and let the consuming page decide. (Detection logic can be added later in a higher-level wrapper.) --- ## Job 3 — Hero integration In `apps/agency-site/pages/index.vue`, replace whatever hero scaffold exists with: ```vue

Done-For-You Local Presence

Polk County contractors deserve a digital presence that actually shows up when someone searches. We build it. We run it.

Book a 15-min discovery call
``` Use the brand color tokens from Tailwind config (defined per the manifest). If they're not yet in the config, add them. If the video file is missing, render a fallback hero (the same content, on a static `bg-[#0a0a0a]` block) and log the missing video in `REPO_STATUS.md`. --- ## Job 4 — Install and wire `@vueuse/motion` 1. `pnpm add @vueuse/motion` in `apps/agency-site`. 2. Register the Nuxt module — `@vueuse/motion/nuxt` — in `apps/agency-site/nuxt.config.ts`. This auto-registers the `v-motion` directive and the `useMotion` composable globally; no per-page imports needed. 3. Create `packages/ui/components/AnimatedSection.vue` — a tiny wrapper that fades children in on viewport entry using `v-motion`: ```ts interface Props { delay?: number // ms, default 0 duration?: number // ms, default 600 direction?: 'up' | 'down' | 'left' | 'right' | 'fade' // default 'up' distance?: number // px of travel for directional variants, default 24 once?: boolean // default true — only animate first time in viewport } ``` Implementation: render a wrapper `
` with `v-motion` bound to computed `initial` / `visibleOnce` (or `visible`) variants. The variant maps direction → `x`/`y` offset, fade → opacity only. Keep the API tiny. Sketch (Claude Code fills in the rest, strict TS, no `any`): ```vue ``` 4. For **staggered groups** (e.g. ServiceAreaGrid cards entering one after another), don't build a stagger prop into `AnimatedSection`. Instead, render the items inside the parent with computed delays: ```vue ``` Keeps the wrapper component dumb; stagger is just `index × stride`. 5. Apply `AnimatedSection` to: - The hero headline + sub-head + CTA (manual 0 / 120 / 240 ms delays) - The `ServiceAreaGrid` items on the homepage (computed `i * 80`) - Each section break on `/services/[city]/[niche]` pages Don't go animation-crazy. The scroll video is the showcase moment; everything else uses subtle 600ms upward fades, max. 6. Respect `prefers-reduced-motion`: `@vueuse/motion` honors it automatically when the user has the OS-level setting on. No extra code needed — just verify with a manual toggle in DevTools. --- ## Job 5 — `CLAUDE.md` motion section The Phase 01 `CLAUDE.md` already says "`@vueuse/motion` only." Just clarify the scroll-scrub exception by adding/updating this block: ```markdown ## Motion - **Library:** `@vueuse/motion`. Registered as a Nuxt module — `v-motion` directive and `useMotion` composable are available globally. - **Wrapper:** `` in `packages/ui` for the common case (in-view fade with direction + delay). - **Stagger:** computed `delay` based on index — don't build a stagger primitive. - **Scroll-driven scrubbing exception:** `` uses native `requestAnimationFrame` + video `currentTime` directly. Not `@vueuse/motion` — scrubbing needs frame-accurate control beyond what the directive offers. This is the only place direct DOM/RAF animation is allowed. - **Banned:** GSAP, Framer Motion (React), motion-v, AOS, anime.js. Keep the surface to one library. - **Reduced motion:** `@vueuse/motion` honors `prefers-reduced-motion` automatically. Verify on every new animated surface. ``` Mirror to `apps/agency-site/CLAUDE.md` if that file exists. --- ## Constraints (carried from Phase 01) 1. TS only, no `any`. Use `unknown` + narrowing, or define the type. 2. `

files.content:
# Claude Code Prompt — Phase 03: Nordic Nerd Ops Plugin

> **Paste into Claude Code at `~/Code/NordicNerd/` after Phase 01 scaffolding is in place. This builds the agency-specific Claude Code plugin so future sessions in the repo have skills + sub-agents + slash commands tuned to your workflows.**

---

You're working in `~/Code/NordicNerd/`. The Phase 01 monorepo scaffolding is in place; the brand assets (Phase 02) have been wired. Now build the **agency-ops plugin** — a Claude Code plugin scoped to this repo that turns the 5-agent system from `~/Personal/NordicNerd-Ops/02-Agents/` into first-class sub-agents, plus slash commands and skills tuned to Adam's daily workflows.

This is the "JARVIS Layer 3" surface — when Adam is in the repo doing real work, this plugin is what makes the work fast.

---

## Goal

A Claude Code plugin at `.claude/plugins/nordic-nerd-ops/` that provides:

1. **5 sub-agents** mirroring the agent rule files in `~/Personal/NordicNerd-Ops/02-Agents/`
2. **Slash commands** for fast common actions
3. **Skills** that bundle multi-step workflows
4. **Connector hints** that surface the right MCPs at the right times

---

## Plugin structure

```
.claude/plugins/nordic-nerd-ops/
├── plugin.json                          # plugin manifest
├── README.md                            # what's inside, how to use
├── agents/
│   ├── outbound-ops.md                  # sub-agent: scrape, deliverability, sequencer
│   ├── copywriter.md                    # sub-agent: copy, sequences, reply drafts
│   ├── pipeline.md                      # sub-agent: HubSpot, Cal, Documenso, Stripe
│   ├── client-delivery.md               # sub-agent: site builds, GBP, reporting
│   └── operations.md                    # sub-agent: briefings, rollups, retros
├── commands/
│   ├── pipeline.md                      # /pipeline — show current HubSpot pipeline
│   ├── money.md                         # /money — MTD revenue + MRR
│   ├── reply-queue.md                   # /reply-queue — pending replies awaiting review
│   ├── weekly-status.md                 # /weekly-status — generate the Monday rollup
│   ├── new-city-niche-page.md           # /new-city-niche-page lakeland kitchen-remodelers
│   ├── ship-monthly-report.md           # /ship-monthly-report acme-roofing
│   ├── draft-proposal.md                # /draft-proposal {{deal-id}} growth
│   ├── trigger-scrape.md                # /trigger-scrape — fire the Apify workflow
│   └── retro.md                         # /retro — open the current month's retro draft
└── skills/
    ├── send-audit/                      # the "send the 5-things audit" flow
    │   └── SKILL.md
    ├── onboard-client/                  # 30-day onboarding kickoff sequence
    │   └── SKILL.md
    ├── ship-monthly-report/             # generate + send the monthly report
    │   └── SKILL.md
    └── weekly-content-batch/            # blog + SEO page generation per content calendar
        └── SKILL.md
```

---

## Job 1 — `plugin.json` manifest

```json
{
  "name": "nordic-nerd-ops",
  "version": "0.1.0",
  "description": "The Nordic Nerd agency operations: 5 sub-agents, slash commands, skills tuned to Adam's daily workflows in this repo.",
  "author": "Adam de Leon",
  "license": "UNLICENSED",
  "agents": "./agents",
  "commands": "./commands",
  "skills": "./skills"
}
```

---

## Job 2 — Sub-agents (mirror `~/Personal/NordicNerd-Ops/02-Agents/`)

Each sub-agent file starts with frontmatter declaring tools, identity, scope, then carries the FULL rules text from the corresponding ops library file.

### Source-of-truth sync

Each agent file is a **mirror**, not an independent copy. The first line of each agent file points back to the ops library as canonical:

```markdown
---
name: outbound-ops
description: Owns the top of the cold-outbound funnel — scrape, list hygiene, deliverability, sequencer state. Invoke for anything touching Apify, Hunter, MillionVerifier, Smartlead/Instantly, Postmaster.
tools: ['Read', 'Edit', 'Bash', 'Grep', 'Glob']
---

> **Canonical source:** `~/Personal/NordicNerd-Ops/02-Agents/outbound-ops-agent.md`. Keep this mirror in sync when you update the canonical file.

[FULL CONTENTS OF outbound-ops-agent.md FROM THE OPS LIBRARY]
```

Same pattern for `copywriter.md`, `pipeline.md`, `client-delivery.md`, `operations.md`.

Read each ops library file, copy its full contents into the corresponding agent mirror, and add the canonical-source pointer + tools declaration.

---

## Job 3 — Slash commands

Each command lives in `commands/<name>.md` with frontmatter + a short skill body. They invoke the appropriate sub-agent and connector tools.

### `/pipeline`
```markdown
---
description: Show the current HubSpot pipeline at a glance — count + $ at each stage, plus anything stale.
---

Invoke @pipeline sub-agent. Use the HubSpot connector to fetch all open deals with properties `dealname,dealstage,amount,hs_lastmodifieddate`. Group by stage. Flag any deal where `hs_lastmodifieddate` is older than 5 days as stale. Output as a tight table, then list stale deals separately. Don't editorialize on whether numbers are "good." Just numbers.
```

### `/money`
```markdown
---
description: MTD revenue and MRR pull from Stripe.
---

Invoke @operations sub-agent. Use the Stripe connector to fetch: (1) balance (available + pending), (2) active subscriptions for MRR sum, (3) charges this month. Report MTD revenue, current MRR, count of active subscriptions. One line per metric, lead with the number.
```

### `/reply-queue`
```markdown
---
description: Cold-email replies awaiting Adam's review — drafted and pending one-click send.
---

Invoke @copywriter sub-agent. Check Gmail (label "Cold Replies" or "Pending Adam") for threads where Adam hasn't responded. For each: pull the original reply, the draft Mimir's reply-triage workflow generated (if accessible via Slack history or HubSpot deal notes), and Adam's SLA clock. Sort by intent_signal (high first). Output as a list with one-line summaries.
```

### `/weekly-status`
```markdown
---
description: Generate the Monday weekly rollup per ~/Personal/NordicNerd-Ops/06-Daily-Ops/weekly-metrics-template.md.
---

Invoke @operations sub-agent. Pull last 7 days from HubSpot (deal movements), Stripe (revenue), Smartlead/Instantly (sends + replies + bounces), Gmail (replies). Fill in weekly-metrics-template.md. Save the filled version to `~/Personal/NordicNerd-Ops/06-Daily-Ops/weekly-archives/YYYY-MM-DD.md`. Output the top-line summary to chat. Same voice as the operations-agent.md rules.
```

### `/new-city-niche-page <city> <niche>`
```markdown
---
description: Generate a new local SEO page for a city × niche combo using ~/Personal/NordicNerd-Ops/05-Content/blog/city-niche-page-template.md.
arguments:
  - name: city
    description: Polk County city (e.g., lakeland, bartow, winter-haven)
    required: true
  - name: niche
    description: Niche slug (e.g., kitchen-remodelers, roofers, hvac-contractors)
    required: true
---

Invoke @client-delivery sub-agent. Read the template at `~/Personal/NordicNerd-Ops/05-Content/blog/city-niche-page-template.md`. Generate a complete `content/locations/{niche}-{city}-fl.md` for the active client repo (or for the agency site if no client is specified). Includes Frontmatter + H1 + hero + service area + common projects + process + recent work + reviews + FAQ + CTA. Word target: 1,200–1,800. Performance budget: pass Lighthouse SEO 100, A11y >=95. After generation, run a quick check (Read the file back, count words, verify all required sections exist).
```

### `/ship-monthly-report <client-slug>`
```markdown
---
description: Generate this month's client report per ~/Personal/NordicNerd-Ops/04-Client-Templates/monthly-reporting-template.md.
arguments:
  - name: client-slug
    description: Client folder name (e.g., acme-roofing)
    required: true
---

Invoke @client-delivery sub-agent. Pull data for the client: GBP insights via the Google Business Profile API, site analytics from Plausible/Fathom, content shipped this month (git log + content folder), reviews delta. Fill the template at `~/Personal/NordicNerd-Ops/04-Client-Templates/monthly-reporting-template.md`. Save the filled PDF + a 2-min Loom script to `~/Personal/Clients/{client-slug}/reports/YYYY-MM/`. Do NOT auto-send — ask Adam to review and send.
```

### `/draft-proposal <deal-id> <tier>`
```markdown
---
description: Draft the proposal for a HubSpot deal at the specified tier.
arguments:
  - name: deal-id
    description: HubSpot deal ID
    required: true
  - name: tier
    description: starter | growth | authority
    required: true
---

Invoke @pipeline sub-agent. Pull the deal + contact from HubSpot. Use the Documenso template matching the tier (template IDs in `~/Personal/NordicNerd-Ops/03-Outbound-System/n8n-workflows/04-booking-to-proposal.json`). Fill merge fields from the deal/contact data. Generate the prep brief per the proposal-template.md cover note. Submit via the n8n manual-proposal-trigger webhook. Confirm in HubSpot the stage moves to Proposal Sent.
```

### `/trigger-scrape`
```markdown
---
description: Manually fire the Apify weekly scrape workflow.
---

Invoke @outbound-ops sub-agent. **CONFIRMATION REQUIRED.** Show the user: estimated lead volume (200-250), estimated cost (~$3.50), affected cities and niches. Ask "Confirm? (y/n)". On 'y', POST to `https://n8n.thenordicnerd.com/webhook/manual-scrape`. On 'n', exit cleanly.
```

### `/retro`
```markdown
---
description: Open or generate the current month's retro draft.
---

Invoke @operations sub-agent. Look for `~/Personal/NordicNerd-Ops/06-Daily-Ops/monthly-archives/YYYY-MM.md` (current month). If exists, open it for editing. If not, generate it per `~/Personal/NordicNerd-Ops/06-Daily-Ops/monthly-retro-template.md` with the auto-fillable sections filled from HubSpot + Stripe data. Walk Adam through the qualitative sections.
```

---

## Job 4 — Skills (multi-step workflows)

Each skill folder contains a `SKILL.md` that Claude can invoke for repeatable multi-step flows.

### `skills/send-audit/SKILL.md`
The flow for sending the "5 things I'd change" one-pager audit to a contractor who replied "send the audit" to a breakup email. Inputs: contact email, company name. Steps: (1) pull contact's GBP via Google Business Profile API, (2) generate the 5-point audit using a Claude API call with the audit system prompt (write this prompt if it doesn't exist in `~/Personal/NordicNerd-Ops/03-Outbound-System/reply-handling/`), (3) format as a clean 1-page PDF, (4) send via Gmail in the existing thread with a soft-CTA to the discovery call, (5) update HubSpot deal with note.

### `skills/onboard-client/SKILL.md`
The Day 0 → Day 30 onboarding sequence per `~/Personal/NordicNerd-Ops/04-Client-Templates/onboarding-checklist.md`. Inputs: client name, tier. Steps: send welcome email (template in onboarding-checklist.md), create client folder in `~/Personal/Clients/{slug}/`, request GBP manager access, send discovery questionnaire, schedule day-7 kickoff call, create HubSpot tasks for each onboarding milestone.

### `skills/ship-monthly-report/SKILL.md`
End-to-end monthly report generation for a client. Reuses `/ship-monthly-report` slash command logic but adds: PDF generation via Pandoc, Loom script generation, email composition (with attachment), CC billing@thenordicnerd.com.

### `skills/weekly-content-batch/SKILL.md`
Generate the week's content batch per `~/Personal/NordicNerd-Ops/05-Content/blog/content-calendar-q1.md`. One blog post + one city/niche SEO page. Both hand-edit-ready. Saves to `apps/agency-site/content/` and creates a PR.

---

## Job 5 — Wire connector hints in `plugin.json`

If the plugin manifest supports `requiredConnectors` or similar, declare:
- HubSpot (used by pipeline, draft-proposal, /pipeline)
- Stripe (used by /money)
- Gmail (used by /reply-queue, send-audit)
- Google Business Profile (used by client-delivery, ship-monthly-report)
- Anthropic (used internally by every skill — already configured)

When a command runs and the connector is missing, the command fails gracefully with a clear instruction to connect it (via `mcp__mcp-registry__suggest_connectors`).

---

## Constraints

1. Plugin files use Markdown for skills/commands/agents. Frontmatter is YAML.
2. TS only when generating any code via skills. No `any`.
3. Don't auto-execute write actions without explicit confirmation in the command body.
4. All slash commands respect the voice DNA from `~/Personal/NordicNerd-Ops/02-Agents/copywriter-agent.md` when generating prospect/client-facing text.
5. The plugin lives in `.claude/plugins/nordic-nerd-ops/` and is committed to the monorepo — version-controlled, not magic.

---

## Verification

After scaffolding:

1. Run `claude code` in the repo. List available agents (`@outbound-ops`, `@copywriter`, `@pipeline`, `@client-delivery`, `@operations`) — all 5 should appear.
2. Try `/pipeline` — should fail with "HubSpot not connected" if connector missing; otherwise produce output.
3. Try `/weekly-status` — should generate a draft (with mostly-empty numbers in pre-launch state) and save to archives.
4. Verify each command file parses (no YAML errors).

---

## Output

Append to `REPO_STATUS.md` at the repo root:
- Plugin scaffolded at `.claude/plugins/nordic-nerd-ops/`
- All 5 sub-agents mirror the ops library agents
- 9 slash commands available
- 4 skills available
- Connector requirements documented in plugin.json
- Next: any connector that's not yet connected needs `mcp__mcp-registry__suggest_connectors`

---

## Before writing any code

1. Read `~/Personal/NordicNerd-Ops/02-Agents/*.md` — these become the sub-agent bodies.
2. Read the templates referenced by each command (`weekly-metrics-template.md`, `monthly-retro-template.md`, `monthly-reporting-template.md`, `city-niche-page-template.md`, `onboarding-checklist.md`).
3. Confirm the Claude Code plugin format current at the time of execution — check `claude code --help` or the latest docs for any breaking changes to the plugin.json schema.
4. List the changes you plan to make in a numbered plan, wait for approval, then execute.

Go.
